ROYAL AFRICAN SOCIETY
18 Northumberland Avenue, London, W.C.2. Telephone : WHITEhall 6733.
Secretary—Miss H. Heather.
Editor of the Journal—Alan Gray.
The Society was founded in 1901 in memory of Mary Kingsley and has the following objects :

To foster and encourage interest in Africa, particularly in Territories within the Commonwealth ; to form a link between those who are, or have been, concerned with Africa and to maintain in the United Kingdom a centre for the study of African affairs.

The Society seeks to further these objects by the periodical publication of a journal, African Affairs , by lectures, meetings and social functions and by providing access to a reference Library and reading room.
The Society is non-partisan and has no political affiliations. It corresponds with African societies and institutions in and outside the United Kingdom.

Members are entitled to use the Library of The Royal Commonwealth Society and to receive the Society's quarterly journal African Affairs , which holds a high place in current literature. In dealing with every aspect of African life and development it endeavours to encourage and facilitate the exchange of information ; and is designed not only to interest those living and working in Africa but also to foster the study of African affairs in all parts of the world. It is non-political ; and, while seeking to maintain a scientific outlook, aims specially at keeping the human interest in the forefront.
ROYAL BOTANIC GARDENS, KEW
Director—Sir George Taylor.

During its span of over 200 years, Kew has been closely associated with the development of Commonwealth countries. From a small start in 1759 as the botanic garden of Augusta Dowager Princess of Wales and mother of King George III, the Royal Gardens at Kew soon became famed for the unrivalled variety of plants collected from many lands. In 1841 control of the Gardens was assumed by the State and increasingly from that time, under successive Directors, Kew supplied the Commonwealth with plants of economic value and with men trained in horticulture. Since 1902 the Director of Kew has held the title of Botanical Adviser to the Secretary of State for the Colonies.

Kew is renowned as an institution for the study of plants and especially their identification and classification. A large scientific staff is able to avail itself of a herbarium which is without equal in the richness of its collection of preserved plants and its fine botanical library. In addition there is the collection of living plants, some 25,000 species and botanical varieties, which forms the part of the establishment familiarly known to the public as Kew Gardens.

An important task, which has been steadily prosecuted since 1856, has been the compilation of floras of different parts of the Commonwealth. Regional floras now in preparation include those of Tropical East Africa, southern Central Africa and Cyprus, while a revision of the Flora of West Tropical Africa is well advanced.

The identification and classification of plants is aided by a study of their anatomy, cytology, and physiology, and work of this kind is done in the Jodrell Laboratory, a building which was rebuilt and enlarged in 1963-64.
Digitized by Google
INSTITUTIONS

Special interest has always been taken at Kew in the plants of use to man, and much information on the economic properties of plants has been accumulated. Kew has played a notable part in the distribution of useful plants to Commonwealth countries, and this work continues in a modified form to the present. To reduce the risk of carrying harmful pests or disease during the exchange of economic plants, there has been built at Kew a plant quarantine house, financed by Colonial Development and Welfare funds, in which plants in transit from one part of the Commonwealth to another can be grown under supervision for a period of quarantine.

The training of gardeners is a function of Kew and, although most of the students are recruited in this country, some places are taken by men from other parts of the Commonwealth. Men trained at Kew continue to fill a number of posts overseas.
ROYAL COMMONWEALTH SOCIETY (formerly Royal Empire Society)
Northumberland Avenue, London, W.C.2.
Telephone : WHitehall 6733.
Telegrams : Recital, Rand, London.
Cables : Recital, London, W.C.2.
Secretary-General—D. K. Daniels, C.B.E.
The Royal Commonwealth Society was founded in 1868 as the Colonial Society, and received its Royal Charter in 1882.

The Society has a dual role, both cultural and social. Its Headquarters provides complete social and residential amenities, with excellent bedrooms (43 single, 12 double), full catering services, with a dining-room to seat 200 and snack buffet, bars, lounges, billiards and card rooms, etc. There are also extensive programmes of a social nature. The Assembly Hall, which seats 400, is fitted with film projectors, microphones and tape recording equipment and may be hired for conferences, film shows and social engagements, with or without catering services. Weekly Lunch-time and Evening Meetings are held in the Assembly Hall, when addresses are given by prominent speakers on subjects of topical interest and importance. There is also an Overseas Discussion Group and an Open Forum for young people and Commonwealth students.

The Society's unique possession is the Commonwealth Library containing over 350,000 books and pamphlets, as well as full sets of the transactions of many Learned Societies and of the Gazettes and other official publications of Commonwealth Governments. It possesses very many valuable works and documents including early editions of Richard Hakluyt, Ogilby, Peter Martyr; the famous Westall drawings; and various collections of drawings, manuscripts and letters relating to many parts of the Commonwealth. Frequent exhibitions are arranged of its most noteworthy possessions.

The Library issues bibliographies of the literature on Commonwealth subjects and handles a large correspondence with students and others who seek advice in research work. It is also responsible for the Commonwealth Diary, containing day-to-day entries, political, social and constitutional, of the Commonwealth's story.

The Information Bureau provides up-to-date information and papers on conditions and emigration prospects overseas, particularly in Colonial territories, giving facts and figures on such matters as health, housing, education, recreation, local taxation and wage rates, etc. in individual territories. The Bureau also conducts a loan service of visual aids for schools and helps in the arrangement of exhibitions of a Commonwealth character.

The Commonwealth Studies Committee holds Summer Schools at University Centres, Study Conferences at Headquarters, and extensive programmes of lectures for schools, clubs, Institutes, etc. It organizes the Commonwealth Essay Competition for children of all ages in schools in the United Kingdom and throughout the Commonwealth. The Committee also publishes the Imperial Studies Series of Monographs.

The Society provides its members with a bi-monthly Journal containing reports of meetings, general information, etc. The proceedings of the Society were formerly published in the name of the Proceedings of the Royal Colonial Institute , and later United Empire , the whole series providing a unique record of Imperial and Commonwealth history and development since the Society's inception.

There are over 31,500 members of the Society, of which 21,000 are overseas. All British subjects and Commonwealth citizens are eligible for membership provided they are proposed and seconded by two Fellows. There is a special rate for Companions (17 to 24 years of age) who form a flourishing part of the Society.

Local Branches are established at Bath, *Bournemouth, *Bristol, Cambridge, Edinburgh, *Hove, *Liverpool and Oxford, and at *Adelaide, *Auckland, Bermuda, *Brisbane, Bulawayo, Canberra (A.C.T.), Cape Town, Christchurch (New Zealand), Colombo, Darwin, Dunedin, Guernsey, Hobart, Jersey, Kuala Lumpur, Lagos, Launceston, *Melbourne, *Montreal, *Nairobi, Ottawa, Perth, Port Elizabeth, Salisbury (Southern Rhodesia), *Sydney, Toronto, Vancouver, Victoria (B.C.), *Wellington (New Zealand) and Winnipeg. The Society also has over 80 representatives in many other centres overseas.
Further particulars may be obtained from the Secretary-General.

ROYAL COMMONWEALTH SOCIETY FOR THE BLIND
Windsor House, 46 Victoria Street, London, S.W.1.
Telephone : TATe Gallery 3228/9.
Telegrams : Besblind, Sowest, London.
Director—John Wilson, O.B.E.

The Royal Commonwealth Society for the Blind, under its previous name of British Empire Society for the Blind, was set up as an independent corporation in January 1950, following the report of a joint Committee of the Colonial Office and the National Institute for the Blind entitled "Blindness in British African and Middle East Territories". Its objects are to stimulate official and voluntary action and to take the lead in a movement "to promote the welfare, education and
Digitized by Google
THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST

employment of the blind, and to prevent blindness". Its area of operation originally included any British Colonies not possessing responsible government, British Protectorates, Protected States, United Kingdom Trust Territories and the High Commission Territories of South Africa, but provision was made for this area to be extended by the Executive Council of the Society to include British territories possessing responsible government, and territories administered under a Condominium to which the British Government is a party. Its area was further extended in 1957 to enable the Society not only to continue its work in former colonial territories which have become members of the British Commonwealth but also to empower the Society to undertake central activities for the benefit of organizations for the blind throughout the Commonwealth. The Society is under the direction of a Council to which three members are appointed by the Secretary of State for the Colonies.

Since the Society's inception, local organizations for the blind have been established in 33 territories, Regional Officers have been stationed in East, West and Central Africa, the West Indies and Mediterranean territories, and ophthalmic surveys have been conducted in East, Central and West Africa. Other activities in which the Society has assisted have been the establishment of some 106 schools and training centres, registration of the blind, Braille production, and training in welfare work amongst the blind. It is also working with Governments and organizations in various territories in the prevention of blindness, in the experimental development of new types of training for blind farmers, peasant cultivators and village craftsmen, and in projects of 'open' education whereby blind children are educated in ordinary primary schools.

It has established a Commonwealth Scholarship Fund to assist those wishing to obtain special qualifications in ophthalmology, to help defray the cost of training blind welfare workers and to provide financial assistance in other deserving cases, e.g. to meet the cost of attendance at a useful international conference or to finance a publication or investigation.

Burlington House, London, W.1.

Since the International Geophysical Year more and more countries have become actively interested in the Antarctic. This increase in international activity has led to a review of the arrangements for providing scientific advice to the British Antarctic Survey, by which name the former Falkland Islands Dependencies Survey is now known.

In June 1962 the Falkland Islands Dependencies Scientific Committee was disbanded and its commitments vested in the Royal Society's National Committee on Antarctic Research. Its terms of reference are:—
To advise the High Commissioner, British Antarctic Territory, through the Director, British Antarctic Survey, on:—

(i) Scientific aspects of the annual programme of work of the British Antarctic Survey as proposed by the High Commissioner of the British Antarctic Territory; (ii) scientific aspects of any other proposals or activities arising from the British occupation of British Antarctic Territory; (iii) the use to be made of scientific specimens and data accumulated during previous season's work by the Survey and to be accumulated in the future.

The School was established in 1916 by Royal Charter and its purposes are to further research in, and to extend the study and knowledge of, the languages of Eastern and African peoples ancient and modern and the literature, history, religion, law, customs and art of those peoples.
Digitized by Google
INSTITUTIONS

The Governing Body of the School includes inter alia a member appointed by the Secretary of State for the Colonies and members nominated for appointment by certain Commonwealth Governments who are Contributing Bodies.

The School is organized in ten Departments, viz., Anthropology and Sociology; Economic and Political Studies; History; Law; Phonetics and Linguistics; and Languages and Cultures of: India, Pakistan and Ceylon; South-East Asia and the Islands; Far East; Near and Middle East; Africa. Instruction is provided for first and higher degrees of the University of London in many of the principal languages and cultures of Asia and Africa. Short courses of instruction for students not wishing to read for degrees are also provided. Language instruction is provided for Cadets of the Overseas Civil Service in those languages for which there is no provision at the Universities of Oxford and Cambridge; and also for those Cadets whose Governments require them to take the short intensive course of preliminary training instead of the full sessional course. Courses are also provided for other members of the Overseas Civil Service home on leave and for members of Government Departments. Special provision may be made for members of commercial and industrial firms about to proceed to the East or to Africa.
Publications of the School are The Bulletin of the School of Oriental and African Studies , the Calendar and the Annual Report of the Governing Body .
TROPICAL DIVISION, BUILDING RESEARCH STATION
Department of Scientific and Industrial Research, Garston, Watford, Herts.

A Colonial Liaison Section was established at the Building Research Station in 1948. It was expanded in 1953 to deal with planning and housing matters. A Tropical Paint Research Fellow was attached in 1957. The Division was renamed in 1958. The whole cost is met from the overseas research vote of the Department of Technical Co-operation.

A Tropical Building Advisory Committee (a sub-committee of the Building Research Board) was established in 1963. It replaces the former Colonial Housing and Town Planning Panel. The main functions of the Division are:
(i) To collect from and supply to overseas territories information on building, housing and planning matters including that on relevant developments in the United Kingdom and elsewhere;
(ii) to assist with the solution of specific technical problems and to answer enquiries on building and housing;
(iii) to advise the Secretary for Technical Co-operation on overseas building, housing and planning matters.

Information is disseminated through Overseas Building Notes ; Notes on Building, Housing and Planning in Tropical and Sub-Tropical Countries ; and by occasional monographs Tropical Building Studies , published by Her Majesty's Stationery Office.

In 1960 the arrangements made in the United Kingdom for the provision of advice on the co-ordination and promotion of research in tropical medicine, and the functions of the Colonial Medical Research Committee, were reviewed. Since 1945 the Colonial Medical Research Committee had, under their terms of reference, advised the Secretary of State and the Medical Research Council on all matters of medical research in, or for, the benefit of the Colonies. Developments in the overseas territories called for review of the advisory machinery, and the Medical Research Council put forward proposals to meet the changing circumstances. The proposals were approved by Her Majesty's Government and as a result the Tropical Medicine Research Board was established in 1960 by the Medical Research Council with the following terms of reference:—
To advise—

(a) the Secretary for Technical Co-operation, through the Medical Research Council, on all medical research overseas or in the United Kingdom financed from the funds of the Department of Technical Co-operation;
(b) the Medical Research Council on all medical research in or for tropical or sub-tropical countries financed from their own budget.
The Tropical Medicine Research Board took over the work of the Colonial Medical Research Committee and that Committee was accordingly dissolved.

TROPICAL PRODUCTS INSTITUTE (formerly Colonial Products Laboratory)
56-62 Gray's Inn Road, London, W.C.1.
Telephone: CHancery 5412-9.
Telegrams: Troprods Westcent London.
Director — E. S. Hiscocks.

Assistant Director (Division "B") — E. M. Thain.
Assistant Director (Division "C") — W. D. Raymond.
Assistant Director (Division "D") — R. H. Kirby.

The Institute has its roots in two bodies, namely the Plant and Animal Products Department of the Imperial Institute and the Colonial Products Research Council. The former of these two bodies came into existence in 1893 to provide a scientific advisory service dealing with the plant and animal products of the British Empire. The Colonial Products Research Council was set up in 1942 to advise the Secretary of State for the Colonies as to the more fundamental research that should be conducted on the renewable resources of Colonial territories to help bring about their greater use.

In 1953 the work of these two bodies was combined and in 1957 the organization moved from the Imperial Institute Building to its present premises, whereupon the name Tropical Products Institute was adopted.
By agreement with the Lord President of the Council, the Tropical Products Institute became a station of the Department of Scientific and Industrial Research with effect from 1st April
1959, although from 1st April, 1963, financial responsibility has been assumed by the Department of Technical Co-operation.

The Tropical Products Institute is charged with the investigation of, and provision of information about, the renewable resources of tropical and sub-tropical countries, particularly British Colonial and Commonwealth Territories.

Laboratory examinations are made and information supplied on plant and animal products such as essential oils, spices, gums and resins, oils and oilseeds, waxes, foods and feeding-stuffs, drugs and insecticides of vegetable origin, fruit and fruit products, vegetables, fish, fibres, pulp, and board making and papermaking materials. Information is also supplied about sources of raw materials, cultural conditions, methods of production, marketing and commercial possibilities of products. Specifications and estimates of cost are obtained for plant, machinery and equipment which may be required for production and processing. Commercial valuations and assessments of the suitability of materials are also obtained and factory tests are arranged when necessary.

In addition to laboratory investigations arising out of samples received for examination, the Institute conducts more fundamental studies of tropical plant and animal products with a view to discovering materials which might form the basis of new industries, and facts about existing products or by-products which might benefit industries already in operation. It is particularly concerned at present with studies on toxic metabolites produced by moulds, physiologically active constituents of plants, the composition of various fixed oils and essential oils, the analysis of pesticides used in the tropics and the production of building materials from waste products.

The Institute's economists are concerned with the various economic problems arising from existing, or in the development of new industries. They conduct market surveys and collect statistical information about tropical plant and animal products and work with other sections of the Institute on the economic aspects of enquiries received.

The development work of the Institute involves selecting from work conducted in the Institute or elsewhere (Universities, etc.), ideas which might beneficially be developed to production scale in tropical areas. Where appropriate pilot scale examination of the selected projects is carried out and if results are favourable full scale production in tropical regions is encouraged.
In addition the Institute awards extramural research contracts to Universities, one of which is in a Colonial territory (Hong Kong).
The quarterly journal Tropical Science (formerly Colonial Plant and Animal Products ), is issued by Her Majesty's Stationery Office.

UNIVERSITY OF THE WEST INDIES, FACULTY OF AGRICULTURE (formerly Imperial College of Tropical Agriculture)

Secretary's Address: St. Augustine, Trinidad, The West Indies. Telegrams: Stomata, Port of Spain, Trinidad. Pro-Vice-Chancellor—H. D. Huggins. Secretary—V. A. A. Archer. Professor of Botany—J. W. Purseglove. Dean of Agriculture—P. N. Wilson. Professor of Zoology—G. L. Underwood. Head of Soil Science and Chemistry Department—(Vacant).

The College was established in 1921, as the outcome of recommendations made in the Report of the Tropical Agricultural College Committee appointed by Viscount Milner in 1919. It was incorporated by Royal Charter in 1926.

The main objects of the Faculty are to provide instruction, and to conduct research, in tropical agriculture. It is the recognised centre in the British Commonwealth for post-graduate training in tropical agriculture and the agricultural sciences for officers destined to serve in the Colonial Agricultural Service. Caribbean agriculture is taught at undergraduate level.

As from 1st August 1960 the Imperial College of Tropical Agriculture was merged with the University College of the West Indies and became its Faculty of Agriculture. Following on this merger a three-year course leading to a pass B.Sc. degree in agriculture was instituted in October 1960. The University College became a full University in 1962.

Almost all Colonial Governments and the United Kingdom Government contribute to the income of the Faculty of Agriculture. The University is also responsible for the administration of the Regional Research Centre for the British territories within the Caribbean area. These are financed in whole, or in part, by Her Majesty's Treasury from Colonial Development and Welfare funds and by industry. The buildings, at St. Augustine, near St. Joseph, Trinidad, are on land granted by the Government of Trinidad and Tobago.
The academic year extends from the beginning of October to the end of June. The following courses and facilities for study are available:

1. Post-Graduate Courses.—Diploma in Tropical Agriculture—Open to holders of a degree or diploma of any British university, university college or other academic institution specifically approved by the Governing Body of

the Imperial College of Tropical Agriculture. Special facilities are available for such graduates to extend their knowledge of, or undertake investigations into, subjects pertaining to tropical agriculture.
Candidates are also accepted for the M.Sc. and Ph.D of the University of the West Indies.
2. Special Courses.—Open to officers of agricultural departments in the tropics, or similar institutions. Details are arranged on application.
3. A Three-Year Degree Course in agriculture open to candidates satisfying the entry requirements of the University.
The tuition fees are as follows:—
(a) Post-Graduate Course £75 per academic year.
(b) Special Course £75 per academic year.
(c) Degree Course £60 per academic year.

The Women's Corona Society is a voluntary and non-political association of women of all races. Originally its members were all connected with Her Majesty's Overseas Civil Service and the public services in Colonial territories, but it is now open to any women in active sympathy with the aims of the Society.
These are: to be a link between its members throughout the Commonwealth; to be of service to them and, through them, to the wider com-

munity, with particular emphasis on helping to promote friendship between the people of the Commonwealth. In Britain this is done by giving help to members and other women going overseas or visiting the United Kingdom, not only by the Headquarters Office in London but also in Scotland, West of England, Oxford and Guildford where groups have been formed. If those going overseas are too far from any of these centres, Headquarters will put them in touch by post with a member on leave from the country in which they are interested and will supply them with up-to-date information on living conditions overseas.

Further help over problems of living in a new country is provided by three-day conferences held in spring and autumn, and shorter ones in intervening months. These meetings, for which no charge is made, are open to anyone who may be interested; besides lectures and films, they provide a chance for women of many countries to meet informally. For overseas visitors hospitality is arranged in members' homes as well as classes in cookery etc., and escorts are provided for sight-seeing and shopping. For members there are other social occasions such as the annual dinner and garden party, lunch meetings and evening parties, which serve as a link between those who carry on the work of the Society. This includes services such as the meeting of and looking after children in transit through London; the compiling of information about holiday homes for children and a register of temporary accommodation.

A Newsletter is circulated twice a year reporting the activities of the Society in Britain and the thirty-five Corona Bodies overseas where local representatives welcome newcomers, helping them to get to know the women of the country and to take part with them in giving voluntary service wherever there is most need.
The subscription is 10s. a year or £4 for life membership. For further particulars, apply to the Organizing Secretary.

MALTA
STATE OF MALTA, G.C.
Area

The total area is 122 square miles made up of the three main islands as follows : Malta 94.876 square miles, Gozo 25.899 square miles and Comino 1.075 square miles. Filfla and Cominotto are mere islets.
Population
The population of Malta is European; on the 31st December 1962 the total population was estimated to be 329,326 divided as follows: Malta 302,144, Gozo and Comino 27,182.
It is the policy of the Government to encourage and assist emigration. From 1946 to 1962, 84,021 persons emigrated; the figure for 1962 was 3,641.

Geographical Features

Malta consists of a plateau with a hard core of coralline limestone on the west side which descends by gentle steps to a plain composed of a thick stratum of soft globigerine limestone on the south-east. The highest point on the Island, situated near Dingli, is just over 800 feet above sea level. The Grand Harbour and Marsamxett Harbour are excellent ports of call, while safe anchorages are also provided by Marsaxlokk Bay, St. Paul's Bay and Mellieha Bay. There are no rivers, lakes or forests. In low-lying places the soil, which contains a high percentage of lime and is rich in phosphates, is deep, but elsewhere it is shallow. Cultivation is mainly in terraced fields where the soil is held by rubble walls.
Climate
Malta has a healthy and fairly typical Mediterranean climate, in which extreme heat and cold are modified by the smallness of the islands and the nearness of the sea.
Mean annual rainfall is about 22 inches, which is about the same as London, though there are three almost rainless summer months.

Winters are mild, the lowest temperature recorded in the year being on average $5^{\circ}\text{C}$ , that is $41^{\circ}\text{F}$ ; and the summers fine and sunny with maximum temperatures in August (which is the hottest month) averaging $30^{\circ}\text{C}$ or $86^{\circ}\text{F}$ . Only occasionally is extreme heat experienced, when a southerly wind called the Scirocco blows, as for instance in July 1962, when a temperature of $41^{\circ}\text{C}$ , that is $106^{\circ}\text{F}$ , was reached.
History
From Roman times until 1530 Malta was subjected successively to Arab and Sicilian domination. In 1530, the Emperor Charles V

gave the Island to the Knights of St. John of Jerusalem, who held it until 1798, when Napoleon Bonaparte invested it on his way to Egypt, having prepared the ground by intrigue and revolutionary propaganda. On 12 June 1798 Ferdinand de Hompesch, the last Grand Master in Malta, capitulated without any resistance and the Order was dispersed. The French installed themselves in the islands, but their interference with Maltese laws and customs brought about a sharp reaction, and the Maltese drove the French troops within the fortified towns and, with the help of the British fleet, held them closely blockaded for two years, after which the French, reduced to extremities, surrendered to the British in 1800.

The representatives of the Maltese people, freely elected, unanimously approved a declaration of rights in 1802 in which, inter alia , they requested the protection of His Majesty the King of Great Britain and Ireland, recognised him as their Sovereign Lord, whilst maintaining that the power to establish the Constitution of the Islands, which His Majesty was bound to keep inviolate, was vested in the General Congress elected by the people.

Malta was a vital point in allied strategy in the Second World War and the Island was subjected to heavy enemy air attacks between the entry of Italy into the war in 1940 and the liberation of North Africa in 1943. In 1942, Malta was awarded the George Cross in recognition of the part she had played in the War.
Constitution

After the war Malta was granted a measure of Self-Government (subject to the reservation of certain powers to the Governor) under a Constitution introduced by Letters Patent dated 5th September 1947. On the resignation of the Government led by Mr. D. Mintoff on the 24th April 1958, and the disturbances that followed, a state of emergency was declared on 30th April and the direct administration of the Island was assumed by the Governor. On the 15th April 1959, the state of emergency was brought to an end, the 1947 Constitution was revoked and replaced by an interim Constitution established by the Malta (Constitution) Order in Council, 1959, and the Malta Royal Instructions, 1959.

Under this Constitution all legislative and administrative powers were vested in the Governor acting, except in certain specified cases, in consultation with the Executive Council. The Executive Council was composed of three ex officio members (Chief Secretary, Legal Secretary, Financial Secretary), three other nominated official members, and four nominated unofficial members.

Following the report of a Constitutional Commission under the chairmanship of Sir Hilary Blood, a new Constitution for Malta was introduced by the Malta (Constitution) Order in Council, 1961, under which the Island is now known as "the State of Malta". The Constitution provides inter alia for the protection of fundamental rights and freedoms of the individual. The Malta Government enjoys concurrent legislative powers with the United Kingdom Government on matters of defence and external affairs (however, in August 1962, the Government of Malta was granted the power to negotiate directly with the "Six" for the eventual association of Malta with the Common Market and to deal directly with other countries for the purpose of obtaining financial and technical aid). The Governor and Commander-in-Chief is required to consult with
Digitized by Google
THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST

the Cabinet composed of a Prime Minister and seven Maltese Ministers, in exercising all his powers, with certain exceptions; a United Kingdom Commissioner is appointed to represent the Government of the United Kingdom in Malta. A Consultative Council is also appointed composed of the Governor as Chairman, the Prime Minister, the United Kingdom Commissioner, three members appointed by a Secretary of State and three members appointed by the Prime Minister, providing for consultation on policy relating to defence and external affairs, and on any matters affecting the relations between the United Kingdom Government and the Government of Malta as may be referred to it by either Government.

Elections for the fifty seats in the Legislative Assembly, as opposed to forty seats under the 1947 Constitution were held on 17th, 18th and 19th February 1962. The state of the parties in the Legislative Assembly now is: Nationalist Party twenty-six seats, Malta Labour Party sixteen seats, Christian Workers Party four seats, Democratic Nationalist Party three seats, Progressive Constitutional Party one seat. The Nationalist Party Leader Dr. Giorgio Borg Olivier, who had been Prime Minister from 1950 to 1955, was entrusted with the formation of a Government and the Cabinet was sworn in on the 5th March 1962.

On 20th August 1962, Dr. Borg Olivier made a formal request for independence within the Commonwealth. A constitutional conference was held in London in July 1963, and on the 1st August 1963, Her Majesty's Government announced their intention that Malta should become independent not later than 31st May 1964.
Land Policy
There is no restriction on the alienation of land to non-Maltese but the Mortmain Law restricts the alienation of land to religious bodies or institutions under certain circumstances.
Taxation
The principal forms of taxation are income tax, import duties, excise duties, stamp duties, entertainment duties, tombola duty, licence duties and succession and donation duties.
Income tax, introduced in 1949, is levied on all personal incomes, with certain exceptions, at the following rates :—
2s. for every £ of the first £200.
3s. for every £ of the next £300.
4s. 6d. for every £ of the next £400.
6s. 6d. for every £ of the remainder.
The tax on companies is at the rate of 5s. on every £ of the chargeable income.
A surtax is also levied on incomes exceeding £2,500 at the following rates :—
2s. for every £ of the first £500.
3s. for every £ of the next £500.
4s. for every £ of the next £500.
5s. for every £ of the next £500.
6s. for every £ of the next £500.
6s. 6d. for every £ of the remainder.

MALTA

Interest at $2\frac{1}{2}\%$ is paid by the Government on £78,281 outstanding balance on 31st March 1963 of the debt known as "Massa Frumentaria" incurred by the Municipality of Valletta under the Government of the Order of St. John of Jerusalem, in connexion with the monopoly of the wheat trade. The funds of the "Massa Frumentaria" were in 1798 appropriated by the French Government then in occupation of the Island.

Under the Malta Treasury Bills Emergency Ordinance, 1958, the Government may raise up to £1,500,000 by the issue of Treasury Bills. The amount of such bills outstanding on 31st March 1963 was £1,300,000.

The Government is also empowered to raise up to £4,000,000 under the Local Loans (Registered Stock and Securities) Ordinance, 1959, and up to £5,000,000 under the Development Loans (United Kingdom Government) Ordinance, 1962. Under the first Ordinance three issues have been made, one of £1,300,000 6% Stock at £98 in December 1959, the second of £1,200,000 6% Stock at £95 in October, 1960, and the third of £1,670,000 6% Stock at £95 in November, 1961. Under the second Ordinance a loan of £1,200,000 was made in March, 1963.
Currency

The currency of Malta consists of notes of £5, £1 and 10s., issued by the Board of Commissioners of Currency, together with British coins, including silver, cupro-nickel and bronze coins and local notes in denominations of 5s., 2s. 6d., 2s. and 1s. Bank of England notes ceased to be legal tender in the Island in 1949. Maltese £5, £1 and 10s. notes are on a par with sterling and £ for £ coverage is maintained at the Bank of England. The amount of local currency in circulation on 31st March 1963 was £24,423,000.
Development Plans

Since the end of the War, direct aid by way of free grant from Her Majesty's Government to Malta has amounted to over £54 million, mainly in respect of capital expenditure including War Damage and Reconstruction grants.

A Five-Year Development Plan for the Maltese Islands covering the period 1959-64, was published in October 1959. This was reviewed towards the beginning of the third year. The Reviewed Plan was published in July 1961 and provides for an expenditure of over £35$\frac{1}{2}$ million of which £29$\frac{1}{4}$ million was made available by Her Majesty's Government in loans (£12.48 m.) and grants (£16.77 m.), including £19$\frac{1}{4}$ million from Colonial Development and Welfare Funds. In December 1962, negotiations were finalized for a long term loan of £2.67 million from the International Bank for Reconstruction and Development to finance in part the cost of a new power station and distillation plant.

The Plan aims at diversifying the Maltese economy which has hitherto been largely sustained by the expenditure of the fighting Services; this expenditure is declining because of changing defence policies and concepts. The measures contemplated in the Plan to achieve this end may be summarized as follows :—

(a) The Harbour, which is Malta's leading natural asset, is to be developed by the conversion of the Dockyard to commercial use and by the construction of modern deep water quays and other port facilities. A £7$\frac{1}{4}$ million loan from Colonial Development and Welfare sources is

being made for the Dockyard operation which has been entrusted to a United Kingdom firm of ship repairers. The new quays have been completed. The few outstanding works are expected to be completed shortly.

(b) Funds are provided for the building of a fully serviced industrial estate at Marsa and the construction of factories in other parts of the Island. It is expected that some seventeen factories will have been built by March 1964. Capital loans and grants are also offered to industry, in addition to an income tax "holiday" and Customs duties easements available under the Aids to Industries Ordinance. An extensive publicity campaign undertaken by an Aids to Industries Board has drawn the attention of industrialists in the United Kingdom, on the Continent and elsewhere to the wide range of aid offered. The results have been satisfactory and some 37 projects for different industries have been approved. Besides these direct aids, the Plan also sets out to provide the basic services necessary for industrialization apart from the port already referred to above, i.e., the expansion of the water and electricity supplies and the modernization and expansion of the telephone services. Technical Education is also to be developed in order to meet industry's increasing need for skills. The construction of a College of Arts, Science and Technology is well advanced and a grant of £100,000 has been secured from U.N.E.S.C.O. towards furniture and equipment.

(c) The tourist industry is being built up to take advantage of Malta's climatic, scenic and historical assets. The Capital loans and grants to industry referred to above will also be made available for hotels. The extension of essential services to hotel resort sites is being undertaken by the Government.
(d) The two indigenous industries, agriculture and fisheries, are being assisted towards greater efficiency.

(e) Although the greater share of the funds is allocated to these economic objectives, substantial provision is made for the social services—primary and secondary education, health, housing, sewers and public buildings.

(f) During the financial year ended 31st March 1963, a total of £3,719,752 was spent on development and other capital works, bringing the total expenditure for the first four years of the Development Plan to just over £17 million. Among the projects brought to completion may be mentioned the Deep Water Quay and the Grain Silo and most of their ancillary works; the construction of several factories and the provision of essential services to the Industrial Estate; the formation of housing estates in several localities and the building of a Fisheries Station. Moreover, the bulk of the school building programme is now completed, whilst good progress has been made on the erection and equipping of hospitals, the formation of roads and the mechanization of the telephone exchanges.
Education

Compulsory education was introduced in 1946. Primary education is carried on principally in free Government schools which are Roman Catholic. There are 112 Primary schools, where Maltese and English are the only languages of instruction and where Italian and French have recently been included in the curriculum, with an enrolment of 51,885 pupils; and six Grammar schools with 3,717 pupils. The Royal University of Malta, founded
Digitized by Google
THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST

in 1592 and raised to University status in 1769, has an average attendance of 330 students and grants degrees and diplomas in theology, law, medicine and surgery, architecture, engineering, dental surgery, pharmacy, arts, science and awards diplomas of notary public, legal procurator, architect and civil engineer and public administration. The number of Maltese studying in United Kingdom Universities or colleges during 1962 was 84. There are 210 evening classes for languages, technical and commercial subjects with an attendance of 3,988 and 67 literacy classes for illiterates and semi-illiterates with an attendance of 900. There are three Secondary Technical Schools providing seven-year courses leading to G.C.E. Ordinary and Advanced level for 2,074 pupils of whom 745 are girls. A Technical Institute is being built to provide three to five year craft and technician courses for 725 pupils between 14 and 17 years. This Institute is to incorporate the various technical schools which are spread over different areas. An apprentices school provides five-year courses for 310 apprentices on the day release system. The College of Arts, Science and Technology founded in temporary premises in 1961 at the Old Dockyard School while permanent buildings were being erected has continued to expand. In addition to the technician level courses in engineering, a part-time professional level course was started leading to the examinations of the British Engineering Institutions. The average number of students attending the College is 140. St. Patrick's Residential School has 111 boarders, age 9 to 15. The School of Art provides seven evening classes attended by 250 persons of all ages. 228 persons are undergoing accelerated full-time technical training in various trades, at two Government Industrial Training Centres in Malta and one in Gozo. These centres were started under the vocational training scheme for prospective migrants. There is a Government home for girls with an average total of 54 inmates. The total number of private (primary and secondary) schools is 86 with an attendance of about 17,750 pupils.
In 1962-63 ordinary expenditure on education was £1,968,271; capital expenditure was £509,068. Expenditure on education in 1935 was £149,521.
Libraries, Museums, etc.

The Royal Library including the Juvenile Library has a stock of 69,290 works, the Gozo Public Library has 18,210. There are also 46 district circulating libraries in the various towns and villages (35 in Malta and 11 in Gozo), with a total book-stock of 88,640.

The pre-historic, historic and artistic heritage of the Islands is cared for by the Museum Department which continually adds to present knowledge in all three fields through excavation, research and selective buying. The following Museums and monuments are open to visitors :—

Health

There are seven Government hospitals in Malta with a total of 2,600 beds and five in Gozo with 289 beds. During 1962 in-patients numbered 17,528, out-patient attendances amounted to 75,791. There are in addition 42 District Medical Officers, 48 Health Visitors (district nurses) and five subsidized Midwives. In the Government dispensaries the number of attendances by the public in 1962 was 95,488.

Communications

The international airport of Malta is Luqa Airfield, which is also a Royal Air Force Station. There are other Service airfields at Hal Far and Ta' Qali. British European Airways operate the following scheduled services :—(a) Twelve services a week to London. These call at Palermo or Naples or Alghero or Ajaccio or at Rome; (b) two services a week to Catania; (c) six services a week to Tripoli, and (d) two services a week to Benghazi.

Skyways Limited, under a pooling agreement with British European Airways operate a scheduled service between Malta and London direct twice weekly. British United Airways operate freight services between Malta and East, Central and South Africa, and between Malta, Frankfurt and London.
During the year 1962 there were 3,805 civil aircraft movements. 50,593 passengers were set down and 53,646 picked up, while 22,291 passengers passed through Malta in transit.
The size of Malta does not provide scope for internal air services and the only railway line ceased to operate in 1931.

Sixteen miles of roads have been resurfaced with hot-laid bitumen macadam. Surfacing for main roads remains penetration macadam (semi-grouting) and 495.36 miles are paved in this method using limestone aggregate and bituminous emulsion; 171.3 miles of country and estate roads are paved in water-bound macadam.
The number and tonnage of ships which entered the Grand Harbour during 1962 are :—
1,987 Steamships, tonnage 3,865,839.
94 Sailing ships, tonnage 3,710.

MALTA

There is no regular passenger line between Malta and the United Kingdom. The Italian Tirrenia Line runs a regular passenger service between Malta and the Continent calling at Syracuse, Catania and Naples. (Frequency: (1) July-September every three days; (2) October-June every 5 days.)
Broadcasting

Under the Broadcasting Ordinance, 1961, broadcasting services in Malta were made the responsibility of the Malta Broadcasting Authority, established on 29th September 1961. The Authority has entered into agreements with Rediffusion (Malta) Limited granting an extension of their existing licence to provide a wired sound service up to 1986, and with the Malta Television Service Limited (a new company constituted in Malta and within the Rediffusion group of Companies) to provide Television and Wireless Sound Services for a similar period. In the case of Wireless Sound, the company has been allowed a period of five years within which to decide whether or not to take up this concession: if it has not done so by then the concession will lapse.

Local television transmissions were inaugurated on 29th September 1962. The service is in English and Maltese and operates on the 625 line standard. In addition, Italian Television transmissions can be received from R.A.I. (Radio Television Italiana).
Television sets number 19,876 with another 10,577 licences for wireless receiving sets; as on 31st July 1963 the subscribers to Rediffusion's wired sound service numbered 41,526.
Principal Occupations

The gainfully-occupied population (86,820) was engaged in 1962 in the following occupations: agriculture (7,280), fishing (640), stone quarrying and clay pits (580), manufacturing and mechanical (15,700), construction (6,450), commerce (12,100), communications (4,240), community, recreation and personal services (8,230), Government (16,900), dockyard and Service Departments, including local servicemen (14,700).

On 30th June 1963, there were 67 registered trade unions with a total membership of 34,360 whereof 39 associations of employees with a membership of 30,047, 19 of employers with a membership of 1,953 and 9 combinations of employers and employees with a membership of 2,360.
Main Crops and Products
The main crops are potatoes, onions, grapes, wheat, barley and forages. Fresh vegetables and potatoes are the main cash crops.

According to the last available census, the live-stock population was: Cattle 8,090; horses 1,906; donkeys 2,388; mules 1,599; sheep 11,603; goats 31,012; pigs 16,392; poultry 360,008; rabbits 53,295.
During 1962, 26,343 cwts. of fish were landed by local fishermen (mainly during the months between May and November) selling at a retail value of £362,411.
Malta has to import nearly all the raw materials required for industry.
The two leading industries are the ship-repair yard—formerly a Royal Naval establishment privately operated since April 1959—and the building industry.

Besides a number of light industries produce, flour, macaroni, edible oil, tomato paste, canned fruits and vegetables, beer, soft drinks, wine; buttons, footwear, gloves, ladies' handbags, knitwear, homespun lace, nylon socks and stockings, shirts and pyjamas, textiles, leather goods; cigarettes, pipes and other smoking requisites; candles, matches, paints, bituminous emulsion, mirrors, crown corks, nails and screws, neon-signs, refrigerators, boats, brooms; flowers and seeds.

Following the grant of assistance under the Aids to Industries Scheme, these new industries have been set up, namely: automobile assembly; bacon; carnation and chrysanthemum cuttings; cutting and polishing of rough diamonds; detergents; expanded plastics materials; hand-made jewellery; infants' wearing apparel; iron and steel products; knitwear; light engineering; marine paints; plastic articles; spring interior mattresses; synthetic rubber seals; synthetic yarn processing; textiles; conversion of synthetic fibre tow into top; toilet paper rolls.
The Malta Trade Fair Corporation holds yearly International Exhibitions in July. In recent years the Malta Trade Fair has attracted the participation of various countries.

ADEN
Area
Aden is situated about 100 miles east of the Straits of Bab el Mandeb on the south coast of Arabia. The area is 75 square miles.
Population
At the census taken in 1955 the total population recorded was 138,441 :—

Since the census was taken, the population of Aden has increased considerably and is now estimated at about 220,000.

Population

Sheikh Othman .. .. .. 29,879
Little Aden .. .. .. 9,277
Geographical Features
The territory consists of two extinct volcanic craters forming rocky peninsulae joined by a flat sandy foreshore, comprising :—

(a) The peninsula on which are situated the main town known as Crater, the wharf and shipyard area of Maala, the modern harbour suburb known as Tawahi, adjacent to which is an area leased to the Air Ministry for R.A.F. and military purposes and known collectively as Steamer Point.
(b) The isthmus known as Khormaksar where a large R.A.F. station and the Civil Airport are situated.
(c) The strip of mainland between the two peninsulae which encloses the modern harbour and contains the township of Sheikh Othman and the villages of Hiswa and Imad.
(d) The Little Aden peninsula where a modern refinery has been constructed by the Aden Petroleum Refinery Limited and where the two fishing villages of Bureika and Fukum lie.
Climate

The climate is not unhealthy, though the heat and humidity are trying between April and October. During the north-east monsoon, i.e. between the latter part of October and April, the climate is generally cool and pleasant. The rainfall is extremely scanty and in some years non-existent, but as much as eight inches has been known to fall in a year.
Digitized by Google
THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST
History

The plundering of an Indian ship on the coast near Aden led to the capture of Aden by a successful expedition sent from Bombay by the East India Company in 1839 under Major Bailey and Captain Haines of the Indian Navy.

The need to establish coaling stations on trade routes because of the replacement of sailing ships by steamers was one of the reasons which led to the occupation of Aden by the British. Little Aden was obtained by purchase in 1868. The coastal strip between the two peninsulas was secured by purchase in 1882 and 1888.

Under the provisions of the Government of India Act, 1935, and the Aden Colony Order, 1936, the Chief Commissioner's Province of Aden ceased to be a part of British India and was constituted as the Colony of Aden from 1st April 1937.

Aden, a free port, owes its modern commercial prosperity largely to the fact that it is a refuelling station situated closer to the oil production centres of the Persian Gulf than any other great port on the main Eastern Trade routes. The opening of the B.P. Oil Refinery at Little Aden in July 1954 has added further to its prosperity and strengthened its position.
Constitution
By Additional Royal Instructions issued in November 1958, the following changes in the composition of the Executive Council of the Colony became effective:—

The Executive Council consisted of five ex officio members including the Chief Secretary, the Attorney-General, the Financial Secretary, and a representative of the Senior Officer of the Forces stationed in the Colony, with five elected members or nominated members of the Legislative Council, not less than three of whom were to be elected members, selected and appointed by the Governor. These five unofficial members were initially styled "Members in charge" but in February 1961, their title was changed to "Ministers". There was provision for the Governor to assign the individual Ministers responsibility for matters at his discretion.

Under the provisions of the Aden Colony (Amendment) Order, 1944, a Legislative Council was constituted consisting of the Governor as President, four ex officio members, and not more than eight unofficial members. The Aden Colony (Amendment) Order, 1955, provided for the introduction of an elected element into the Legislative Council. Under this Order, the Council consisted of the Governor as President, four ex officio members, not more than five nominated official members, not more than five nominated unofficial members, and four elected members.

The Aden Colony (Amendment) Order, 1958, provided for a Legislative Council consisting of a Speaker, five ex officio members, twelve elected members, and six nominated members. The first elections under the provisions of this Order took place on the 4th January, 1959.

In accordance with the provisions of the Federation of South Arabia (Accession of Aden) Order, 1963, Aden, while remaining under British sovereignty, became the twelfth Member State of the Federation on 18th January 1963 (see page 59). The title of the Governor was changed to High Commissioner for Aden and the Protectorate of South Arabia.

On the same date there was brought into operation a more advanced constitution for Aden, prescribed in the Aden (Constitution) Order in Council, 1962. The Executive Council was replaced by a Council of Ministers, which consists of a Chief Minister and not less than six other Ministers (appointed by the High Commissioner on the advice of the Chief Minister), in addition to the Attorney-General. The High Commissioner is normally bound to act upon the advice of the Council of Ministers, subject to the retention of his general reserved powers, but he is responsible for external affairs, defence, internal security, Police, and the Aden Public Service. The Legislative Council now consists of 16 elected members, six nominated members, and the Attorney-General, as well as a Speaker.

The Federal Constitution having been given the force of law in Aden by the Accession Order in Council, the Government of the Federation of South Arabia has the power to legislate for Aden State on matters under its exclusive authority or under the concurrent authority of the Federation and the State. In any conflict of laws, Federal law will prevail over State law. The High Commissioner, however, retains over-riding powers in respect of security in Aden.
The new Constitution for Aden also includes a Code of Human Rights to which Federal legislation which applies to Aden will be subject.
Land Policy

Under the Crown Lands Ordinance, 1945, the High Commissioner is empowered to grant leases, or alienate, on Her Majesty's behalf, any Crown Land for building, agricultural or any other purpose, on any terms and conditions, as he may think fit.
The administration of the Crown Lands Ordinance is under the charge of the Commissioner of Lands, who is appointed by the High Commissioner.
Taxation
Aden is a free port and there is no general customs tariff, although certain items bear Federal customs duty.

Income tax is a State matter and in Aden the Income Tax Ordinance, 1961, provides for the payment of a graduated tax by individuals ranging from Sh. 1/- to Sh. 15/- in the £ on chargeable income. Companies pay at the rate of Sh.6/50 in the £.

Libraries
The Lake Library, administered by the Aden Municipality, contains 25,989 books in English, Arabic and Urdu and has 1,310 members.

There are well-stocked and expanding libraries at Aden College, the Technical Institute, the Boys' Secondary School and the Girls' College. Amenities for the teaching profession include a club with a library of over 2,000 volumes.
Roads
Aden has a total of 122 miles of roads, of which approximately 110 miles have a bitumenous surface. There are no railways.
Port

Vessels of the principal steamship companies call regularly at Aden and the number of vessels which entered the Port of Aden during 1962, exclusive of country craft, was 5,929, with a net registered tonnage of 28,835,835. The number of country craft which entered the port during the same period was 1,365 with an aggregate net registered tonnage of 134,128. There are 20 first class berths and 7 second class berths. The first class berths are suitable for ships of from 350 to 800 feet in length.

At present three of these berths are being modernised by the installation of dolphin mooring facilities, one of which was brought into commission during 1963. The second class berths are suitable for ships of about 300 feet in length. Thirteen of the first class berths are fitted with submarine pipelines for fuelling ships. There is also an 800 feet long quay with a depth alongside of 18 feet suitable for coasting vessels of up to 2,500 tons gross. Transit and storage sheds on the public wharves provide a total covered space of 400,000 square feet. The approach channel is dredged to 36 feet and three of the largest fuelling berths are dredged to 39 feet. The oil harbour at Little Aden has four jetty berths, the approach channel being dredged to 38 feet and the oil harbour to 40 feet.
Principal Occupations

Of the estimated local labour force of 77,089 some 7,456 are engaged in Port activities such as bunkering, handling of cargo, ship repairing, manning harbour vessels, etc.; 12,646 in building and construction which includes 1,559 men employed by the Services' Works Department and 1,422 by the Aden Government and Aden Municipality; 1,367 in oil refining, 12,495 in industrial undertakings, 10,082 in retail and wholesale trades, 16,297 in Government and Services Departments, 1,113 in miscellaneous trades, and 17,000 in the hotel and catering trade and in private domestic service.
Main Products

There is no agriculture in Aden State and the only important local product is salt which is made from sea water by solar evaporation. The future of the industry is, however, uncertain because of the difficulty in finding suitable markets for it. Production in 1962 was 78,000 tons.

The Oil Refinery at Little Aden produces refined petroleum from imported crude oil. In July 1962 the Refinery had been in commission for eight years and in that time had processed over 31 million tons of crude oil. The products were exported either by tanker or as fuel for ships' bunkers: a small percentage was retained in Aden to meet local demand.
Because of its favourable geographical position and its efficient service to ships, Aden has become one of the busiest oil bunkering ports in the world.

There are small factories in Aden which provide a variety of items: soap, carbon dioxide, recombined acetylene milk, soft drinks, aluminium pots and pans, cigarettes, dyed and printed cloth, wooden furniture, bricks, tiles and retreaded tyres. There is a small but flourishing industry for the blending and packing of perfumes, hair oil and toilet waters; a modern mechanical mill crushes oil seeds. Some Aden produce is sold locally and some is exported to neighbouring territories.
PROTECTORATE OF SOUTH ARABIA
(Previously known as the Aden Protectorate)
Area
The total area is approximately 111,000 square miles, including the Island of Soqotra. The Protectorate is divided into Western and Eastern Areas.
Digitized by Google
THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST
Population is estimated at :—
Western Area .. .. .. 550,000
Eastern Area .. .. .. 318,000
Towns
There are no large towns in the Western Protectorate but in the Eastern Protectorate Mukalla, the sea port, has a population of some 25,000.
Geographical Features

The Protectorate of South Arabia is bounded on the east by the Sultanate of Muscat and Oman, on the north and west by the Ruba' al Khali (or Empty Quarter) and the Yemen, and on the south by the Gulf of Aden and the Indian Ocean.

The Western Protectorate is divided into (a) the litoral belt which varies between 4 and 40 miles in depth; (b) the maritime ranges, about 1,000-2,000 feet above sea level; (c) the intramontane plains, over 3,000 feet high; and (d) the highland plateau ranging from 5,000 to 8,000 feet high and which falls away into the Empty Quarter with a mean altitude of 2,500 feet. The country between the litoral belt on the one hand and the intramontane plains and the plateau on the other is a tangle of mountains, ravines and valleys.

The Eastern Protectorate also has a litoral belt, which is backed by a rock plateau rising to a height of 7,000 feet and continuing inland for about 100 miles, where it breaks up into a system of wadis, some fertile, leading into the main Wadi Hadhramaut. Beyond, to the north, are the sands of the Empty Quarter.
Climate

The altitude, which ranges from sea level up to 8,000 feet, is responsible for considerable variations in temperature, humidity and rainfall. On the coastal plain torrid tropical conditions of great heat and humidity exist in the summer months. During the same period in the mid-altitude zones it is hot but less humid, while at the highest altitudes the climate is warm and temperate but dry; in the winter it can be extremely cold. In the Wadi Hadhramaut, although the range of summer temperature is high, the air is dry and far less humid than on the coastal plain. The whole area falls within the influence of the north-east and south-west monsoons. Rainfall in the coastal areas averages approximately two inches a year; in the most favoured areas of the Western Protectorate highlands it may average ten inches; in the coastal areas of the eastern part, on the other hand, rainfall averages approximately two inches a year. In general, rainfall decreases from west to east until, in the north-east section, conditions of extreme aridity are encountered.
History

For a time in the eighteenth century, the Western Protectorate formed part of the domain of the Imam of Sana'a in the Yemen and several of the rulers of the tribal districts were the Imam's "wakils" or governors until his power declined and they declared their independence.

After the occupation of Aden by the British in 1839, some of the neighbouring chiefs entered into protective treaty relations with Her Majesty's Government. Others followed suit as time went on, the last to do so being in 1954.

In July 1915, during the first World War, the Turks, who had conquered the Yemen in the course of the previous century, occupied the Abdali, Haushabi and Amiri districts of the Protectorate until the Armistice of 1918.

In 1934 a Treaty of Friendship was signed between Her Majesty's Government and the Kingdom of the Yemen. There was an Exchange of Notes in 1951 when it was agreed to establish reciprocal diplomatic missions and to set up a Frontier Commission.

In 1944 and 1945 five Western Aden Protectorate chiefs (the Fadhli, the Lower Aulaqi and the Lower Yafa'i Sultans, the Sharif of Beihan and the Amir of Dhala) entered into closer relations with Her Majesty's Government by new treaties in which they agreed, inter alia , to abide by the advice of the Governor of Aden in the improvement of their administrations. Treaties were signed by the Upper Aulaqi Sheikh and the Audhali Sultan in 1952, and by the Sheikh of Shu'eib in 1954. The Wahidi Sultan of Balhaf signed an Advisory Treaty in 1949, and in 1952 the newly elected Sultan of Lahej accepted a political officer as adviser.

The most important aspect of recent history in the Eastern Protectorate has been the decline of the traditional power of the Kathiri Sultans and the rise of the Qu'aiti—a new Yafa'i dynasty which gained power and wealth from military and other services rendered by its members abroad to the Nizam of Hyderabad. The Qu'aiti rulers first entered into a treaty with Her Majesty's Government in 1882. This was strengthened in 1888 by the conclusion of a Protectorate treaty in the common form of the treaties with other Protectorate chiefs. In 1918 the Kathiri Sultan made an agreement with the Qu'aiti Sultan acknowledging this treaty as binding on him also. In 1937 and 1939 respectively the Qu'aiti and Kathiri Sultans signed treaties undertaking to accept the advice of a British Resident Adviser in all matters except those concerning Mohammedan religion and customs. An advisory treaty was signed by the Sultan of Qishn and Soqotra in 1954.
Administration
Her Majesty's Government does not administer the Protectorate directly but the British Advisory Staff advise the local rulers on the administration of their areas.

In the Western Protectorate the States nominate their own heads, who have subsequently to be recognized by Her Majesty's Government. Not all the heads of the States and tribal chiefs have complete control over their subjects but, since 1943, the authority of some of them has been greatly increased by the introduction of simple administrative machinery and in several States an efficient administration is now functioning. There are now State Councils in Lower Yafa'i, Amiri, Lower Aulaqi, Shu'eibi, Dathina, Beihan, Upper Aulaqi and Wahidi and there are Legislative Councils in Lahej and Fadhli. In 1937 the Aden Government raised a force called the Government Guards for police duties in the Western Aden Protectorate.

In the Eastern Protectorate the principal States are the Qu'aiti and Kathiri which are administered by their Sultans as constitutional rulers and have State Councils. The island of Soqotra is linked administratively with the mainland territory of Qishn under the Sultans of Qishn and Soqotra. Provincial administration of the Qa'iti and Kathiri States is marked by a

Currency

The currency generally in use is the East African shilling, though Maria Theresa dollars are also to be found. The East African currency, which is issued by the East African Currency Board, is in various denominations—in notes (Shs. 100, Shs. 20, Shs. 10 and Shs. 5) and in coins (Sh. 1, Cts. 50, Cts. 10, Cts. 5 and Cts. 1).

The Federation, which is responsible for currency matters, is planning the issue of a new South Arabian currency based on the dinar; 1 dinar is equivalent to E.A.S. 20s. or £1 and is comprised of 1,000 fils. Legislation to create a new South Arabian currency authority is expected to be enacted during 1964.
Development

Between 1945 and 1963 the Protectorate of South Arabia received allocations totalling £3,400,000 under the Colonial Development and Welfare Acts. Under the 1963 Act a further £2,250,000 has been allocated for the three-year period up to 1966.
Principal Occupations
With the exception of Aden, the people of the Protectorate of South Arabia depend largely on agriculture for their livelihood, cotton, dates and coffee being the most important cash crops.
In the Wadi Hadhramaut, with the assistance of a pump irrigation scheme, large quantities of dates, cotton and wheat are grown.
Livestock are raised with the related industry of preparation of hides and skins as well as dyeing and weaving. The principal occupation of people in the coastal region is fishing.
Main Crops and Products

The main crops are cotton, sorghum bulrush millet, wheat, barley, sesame and dates; other crops include finger millet, tobacco, lucerne, a wide variety of temperate vegetables, citrus and both tropical and temperate-zone fruit trees.

Long-staple cotton is the major export crop. It was first introduced into the Abyan area in 1949 and is now widely grown. The cotton crop is now expected to produce some 5,000 to 6,000 tons of cotton lint and some 9,000 to 10,000 tons cotton seed annually to a value of some £2,000,000.

Fruit and vegetables are produced in increasing quantities each year and nearly all are sold in Aden through the Central Wholesale Produce Market. The annual total of fruit and vegetables passing through the Central Wholesale Produce Market amounts to some 19,000 tons, worth up to £750,000.
The annual production of hookah (hamami) tobacco from the Eastern Aden Protectorate is about 700 tons and worth some £210,000; most of it is exported.

Fisheries

The amount of fish caught in Aden waters is very closely linked with the effect of the monsoon winds and consequently tends to be erratic. However, in a good season total landings may reach 5,000 tons, of which about 1,500 tons is consumed locally, the surplus being sold overseas through a group of exporters in the Salt and Dried Fish trade. Mechanisation of the local fishing boats and improvements in equipment have helped to maintain the supply of fresh fish in Aden in spite of the fact that a number of fishermen were drawn to employment ashore in the construction of the Oil Refinery. The increase in landings at fishing villages outside Aden has also helped to steady the local supply. Foreign interests have sent representatives to the area to investigate the possibility of starting an export trade in frozen fish to Europe. There has been a 90% changeover to nylon netting and the use of synthetic ropes and floats along the entire coast. Present policy is to develop the Ring net method for enclosing schools of surface fishes of the following types: Kingfish,
Digitized by Google
THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST

Indian Mackerel, Indian Longtail Tunny and various types of Jack Mackerel. Recent assistance from the United Nations Technical Assistance Bureau is designed to develop Floating Longline methods for the capture of Yellowfin Tunny and adapt this method to local fishing vessels. The Crawfish Industry has been advanced in the last eighteen months, following active and successful marketing efforts. Cold storage facilities are provided from C.D. & W. funds.
The Co-operative and Marketing Department has introduced co-operation to the fishermen for fishing requisites in Aden waters and two societies have been formed.
The annual catch of wet fish has been estimated at 51,183 tons for the Eastern Aden Protectorate. Most of this fish is used for local consumption.
Crawfish have been discovered in commercial quantities in the Eastern Aden Protectorate and various methods of catching are being tested.

There is a market interest in the use of nylon fishing nets and this new knowledge is disseminated along the Protectorate coast by migrant fishermen and by demonstration by the staff of the Fisheries Department.
Co-operative and Marketing

The Co-operative and Marketing Department was established in mid-1955 and started field work towards the end of 1956. It has introduced and spread co-operative ideals and principles mainly among the farmers, fishermen and school students of Aden and the Protectorate of South Arabia.
The following is a list of the Co-operative and Marketing organisations in Aden and the Protectorate:—

The total membership of these societies is about 16,000.

The Central Wholesale Produce Market, which opened at Khormaksar in November, 1959, is administered by a statutory Marketing Authority under the provisions of the Produce Marketing Ordinance of Aden. Its purpose is to rationalise the wholesale of fruit and vegetables in the interests of both the producers in the Protectorate and the consumers in Aden. It is a self-financing organisation with a turn-over of 21½ million shillings.

In the Eastern Protectorate the Co-operative Marketing Department has introduced co-operation among fishermen in the Qu'aiti State for the marketing of crawfish and turtles and among farmers in the Wadi Hadhramaut for the sale of dates and the rearing of chickens. A number of societies have also been formed particularly in the pharmaceutical trade, and thrift organisations exist among Junior Secondary and Secondary schools. Cold storage, fumigating and marketing facilities are establishing an export market for crawfish and for dates.
THE STATES OF THE EASTERN PROTECTORATE
The Eastern Protectorate consists of the Qu'aiti Sultanate of Shihr and Mukalla, the Kathiri Sultanate, the Mahra Sultanate of Qishn and Soqotra.
Land Policy

Land ownership is subject to tribal law and Mohammedan Law. Except for a fractional area, land is held by the indigenous inhabitants. The local State Governments are, in some cases, important owners of land. Land is held in aboslute ownership and in trusts (waqfs).
Taxation

A small Income Tax is levied in the Qu'aiti and Kathiri States. An import tax tariff is levied on products of every kind except those intended for agricultural or marine use. This tax varies between 17% and 22%. A tax of indigenous origin analogous to a "tithe" is applied to agricultural produce, but it varies with local agricultural conditions and is not an important source of revenue. In some areas, a tax is applied to each fertilised date-palm tree each season.
Rates are collected also by municipalities and councils on market transactions, irrigation pumps, cars, bicycles and wirelessly in most areas.
Public Finance

Development Plans

The E.A.P. Development Plan 1963–68 provides for a total expenditure of £1,743,939 over a five-year period from C.D. & W. sources. Of this sum, £476,205 has been allocated to economic projects; £942,564 to the social services; and £237,670 to communications and public utilities. There is a general reserve of £87,000. Towards these projects the Qu'aiti State makes a considerable contribution each year (between £20,000 and £30,000). The education and health services, of necessity, represent the bulk of expenditure (the expenditure on education constitutes by far the largest single head—£634,527—and is almost double any other single service).
The Qu'aiti State has allocated a total of £350,000 to its own Development Plan which is dovetailed with the E.A.P. Development Plan.

STATES OF THE WESTERN PROTECTORATE : SOUTH ARABIA 59

Of this sum, the largest amounts are for education (£90,000), health (£92,000) and roads (£70,000). Water supply and fisheries are other important heads of expenditure. Up to the 31st March, 1963, the state had spent a total of £248,373 (£126,020 in 1962–63). Since 1950, considerable allocations, £153,000, have been made to agricultural development.
Communications

There are no railways in the Eastern Protectorate. A coastal road runs from Reidat al Abdal Wadud in the east through Shihr, Mukalla, Bir Ali and Balhaf to connect with the Western Protectorate. Roads run inland from Shihr and Mukalla to the Wadi Hadhramaut and from Balhaf to Azzan and Habban. The approximate total mileage of motorable tracks in the Eastern Protectorate is 2,680 miles. The desert between the Wadi Hadhramaut and Beihan is also traversed by motors. Where these cannot yet penetrate the traffic is carried on camel caravans, which still travel to Mukalla and Shihr from other parts of the Protectorate in large numbers. Donkeys are also employed for both passengers and goods. Sea communications along the coast are mainly by dhows, some of which have auxiliary engines. Small powered craft and coastal steamers make occasional and irregular visits to Mukalla and Shihr from Aden and ocean going vessels of the conventional type call at Mukalla from time to time.
THE STATES OF THE WESTERN PROTECTORATE

The principal States in the Western Protectorate are Abdali (i.e. Lahej), Alawi, Amiri, Aqrabi, Audhali, Upper Aulaqi, Lower Aulaqi, Aulaqi Sheikhdom, Baihan, Dathina, Dhala (including Qutaibi,) Fadhli, Haushabi, Lower Yafai, Upper Yafai, Sha'ib and Wahidi.
These along with the five small States in the Upper Yafai area (Bu'si, Dhudhai, Maflahi, Mausatta, Hadhrami) comprise the States of this part of the Protectorate.
Land Policy

Land ownership is subject to tribal law and Mohammedan Law. Except for a fractional area, land is held by the indigenous inhabitants. The local State Governments are, in some cases, important owners of land. Land is held in absolute ownership and in trust (waqfs). Land settlement is in progress in the Abyan area.
