Communications

A system of undeveloped but motorable roads links the principal towns and villages of the Protectorate, suitable for heavy lorries and vehicles of the landrover type. Surfaces vary widely from tarmacadam, as yet only in short stretches in the Abyan and Lahej areas relatively close to Aden, to open desert in the north towards Beihan. The principal route eastwards from Aden into the Protectorate lies for the first forty miles along a sand beach, where the going, at low tide, is excellent. For much of the area, the principal routes traverse mountain areas following the lines of seasonally filled water courses. There is an ambitious programme for road building, the pace of execution of which depends on funds being available. Despite the natural handicaps to easy land communication, the flow of passengers and goods is maintained with rare interruption. Apart from trunk routes, which are a Federal responsibility, local routes are maintained by the member States of the Federation from State sources. Donkey and camel caravans are still used.
Regular air services connect Aden with the main centres of population in the Western Protectorate—Dhala, Mukeiras, Beihan, Dathina, Ataq, Mahfid.
Sea communications along the coast are mainly by dhow.
There are no railways in the Western Protectorate.
FEDERATION OF SOUTH ARABIA
Population
The population of the Federation, including Aden, is currently estimated at 700,000, and the area at 22,000 square miles.
History

On the 11th February, 1959, a Federation of six States in the Western Aden Protectorate, called the Federation of Arab Amirates of the South, was inaugurated and a treaty was signed between the United Kingdom and the Federation. The six federated states were the Amirates of Beihan and of Dhala, the Fadhli, Audhali and Lower Yafai Sultanates and the Upper Aulaqi Sheikhdom. The treaty extended Her Majesty's protection to the Arab Amirates of the South and provided for military and financial assistance for it. The treaty also contained a provision for the accession of other states to the Federation (see Cmnd. 665). The Sultanate of Lahej acceded to the Federation in October, 1959, and three further accessions occurred in February, 1960—the state of Dathina, the Lower Aulaqi Sultanate and the Aqrabi Sheikhdom. In April, 1962, the Wahidi Sultanates joined the Federation. The Federation of the Arab Amirates of the South became known as the Federation of South Arabia on the 3rd May, 1962. Aden acceded in January, 1963, followed subsequently by the Haushabi Sultanate and the Shaib Sheikhdom in March of that year. There are now 14 member states of the Federation.
C 2
THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST
Administration

The legislative body of the Federation is the Federal Council, which consists of 94 members, each state being entitled to be represented by 6 members with the exception of the Aqrabi Sheikhdom (1 member), Aden (24) and the Sheikhdom of Shaib (3). Each state is free to select its representatives to the Federal Council by whatever means it considers appropriate.

The executive of the Federation is the Supreme Council consisting of 15 Ministers elected by and from Members of the Federal Council for a period of office not exceeding 5 years. The chairmanship of the Supreme Council is held by Ministers in rotation.

For internal security within the Federation, the Government Guards and the Tribal Guards of the Federated States have now been amalgamated into one force known as the Federal Guard. The Federal Minister of Internal Security is responsible to the Supreme Council for the control of this force.
In November, 1961, the Aden Protectorate Levies became the Federal Army which constitutes the defence force of the Federation.
Taxation

The levying and collection of customs dues is a Federal responsibility and receipts from customs dues represent the largest individual item in the Federal revenue. Taxes are levied on alcoholic beverages, tobacco and manufactures thereof, non-potable alcoholic mixtures, qat, aerated waters and petroleum products.
Public Finance
The revenue and expenditure figures for the Federation of South Arabia for the period 1960–63 are:—
Development

The Federal Government is engaged in the preparation of a development plan which is not yet completed but which will cover education, agriculture, fisheries, communications, health, marketing and information services.
The Federation of South Arabia, which is an administering authority for C.D. & W. Schemes, has been allocated £1,250,000 from C.D. & W. Funds for the period 1963–66.

In the past the largest development scheme has been that in Abyan; this was initiated in 1947 with the aim of achieving a better use of land and water resources and has developed at a satisfactory rate in all fields, where adequate staff, machinery and materials could be mustered. The scheme is administered by the Abyan Board, which consists of representatives of the two States in the Abyan area, with Governmental advice available, and which works in partnership with landowners and owner-occupiers or tenant farmers. Cotton is grown as a main crop and marketed by the Board.
An area of approximately 50,000 acres is now controlled for irrigation farming out of an estimated gross area of 120,000 acres.
Education
The Federal Ministry of Education is divided into two parts—Aden and the States.

In Aden the Education system consists of 4 years Primary, 3 years Intermediate and 4 years Secondary schooling, with facilities for a two-year "A" level course. Primary and Intermediate education is provided free for Adeni boys and girls; moderate fees are charged for Secondary education, with remission granted to poor pupils, while allowances are made to teachers in training. In addition, generous grants are made to Grant-in-aid schools. There are 21 Government Primary schools, 12 Grant-aided and 5 Private schools, providing education for 7,857 boys and 4,003 girls. Intermediate education is provided at 7 Government, 10 Grant-aided and 4 Private schools for 3,968 boys and 1,924 girls. Secondary education is provided mainly at 4 Government schools (3 for boys and 1 for girls) and the Technical Institute in Maalla with its branch at Little Aden (providing a total of 2,184 places), a new Men's Teacher Training Centre (78 men were enrolled in 1962) and a modern Women's Teacher Training Centre was opened in 1961, with 23 students (65 women were enrolled in 1962). Adult education is provided by part-time and evening classes at the Technical Institute, by evening and G.C.E. classes, special classes for the Police, Arabic language courses for non-Arabs and various courses for women at the Government Secondary School and at the Besse Centre. The expenditure on Aden education has risen from £6,600 in 1936 to £784,090 in 1962–63. There is a provision under the current Development plan (1960–64) for capital expenditure estimated at £550,000.

In the States there are 21 Primary schools providing education for 10,538 boys and 1,025 girls and 10 Intermediate schools providing education for 1,094 boys. In addition, secondary education is provided for 35 boys now in their first year at the new Al Ittihad College and teacher training is provided at a new Men's Teacher Training Centre for 40 students.

The Federal Ministry also undertakes a considerable amount of administration for the Overseas Studies Board. In January, 1960, an Aden Students' Office was established in London, with a Students' Liaison Officer. At the end of 1962, there were 88 scholars taking courses in higher education abroad; of these, 40 were provided for by the Government, the remainder being privately sponsored students. In addition, there is a considerable number of private students.
Health
The Federal Ministry of Health contains three main divisions—Curative, Port Health and Public Health.

The Curative Services are based on the Queen Elizabeth Hospital, two maternity clinics, three rural hospitals, five general dispensaries and over forty health units. The latter are satellites of the rural hospitals. Specialist facilities for surgery, medicine, ophthalmology, oto-rhino-laryngology and dermatology are based on the main general hospital in Aden. A mental hospital and infectious diseases hospital are now being constructed and four rural hospitals and many new health centres are to be built in the near future.

In the State of Aden, Public Health is under the control of the Aden Municipality and the townships of Sheikh Othman and Little Aden. In the rural areas, Public Health comes under the State Councils and small township authorities. In 1962, surveys were carried out in the fields of malaria, bilharzia and malnutrition. Malaria control is now being organised on a pre-eradication basis but the eventual eradication depends on the co-operation of neighbouring territories.
The estimates of the Ministry of Health for 1963–64 show a total expenditure of £780,555.
Civil Aviation
Aviation matters in the Federation, aerodromes and ancillary services, including the safety of aircraft, are under the exclusive authority of the Federal Government.

The main Civil Air Terminal is located at Khormaksar in Aden some 7 miles from the seaport and the aerodrome lies across the isthmus between the upper reaches of the inner harbour and the shore of the Gulf of Aden. The aerodrome is jointly used by the Royal Air Force and the Federal Department of Civil Aviation. The main runway, which is 10 feet above sea level, is bitumenised and has a length of 8,386 feet; its form of construction permits its use by the heaviest of modern aircraft. Navigational services for the common use of military and civil aircraft are jointly provided by the Royal Air Force and the Federal Department of Civil Aviation.

Twelve airlines operate scheduled services from, to and through the airport: Aden Airways, Air-India International, Alitalia, B.O.A.C., East African Airways Corporation, Ethiopian Air Lines, Ghana Airways, Middle East Airlines, Saudi Arabian Airlines, Sudan Airways, United Arab Airlines and Yemen Airlines. Seven of these airlines operate Comet or Boeing 707 jet aircraft.
Operating a fleet of six DC 3 and two Viscount aircraft the local airline, Messrs. Aden Airways Limited, an associate of B.O.A.C., maintains a

domestic network of regular services to destinations in the Federation of South Arabia and the Protectorate as well as scheduled services on international trunk routes to East Africa, Ethiopia, the Somali Republic, Saudi Arabia, Sudan, Bahrein and Egypt.
Broadcasting

The South Arabian Broadcasting Service, which till 1963 was known as the Aden Broadcasting Service, and as such was inaugurated in 1954, now transmits for 11 hours daily in Arabic with $7\frac{1}{2}$ KW on 5,060 Kc/s and 5 KW on 785 Kc/s. It is estimated that about 200,000 receivers are in use in the Federation. These numbers are increasing rapidly with the advent in recent years of inexpensive transistor-type radios. Programmes in English are also broadcast daily on the network operated by the official Forces Broadcasting Service and the voluntary Aden Forces Broadcasting Association. Primarily for Service personnel and families in the Middle East Command, these broadcasts, which are of increasing popularity, provide a valuable complementary service to those operated by the Broadcasting Service in Arabic.

The South Arabian Broadcasting Service is now under the exclusive authority of the Federal Government which may shortly enact legislation making the service into a public corporation which will broadcast 3 to 4 hours television nightly in both Arabic and English. Both sound and vision will be commercial.
Trade

Commerce and Industry are concurrent subjects in the Federal Constitution—i.e., matters in respect of which both the Federation and the State have legislative and executive authority, but there is a Federal Ministry of Commerce and Industry to provide general co-ordination and direction.
The principal imports are grain, flour, rice, dates, sugar, sesame oil, tea, kerosene, spice, cloth, cotton piece-goods, tobacco, cigarettes, soap and glassware.
The principal exports are cotton, coffee, skins and hides, lime, Hamami tobacco, cattle, ghee, sheep, goats and fish.
The bulk of the trade with the Eastern Aden Protectorate goes by sea to the port of Mukalla. No reliable figures are available.

The trade of Aden is mainly transhipment and entrepôt, the port serving as a centre of distribution to and from neighbouring territories. Traditional trade is mainly in textiles, gums and resins, grains and flour, coffee, tea, hides and skins, raw cotton and cottonseed and cheap consumer goods. There is also trade in dates, sugar, spices, oils, tobacco and shells and a flourishing trade in luxury goods such as cameras, watches, binoculars and cosmetics, which are sold to passengers coming ashore from ships refuelling in Aden harbour.

THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST
THE ISLANDS
KAMARAN

The island of Kamaran in the Red Sea (area 70 square miles) has been in British occupation since 1915. The High Commissioner for Aden has direct legislative powers over the island provided for in the Kamaran Order in Council, 1963, and the Kamaran Royal Instructions, 1963. The island is administered by the Commissioner for Kamaran and Perim. Until 1953, a quarantine station was situated in Kamaran for pilgrims on their way to Jeddah by sea; this has been closed. Expenditure on the administration of Kamaran in 1962–63 was £38,172.
PERIM

Perim Island was first occupied by the French in 1738. In 1799, the British took formal possession but evacuated the island the same year. It was reoccupied by the British in January, 1851, and was later used as a coaling station.

The Perim Order in Council, 1963, and the Perim Royal Instructions, 1963, vest the High Commissioner for Aden with legislative powers, and the island is administered by the Commissioner for Kamaran and Perim. Expenditure on the administration of Perim in 1962–63 was £22,123.
KURIA MURIA

The five Kuria Muria Islands were ceded to the United Kingdom in 1854 by the Sultan of Oman for the purpose of a cable station. The Kuria Islands Order in Council, 1963, and the Kuria Muria Islands Royal Instructions, 1963, vest the High Commissioner of Aden with legislative powers over the Islands but administration is carried out through the Political Resident, Persian Gulf.

ANTIGUA
Area

The area of Antigua is 108 square miles, the Dependency of Barbuda 62 square miles, the Dependency of Redonda (uninhabited) half a square mile. Barbuda lies 25 miles to the north, Redonda 25 miles to the south-west of the main island.
Population
The population at the 1960 census numbered 54,304.
Principal Town
St. John's, the capital, had a population of about 21,600 at the census.
Digitized by Google
THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST
Geographical Features

The western part of the island of Antigua is composed entirely of volcanic rocks (highest point Boggy Peak, 1,330 feet). The eastern and northern parts are of limestone, below 500 feet above sea level, and a central plain stretches diagonally across the island. Barbuda is very flat with a large lagoon on the west side. There are no rivers.
History

The first settlers were a few English who arrived in 1632. In 1663 Charles II made a formal grant to Lord Willoughby, who at once proceeded to send out large numbers of colonists. For a short time the island was in French occupation, but in 1667 it was declared an English possession in terms of the Treaty of Breda and has remained British from that date.
Constitution
Following decisions taken at a Constitutional Conference in London in June 1959, a new constitution came into force on 1st January 1960. The main features are:

(a) The post of Governor is abolished and the Administrator has become Her Majesty's Representative in the Territory. The Administrator is now appointed by Her Majesty and exercises such of the former powers of the Governor as have been retained under the new constitution (these powers are considerably reduced under the new constitution);

(b) The Executive Council has the general control and direction of the government of the Territory. It is presided over by the Administrator and there are one ex officio member (the principal law officer) and five unofficial members (including the Chief Minister and three other Ministers). Of these the four Ministers are appointed from the elected members of the Legislative Council and the other unofficial member is appointed from either the elected or the Nominated members of the Legislative Council. The Administrator appoints as Chief Minister the member of the Legislative Council who, in his judgment, is most likely to command a majority in the Legislative Council. The other unofficial members are appointed by the Administrator on the advice of the Chief Minister. Ministers are assigned responsibility for any Government business (including financial matters) except criminal proceedings, internal security, the audit of public accounts and the public service;

(c) The Legislative Council is composed of one ex officio member (the principal law officer), two Nominated members appointed by the Administrator in his discretion and ten members elected by universal adult suffrage. It is presided over by a Speaker elected by the Council either from within or without the Council; and having only a casting vote. There is a Deputy Speaker elected from within the Council;

(d) The appointment, dismissal and disciplinary control of public officers is vested in the Administrator in his discretion acting after consultation with an advisory Public Service Commission. The Administrator is also responsible in his discretion for appointing the Head of the joint Police Force of Antigua, Montserrat and the Virgin Islands, and for appointing gazetted ranks of the joint Police Force after consulting the single advisory Police Service Commission which has been established

to serve the territories of Antigua, Dominica, Grenada, Montserrat, St. Kitts, St. Lucia, St. Vincent and the Virgin Islands. Further details about the Police Service Commission and about the Judiciary are given on page 196.
Land Policy

Land may not be alienated or leased to aliens without the consent of the Administrator in Council. Except in a few cases, alien occupation of land is confined to leases for not more than 99 years. There is a small amount of Crown land which, when not used for public purposes, may be leased or sold.
Taxation

The main forms of taxation are income tax, trade tariffs and excise duties. The rates of individual income tax range from 2½ cents to 65 cents per $ of chargeable income. Company tax is 40% of chargeable income.

Provision has been made for double taxation relief with the United Kingdom, Canada, New Zealand, Sweden, Denmark, Norway and the U.S.A., and consideration is being given to extending the arrangements to other countries.

Provision was made for a grant by Her Majesty's Government in aid of the Colony until 1962. No provision was made in 1963.
Currency

The currency is issued by the Board of the Commissioners of Currency, British Caribbean Territories (Eastern group) consisting of Barbados, British Guiana, the Leeward Islands, Trinidad and Tobago and the Windward Islands. Currency notes are of denominations $1, $2, $5, $10, $20 and $100, and coins of 50 cents, 25 cents, 10 cents, 5 cents, 2 cents and 1 cent. The West Indian $ has a fixed equivalent value of 4s. 2d., that is $4.80 to £1 sterling.
Development Plans

Antigua was originally allocated £499,000 under the Colonial Development and Welfare Act of 1945. There were further allocations of £210,000 between 1952 and 1955, of £680,000 in 1955, £95,000 in 1958, £680,000 in 1959, and £60,000 in 1963 making £2,224,000 in all. The current development planning period ends in 1964.
Education
There are 33 Government elementary (primary and post-primary) schools and nine recognized private schools, with a total enrolment of 13,808 pupils. The ten secondary schools—four grant-

aided, two Government and four private—have a total enrolment of 2,923 pupils. A Teachers' Training College accommodates 30 resident female teachers and 15 resident male teachers. The graduation standard from this college approximates to that of the Teachers' Training Colleges of the larger British Caribbean Colonies. There are 246 students studying abroad. Government expenditure on education in 1962 was $807,902.
Libraries
The Public Library in St. John's has a book stock of 30,645 volumes. Library services have recently been established in the rural areas.
Health

The expenditure on medical and allied services in 1962 was $1,189,957 compared with $86,318 spent in 1935. In 1962 the Hospital (180 beds) treated 3,425 cases. Apart from the dispensaries maintained at the residences of District Medical Officers, there are three health centres and 16 dispensaries.
Communications

Coolidge Airfield, which was constructed by the American Army Air Force as a military airfield in 1942, is now used by the local Government as a civil airfield. It is used mainly by Pan American Airways, British West Indian Airways, Trans-Canada Airlines and B.O.A.C. with direct services to other West Indian Islands and to the United States of America. Travel to other parts of the world is made by connexions from Trinidad, Jamaica and America. One runway was extended in December 1961 to accommodate a jet service.
There are 68 miles of main roads and 90 miles of secondary roads.
Principal Occupations
The principal occupations are agricultural. In 1962 about 20% of employed persons were engaged in sugar production.
Main Crops and Products
The main crops and products are sugar and cotton.

BAHAMA ISLANDS
Area, etc.
The total land area of the Colony is 4,404 square miles. It is separated on the west from Florida by the Straits of Florida and on the south from Cuba by the Old Bahama and Nicholas Channels.
Population
The population at the end of 1962 was estimated at 112,556. Except for about 20%, the inhabitants are of the negro race. English is universally spoken.
Digitized by Google
THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST
Principal Town
Nassau, the capital, is the chief port and the only town of any size. It is situated on the island of New Providence, the population of which is estimated to be 57,858.
Geographical Features

The Colony consists of an archipelago of about 700 islands, of which only 20 are inhabited and over 2,000 rocks. The islands are generally long, narrow and low-lying and are of aeolian limestone formation. The soil, except in small pockets, is shallow and overlays hard rock. On the whole, it is suitable only for peasant farming. Conditions are sub-tropical and both temperate and tropical trees grow literally side by side. It is probable that at one time all the islands were thickly wooded but little forest now remains except on Abaco, Andros and Grand Bahama, where there are extensive pine forests.
Climate

The winter climate is delightful. Frost is unknown, the average temperature is $70^\circ$ , the rainfall is slight and cool breezes prevail. In July, August and September the heat ranges from $80^\circ$ to $90^\circ$ and though the islands are never unhealthy, the change in temperature throughout the 24 hours is so small that those who remain in the Colony for the whole year may find the long summer somewhat enervating.
The rainfall averages about 40 inches a year. The rainy months are May, June, September and October.
History

San Salvador, one of the islands composing the Colony, and identical with Watling's Island, was the first land discovered by Columbus on his voyage in 1492. A few years later all the Arawak inhabitants were transported to work in the Hispaniola mines. It does not appear that the Spaniards had settlements on any of the islands of the group at any time. In 1649 Parliament, on the petition of William Sayle, a former Governor of Bermuda, and others, passed "An Act for the Adventurers for the Eleutherian Islands" which constituted Sayle and his associates the proprietors of the islands. Notwithstanding an earlier Royal Grant in 1629 and the Cromwellian Act of 1649, Charles II in 1670 granted the islands to six of the Lords Proprietors of Carolina. Before the 1670 Grant the inhabitants of the islands had organized the settlement and instituted a form of government, which included an elective House of Assembly, and had selected their Governor. His appointment was later confirmed by the Lords Proprietors and a regular system of government was established including a parliament, the lower house of which was elective, and this was continued with several breaks until the civil and military government of the islands was resumed by the Crown in 1717.

The settlement on New Providence was sacked by the Spaniards on several occasions between 1680 and 1684. In 1684 all the inhabitants were driven away, and it was not until 1686 that the settlement was re-formed by their return, principally from Jamaica. By 1703 it had reached some importance, when it was almost annihilated by the French and Spaniards. However, a year or so after this the dispersed inhabitants returned once more. The islands became a regular rendezvous for pirates and this finally determined

the Crown to resume the civil and military government of the place; since 1717 there has been a continuous line of Royal Governors. The islands were surrendered to a fleet of the American rebels in 1776, and again to the Spaniards in 1782, but were re-taken by a British force under Colonel Deveaux and British possession was confirmed by the Peace of Versailles, 1783. After the American Revolution a number of loyalists settled throughout the Bahamas group and received substantial assistance from England, and in 1787 the Lords Proprietors surrendered all their proprietary rights to the King for the sum of £12,000, which was provided by Parliament.

The constitution of the Bahamas was eventually settled in 1729, when the Crown assumed direct control of the colony. The government was modelled upon that of England in the early days, the Governor representing the Sovereign, and the nominated Legislative Council and the elected House of Assembly representing respectively the Houses of Lords and Commons.

Up to January, 1964, representative, but not responsible government existed. The executive government was in the hands of a Governor, appointed by the Crown, who had the power of veto, and was advised by an Executive Council of not more than nine members of whom six were unofficials. Various executive powers and the right to enact certain subsidiary legislation were vested by law in the Governor in Council. A Legislative Council was created as a separate Council by Royal Letters Patent in 1841; and in 1963 it consisted of eleven members nominated by the Crown, of whom nine were unofficials.

The General Assembly Elections Act of 1959 provided for, inter alia , adult male suffrage, and a limited second vote in respect of ownership and/or rental of real property, the latter to be exercised only in a constituency other than that in which the elector resided. The Act also provided for the creation of two additional seats for the Southern and Eastern Districts of New Providence and the redistribution of the New Providence seats after by-elections to fill the new seats. Accordingly from 1960 the House of Assembly was composed of 33 members.
A law was promulgated with effect from mid-1962 extending the franchise to women and making women eligible for election to seats in the House of Assembly.
Constitution and Administration

At the Bahamas Constitutional Conference held in London in May, 1963, the Secretary of State agreed to further constitutional changes, and these were embodied in a new Constitution which came into effect on the 7th January, 1964.
The bicameral Legislature has been reconstituted to consist of an Upper House called the Senate and a Lower House called the House of Assembly.

The Senate consists of fifteen members of whom eight are appointed by the Governor after consultation with the Premier and such other persons as he may in his discretion decide to consult, five by the Governor on the advice of the Premier, and two by the Governor on the advice of the Leader of the Opposition.

The House of Assembly remains unchanged until it is dissolved in the ordinary course. Thereafter the House of Assembly will consist of thirty-eight elected members, of whom not less than eighteen nor more than twenty-two members will represent Out Island constituencies, and not
Digitized by Google
BAHAMAS

less than sixteen nor more than twenty members will represent New Providence constituencies. The next General Election will be contested on the basis of twenty-one seats for the Out Islands and seventeen for New Providence.

The Cabinet will consist of the Premier and not fewer than eight other Ministers. The Governor appoints as Premier the person who appears to him to be best able to command a majority in the House of Assembly. The remaining Ministers are appointed by the Governor on the advice of the Premier.
Land Policy
The Status of Aliens Act 1950 empowers an alien to acquire and hold property within the Colony.
Taxation

There is neither income tax nor excise duty. The general Customs duty is 20% ad valorem plus 7½% ad valorem emergency tax. Staple foodstuffs pay a low specific duty and no emergency tax; spirits and tobacco are taxed at higher rates than the general and gasoline pays a specific duty with additional sales tax. Preferential margins are designed to implement the Ottawa and Anglo-American trade agreements.
Public Finance
Revenue Expenditure

The main sources of revenue are Customs dues and receipts from fees, Post Office, Licences and public utilities. In 1962 £5,338,857 of the revenue was derived from import duties. For the same year the main heads of expenditure were :—
Development Board (£1,004,132), Medical Department (£972,066), Public Works (£1,095,032), Education (£770,962) and Telecommunications (£636,459).
In December 1962 the outstanding amount of the authorized Public Debt was £1,610,526.
Currency

The legal tender currency is British sterling, although United States and Canadian currencies are also generally accepted in the Colony. Currency notes of the denominations of 4s., 10s., £1 and £5 are issued by the Bahamas Government. Accounts are kept in sterling. There are branch banks of the Royal Bank of Canada, Bank of Nova Scotia, Canadian Bank of Commerce, Barclays Bank, D.C. and O., Bank of London and Montreal, First National City Bank of New York and Chase Manhattan Bank in Nassau.
Development

The development of a Free Port in the island of Grand Bahama offers excellent prospects for employment in the future. The proposed development, financed by American capital, embraces the construction of a large deep-water harbour at Hawksbill Creek. The Free Port Authority has acquired 50,000 acres of land and plans have been drawn up for zoned areas in the

vicinity of the proposed harbour, for industrial projects of all kinds and town planning on the most up-to-date lines. This large-scale project is not a Government scheme but was approved by the Bahamas Legislature. Licences to operate within the Free Port area are granted by the Free Port Authority.

The Bahamas Government has undertaken to supply administrative, police, customs and immigration staff which will remain under its control. The Free Port Authority will provide its own medical and educational facilities but there is provision for inspection of these services by Bahamas Government medical and education officers. In general the closest liaison has been established between the Government and the Authority.
Education

A new comprehensive Education Act known as the Education Act 1962, and modelled in principle on the 1944 Education Act in the United Kingdom, came into effect on 1st September, 1962. The new Act places all government effort in the field of education under one authority, the Board of Education, and provides the machinery for the development of a modern system of education in the future. Under the Act, education is compulsory between the ages of 5 and 14. Of a total of 144 Government primary schools, 130 are rural, all-age schools.

Secondary education is provided by the Board of Education and by independent religious authorities. Of a total of 15 Secondary Schools, 5 are owned by the government and 4 are grant-aided; 6 of them (all independent) have their own primary departments.
There is one reformatory school for boys under the Industrial School Act and one for girls.
No local provision is made for Higher Education and students seeking this type of training must go abroad. Bahamians studying abroad in 1962–63 session were:
116 in the United Kingdom
9 in Canada
160 in the United States of America
Total 285
The Teachers' Training College which was temporarily closed in 1957 was re-opened in September 1961. In addition, the Board now has a scholarship scheme for:
(a) Refresher courses for experienced teachers.
(b) Degree courses.
(c) Specialist Diploma courses. All these scholarships are tenable in the U.K.

THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST
Libraries
There are three Public Libraries in Nassau and 27 in the Out Islands.
Health

The medical needs of the Colony are met by the Princess Margaret Hospital (General), Prospect Hospital (Isolation and Geriatrics), the Sandilands Mental Hospital, Out Island Clinics on every major island, Cottage Hospitals at Eleuthera and Inagua, and the private Rassin Clinic. There are also District Medical Officers in Out Islands. In addition there is a Public Health Nursing Service.
Hospital services are chargeable at sub-economic rates and provided free to needy persons. Clinics of all kinds are held and there is a schools medical service.

In 1962 the in-patients numbered 11,408 with 240,773 in-patient days' treatment, and out-patients 63,296. The principal diseases are tuberculosis, respiratory complaints, general abdominal complaints, hypertension and malnutrition.
Expenditure in 1962 was £972,066, compared with £25,695 in 1936.
Communications

Nassau International Airport is the main airfield in the Bahamas. It provides 24-hours service for long range international and local airlines. In addition, there are 23 landing strips completed in the various Out Islands. Scheduled international air services are operated into Nassau by B.O.A.C. from London via New York and Bermuda, and from Montego Bay, Jamaica. Pan American World Airways operate daily flights from Miami and New York, Trans-Canada Airlines from Montreal via Tampa, Florida, Mackey Airlines from Tampa, Fort Lauderdale and Palm Beach. Bahamas Airways Limited operates between Miami and Nassau several times daily.
Internal air services are run by Bahamas Airways Limited, and by Out Island Air Services with a fleet of amphibian aircraft.
During 1962 16,040 landings were recorded at the main airport.

On Eleuthera there is an asphalt main road 60 miles in length covering the greater part of the Island and on Grand Bahama, 65 miles of motor road has been built. Asphalt roads are under construction at Andros, Exuma, Eleuthera, Cat Island, Spanish Wells and San Salvador, where previously only rough tracks existed.
There are no railways in the Colony.

There are regular freight services between the Colony and New York and Florida ; Halifax and Montreal (Canadian National Steamship Line) ; and the United Kingdom (Royal Mail Line and Pacific Steam Navigation Company). West India Steamships Ltd. operate a service between Nassau and Inagua with intermediate stops.
In 1962 6,162 ships entered and 4,843 were cleared at Nassau. Their total tonnage was 10,319,086, of which 1,288,218 tons was British.
Ships also call at Inagua and Pine Ridge to take on salt and lumber exports.
Sound and Television Broadcasting

Government owns, and the Bahamas Broadcasting and Television Commission operates, a broadcasting station located in Nassau. The power of its transmitter is 5kW., frequency 1,540 kc/s, and call sign ZNS. Commercial operation began in 1950, though a broadcasting station has existed since 1936. The Commission took over on 1st January 1957. The broadcast receiver licence fee was abolished in 1954. At that time there were 5,990 licence holders. Government supplied and maintains 60 community receivers in school-houses in the Out Islands where there may be as many as 200 listeners in the evenings.
Principal Occupations
The tourist traffic continues to be the main industry of the Colony and is the chief source of revenue and of dollars.
The following table shows the number and nationality of visitors to New Providence during the years 1957-62:
Nationality—per cent.
Agricultural work in the United States provides employment for an average of 1,195 unskilled Bahamian labourers and their earnings in 1962 amounted to $2,925,765.
Main Crops and Products
The main crops are fresh vegetables, tomatoes, okra, pineapples, peas, citrus, bananas, avocados and sisal.
The estimated livestock population is: horses and mules 3,520, cattle 3,000, sheep 22,000, goats 14,000, swine 10,500, and poultry 600,000.
Exports of timber in 1962 totalled 17 M. ft., valued at £625.

Crawfishing and scale fishing are extensively carried out during the open season (October 1st- March 15th). The bulk of the catch is exported to the United States. The following marine products are also exported :—sponges, salt, turtle shell, beach shells and marine curios.

There are four canning plants in the Colony which are engaged principally in canning tomatoes and pineapples. Straw products are manufactured as cottage industries and the raw material for this work is obtained chiefly from palm fronds and sisal fibre. Most of the canneries' output is consumed locally.

In 1938 23.8% of total imports came from the U.K.; 18.5% from other Commonwealth countries and 57.7% from foreign countries. The corresponding percentages in 1962 were 16.6, 11.5 and 71.9.
In 1938 22.6% of exports went to the U.K.; 22.5% to other Commonwealth countries, and 54.9% to foreign countries. The corresponding percentages in 1962 were 4.8, 1.9 and 93.3.
Nearly all the Colony's requirements are imported. The chief imports are apparel, liquors, machinery, motor vehicles, electrical apparatus, petroleum oils and foodstuffs of all kinds.
Marketing and Co-operative Organizations

The Bahamas Produce Exchange in Nassau handles much of the agricultural produce of the Colony. It assists the farmers in New Providence and Out Islands in disposing of their produce without glutting the market and depressing prices unnecessarily.

There are no co-operative societies in the Colony other than 17 Tomato Growers' Associations on the Islands of Eleuthera and Cat Island and 21 active Farmers' Associations situated throughout the Colony. These associations are assisted by the Board of Agriculture to the extent of advancing seed and fertilizer, etc., for the tomato and other crops. The Board markets the crops and deducts their advances from the proceeds of each consignor.

BARBADOS
Area, etc.
Barbados is the most easterly of the Caribbean islands. Its total area is 166 square miles.
Population
On the 31st December 1962, the estimated population was 235,132.
Principal Town
Bridgetown, the capital, is the only town. At the 1960 census its population was 11,452.
Geographical Features

The Island is comparatively flat, rising in a series of tablelands marked by well-defined terraces to the highest point (1,104 feet) at Mount Hillaby. The north-east corner of the Island, the Scotland area, is broken country, much eroded and rather barren. The formation of the rest of the Island is coral limestone. There are no rivers, but deep gullies which fill with water during heavy rain have cut their way through the coral terraces in many places. Indigenous forest covers about 46 acres.
Digitized by Google
BARBADOS
Climate

The climate is extremely pleasant. North-easterly trade winds blow steadily from December to June but during the remainder of the year, the wet season, the wind moves to the south-east and is less strong, resulting in humid, hotter conditions. The average temperature is $79.8^\circ$ . The rainfall is very varied : in the high central district the yearly average is 75 inches while in some of the low-lying coastal areas the average is 50 inches.
History

Uncertainty remains on some points in the early history of Barbados and differing versions have appeared from time to time. The brief account which follows is based on what is taken to be the most authentic version.

As far as is known the original inhabitants were the Arawak Indians who migrated from the mainland of South America. When they arrived is not known, but relics of their occupation down to the latter years of the sixteenth century have been discovered in various parts of the Island. By the time the English settlers came they had disappeared, partly through Carib raids from the neighbouring islands, and partly through the Spaniards who deported them to work in the mines of Hispaniola.

In spite of the belief to the contrary, there is no satisfactory evidence that the Olive Branch under Captain Catlin ever touched at Barbados when it was driven off its course from England to the River Wiapoco settlement in 1605 and fetched up at St. Lucia. The first Englishman to visit Barbados may have been Captain Simon Gordan, who claimed that he had landed some time before the settlement of 1627 and had found the Island uninhabited. He may, however, have been on a ship under the command of Sir Thomas Warner that visited sometime between 1620 and 1624 and found the Island suffering from such a severe drought that the latter preferred settlement of his colonists at St. Kitts. In 1624 or early 1625 a ship, belonging to Sir William Courteen and under the command of Captain John Powell, touched at Barbados, when en route from Pernambuco to England ; some men were landed who erected a cross and on a tree nearby inscribed "James K. of E. and of this Island". On receiving Powell's information about Barbados Sir William Courteen fitted out an expedition to the Island under the command of his informant ; but Powell, who carried letters of marque, captured a Spanish or Portuguese prize and returned with it to Cowes in 1626. Courteen then fitted out a second expedition and in February 1627, the William and John under the command of Captain Henry Powell—Captain John Powell's younger brother—landed 80 settlers. During 1627 and 1628 the colony grew rapidly and by the end of 1628 it was claimed that Courteen had planted 1,850 settlers. The Earl of Carlisle obtained a grant from Charles I for all the Caribbean islands and landed settlers in 1628. After his death his son leased the Island in 1647 for 21 years to Lord Willoughby of Parham who assumed the government of the Island and during the Civil War held it for the Royalists until his capitulation to the Cromwellian fleet in 1652. A Governor was appointed by the Commonwealth in place of Lord Willoughby and at the Restoration the claims of the Carlisle Patent were revived. An agreement was reached by which the Patent was surrendered to the Crown for compensation in the form of $4\frac{1}{2}\%$ duty on exports. This duty was a continual

source of complaint but was not abolished until 1838 by Act of Imperial Parliament. The importance of the agreement, however, is that it marked the end of proprietary rule. Since its settlement Barbados has been a possession of Great Britain.
Constitution

Barbados has one of the oldest constitutions in the Commonwealth and one in which conventions play an important part. Amendments to the constitution providing for full internal self-government came into operation on the 16th October 1961.

The Legislature consists of the Governor; an Upper House—The Legislative Council—consisting of members (usually about 15) appointed by Her Majesty; and a Lower House—the House of Assembly—consisting of 24 members elected by universal adult suffrage. Members of the Legislative Council may be appointed for periods of up to 15 years but are normally appointed for five years at a time. The life of a House of Assembly is limited to five years.

The revised Constitutional Instruments include provision for a change in name of the Executive Council to the Privy Council; formal provision for the Cabinet; and the establishment of Judicial and Legal Service, Public Service and Police Service Commissions with executive powers to deal with appointments, dismissals and disciplinary control of members of the services for which they are responsible.

The Privy Council consists of the Governor, the Attorney-General and other members (at present three, including the Premier) appointed by Her Majesty. It advises the Governor in the exercise of the Royal Prerogative of Mercy and in the exercise of his disciplinary powers over members of the Public Service and the Police Service who may appeal to the Privy Council against disciplinary action taken against them.

The Executive Committee consists of the Governor, the Attorney-General, not less than one member of the Legislative Council (at present one) and not less than four members of the House of Assembly (at present six). Since most of the powers and functions of the Governor-in-Executive Committee have been delegated to Ministers or to the Cabinet, the Executive Committee now survives for a limited number of largely formal functions only.
The Cabinet consists of the Premier and not less than five other Ministers (at present six) appointed by the Governor on the advice of the Premier, and it is the principal instrument of policy.

The Governor appoints as Premier the person who appears to him to be best able to command a majority in the House of Assembly. On the Premier's advice the Governor also appoints other Ministers who become the members of the Executive Committee appointed from the House of Assembly, and a Minister without Portfolio who becomes the member of the Executive Committee appointed from the Legislative Council.
Land Policy
The systems of land tenure are similar to those of the United Kingdom.
Taxation
Income tax is the main form of direct taxation. The tax rates range from 3 cents on every complete $ of taxable income up to $500, to
Digitized by Google
THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST

75 cents on every complete $ of taxable income beyond $24,000. Tax is not paid on incomes below $800 per annum in the case of single individuals and $1,400 in the case of married persons. A wife's earned income allowance not exceeding $300 is granted. Deductions from assessable income of $270 to $600 (varying according to the age of the child, and educational establishment at which full time instruction is being received) are allowed for children and $240 for other dependants, in the latter case not exceeding two. Deductions are also permitted in respect of life insurances paid. Medical expenses may be deducted in excess of $20 but not exceeding in the aggregate $100, in the case of a tax-payer of married status, or $50 in the case of any other person, plus $25 for each child, not exceeding two. Companies pay a flat rate of 40%. The rate for life assurance companies and building societies is the average paid by an individual with a taxable income of $4,800.
A non-cumulative system of Pay As You Earn is in operation.

Estate duties range from 1% on estates exceeding $4,800 and not exceeding $14,400 to 30% on estates over $1,920,000. In addition, succession duty ranges from $\frac{1}{2}\%$ between $96 and $2,400 to 2% of values exceeding $9,600 where the successor is a lineal descendant or ancestor 1% to 6% where successor is a brother, sister or their descendants, and from 2% to 10% where the successor is a stranger.
Entertainment tax is charged on certain forms of entertainment.
The other main forms of taxation are the Customs tariffs and excise duty on rum production.

Currency
The currency in circulation is a West Indian currency issued by the Board of the Commissioners

of Currency, British Caribbean Territories (Eastern group) consisting of Barbados, British Guiana, the Leeward Islands, Trinidad and Tobago and the Windward Islands. Currency notes are of denominations $1, $2, $5, $10, $20 and $100. British coin is legal tender but the chief medium of circulation is the new British Caribbean coin in denominations of 50 cents, 25 cents, 10 cents, 5 cents, 2 cents, 1 cent and a $\frac{1}{2}$ cent. The West Indian $ is fixed at 4s. 2d., that is $4.80 to £1 sterling.

The aim of the plan is the promotion of the Island's economic development resulting in increased production and higher national income. Great emphasis is placed on a three-pronged drive for rapid industrial development, agricultural diversification and a bigger tourist trade. In the main, the plan aims at provision of substantial incentives to investment by way of fiscal reliefs, intensification of promotional efforts by the Development Board and the Tourist Board, the creation of a development finance corporation to channel overseas and local capital into manufacturing industry, the expansion of the facilities for technical education and the establishment of a hotel training school, improvement of the basic communication facilities (airport, roads), the continuation of the housing programme and the encouragement of private investment in housing through mortgage insurance and the promotion of building societies, carrying out of research investigation and the exploration of the commercial feasibility of new productive possibilities, completion of the new general hospital and construction of district hospitals, and provision of increased office accommodation.
Education
Education policy aims at the integration of the primary and secondary systems of education and the eventual introduction of compulsory education for children between the ages of 5 and 14 years.

Education at the primary stage is provided in Government primary schools and in preparatory departments of independent schools. Secondary education is provided in senior departments of primary schools, in Government secondary modern
Digitized by Google
BARBADOS
schools, Government secondary grammar schools and some independent schools. No tuition fees are charged in Government primary or secondary schools.

There are 116 Primary Schools, 5 Secondary Modern Schools and 10 Secondary Grammar Schools which are entirely maintained by Government funds. At the close of the school year 1962-63 the number of children in Primary Schools was 40,952 (20,708 boys, 20,244 girls), in Secondary Modern Schools 5,241 (2,629 boys, 2,612 girls), in Secondary Grammar Schools, 4,523 (2,664 boys, 1,859 girls). Erdiston Training College for teachers provided a one year emergency training course for 82 teachers, including 28 from the Windward and Leeward Islands and in addition, from September, 1962 a two-year course which was attended by 35 students, 6 of whom were from Dominica. 86 students were residential during the period under review.

Under the Barbados Government Training Programme, the Commonwealth Scholarship and Fellowship Plan and the Commonwealth Bursary Scheme, Scholarships and Bursaries are awarded annually to teachers in Government Schools, and other qualified applicants for study in Commonwealth countries, and at the University of the West Indies. Codrington College affiliated to the University of Durham, is a Theological College. There were 26 students in residence during 1962-63.

During the academic year there were 56 Government scholars, exhibitioners and bursars attending universities overseas: 16 in the United Kingdom, 2 in Canada, and 38 at the University of the West Indies. In addition 25 students were advanced loans from the Higher Education Loan Fund; 5 were attending universities in the United Kingdom, 8 were at the University of West Indies and 4 at universities in the United States, Canada, India and Germany.

Evening classes in Academic, Commercial and Technical subjects, Handicrafts and Home Economics are conducted at various centres throughout the island, including the Housecraft Centre and the Evening Institute under the Adult Education programme. Technical Education is provided at the Technical Institute established in 1955. It offers courses at three levels, for craftsmen, for technicians and for those desirous of proceeding to advanced technological studies. The Apprenticeship Board is responsible for the organisation of Industrial training and 48 bursaries are awarded annually for training in various trades under apprenticeship schemes with master workmen.
Expenditure on education services in 1962-63 was $5,732,397 as compared with $1,974,725 in 1952-53 and $568,910 in 1942-43.
Libraries and Museums

A free Public Library (84,038 books) is maintained by Government. The Barbados Museum and Historical Society possesses collections of books, pictures, models, specimens of birds and fishes, and a collection of relics of the original Arawak inhabitants of the island.
Health

The Barbados General Hospital has accommodation for 46 paying patients and 407 non-paying patients. In-patient attendances numbered 10,305, out-patient attendances 154,659 in 1962-63. The Government maintains a mental hospital with accommodation for 307 males and 441 females and

a leper hospital at which there are 12 patients. For the training of midwives the Government Maternity Hospital has accommodation for 20 expectant mothers. The new general hospital is still under construction. Each of the 11 parishes has an infirmary for the care of the destitute sick and for the housing of the aged and poor. These infirmaries provide medical care and in some parishes function as district hospitals. A district hospital at Oistins, Christ Church, was opened in December 1960. It provides 20 beds: 10 for maternity and 10 for ante-natal cases.

Preventive medicine is based on the Health Centre System. Three centres are operating in St. Michael, St. Peter and St. Philip. Two of these centres operate out-station clinics in various parts of the Island.
Estimated expenditure on Health Services amounted to $3,277,406 in 1962-63 compared with $206,870 in 1935-36.
Communications

An International Airport is situated at Seawell, 12 miles from Bridgetown. Air France, British Overseas Airways Corporation, British West Indian Airways, Pan American World Airways and Trans-Canada Air Lines operate frequent scheduled services connecting Barbados with the major world air routes.
There are 698 miles of road open to traffic, most of which has a bituminous surface.
Bridgetown is the only port of entry, although some loading of sugar takes place at Speightstown and oil is pumped ashore at Spring Gardens and an Esso installation on the West Coast.

The main shipping companies visiting Barbados are Harrison Line, Elder and Fyffes Line, Royal Netherlands Steamship Company, Compagnie Générale Transatlantique, Saguenay Shipping Ltd., Booth Line, Lamport and Holt Line, Alcoa Steamship Company, M.A.N.Z. Line, Moore McCormack Line, Nourse Line, Caribbean-Hamburg Line (formerly Three Bays Line) and Federal Shipping Service. Occasionally ships of the Linea "C" and Grimaldi Lines visit port.
Broadcasting
A wired system which is now island-wide, is operated by a local company—Barbados Rediffusion Service Limited. It has 21,829 subscribers.
Principal Occupations

Most of the population is engaged in the cultivation of the sugar crop and auxiliary occupations connected with the sugar industry. Approximate figures are: 20,000 persons engaged in agriculture, 14,800 in domestic service, 12,500 in the retail trade and 8,500 in construction work. About 1,700 persons are engaged in the fishing industry.
At the end of 1962 there were nine Trade Unions of which 3 were employers' associations with membership totalling about 340 business concerns and six workers' organizations with about 15,300 members.
Main Crops and Products
Sugar cane is the principal export crop grown on all plantations and on nearly all small holdings. In 1963 sugar production was the equivalent of 190,697 long tons.
Digitized by Google
THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST

Yams, sweet potatoes, eddoes, cassava, maize and pigeon peas comprise the main food crops which are grown in accordance with regulations issued annually. Green vegetables and other crops are cultivated on a minor scale and there is a limited production of tree crops and citrus fruit.

A marketing Corporation has been established by statute for the purpose of improving the facilities for marketing in order to encourage an increase in the production of food crops, meat and dairy produce and fish.
The estimated numbers of livestock are as follows:
Cattle, 12,000; Pigs, 88,000; Sheep, 36,000; Goats, 6,290; and Poultry, 125,000.
A Central Livestock Station has been established to investigate the possibility of developing an economic system of animal husbandry, with special attention to dairying.

A Fisheries Section of the Ministry of Agriculture, Lands and Fisheries was established in 1944 for the investigation of more efficient methods of marine fishing and to encourage their use. Large scale mechanisation of the fishing fleet has been practically accomplished with the assistance of loans from Government. Cold storage facilities are available for meat and for fish and a shrimp industry is being developed. The estimated catch of fish during 1962 was 7,377,969 lb valued at approximately $1,844,092.
Production of natural gas during the year ended 31st March, 1963, was 121.5 million cubic feet. During 1962, 11,042.6 tons of temper, building and manurial limestone were exported.
Manufactures include the production of sugar for local consumption, rum, bread and a variety of biscuits, edible oil, aerated waters, cigarettes, soap, ice and furniture.

BERMUDA
Situation and Area

The "Bermudas" or "Somers Islands" are situated in the Western Atlantic Ocean, about 570 miles east of Cape Hatteras in North Carolina, and until 1940 had an area of 19.34 square miles. As a result of work done by the United States authorities since 1940 to unite and extend some of the islands with materials dredged from the sea, their total area is now 20.59 square miles, of which 2.30 square miles are leased to the Government of the United States for naval and military bases, leaving 18.29 square miles available to the civil population. The United States Bases include a large airfield which is used by both military and commercial traffic.
Population
The estimated civil population on the 31st December 1962 was 45,921, of whom 16,683 were white and 29,238 coloured.
Principal Towns

The City of Hamilton, the capital since 1815, with a population estimated at about 2,800, is situated on the main island. The town of St. George on the island of St. George was formerly the capital of the Colony. Its population is estimated at about 1,800.
Geographical Features

The Bermudas consist of about 300 coralstone islands and islets, roughly in the form of a fish-hook, along the southern rim of the oval plateau summit, about 22 miles from east to west and 14 miles from north to south, of a steep submarine volcanic mountain which is reputed to be between 14,000 and 15,000 feet in height. The ten principal islands are connected by bridges and form a chain about 22 miles long between its north-east and south-west extremities. These islands vary in width but the main island, which is about 14 miles long, has an average width of about 1 mile; it contains about 9,000 acres of land, the highest point being only 259.4 feet above the sea. All the other islands and the areas reclaimed for the United States Bases aggregate about 4,240 acres.
Climate

The climate is generally mild and humid with a mean annual temperature of 70° F. and average annual maximum and minimum temperatures of 90° F. and 47° F. respectively. The coldest and hottest months are February and August with mean temperatures of 62° F. and 80° F. respectively. The average annual rainfall is 58.1 inches. The climate shows very little variation throughout the Colony.
History

According to a 17th century French cartographer the islands were probably discovered in 1503 by a Spanish mariner, Juan Bermudez, but the Spaniards took no steps to form a settlement and the islands were still entirely uninhabited when in 1609 Admiral Sir George Somers' ship The Sea Venture was wrecked upon one of the sunken reefs while conveying a party of colonists to Virginia. The reports of the beauty and fertility of the land induced the Virginia Company to seek an extension of their charter so as to include the islands within their dominion and this extension was granted by King James I, in 1612 and the first emigrants went out in that year. Shortly afterwards the Virginia Company sold the islands for the sum of £2,000 to a new body of adventurers called "The Governor and Company of the City of London for the Plantation of the Somers Islands", and thenceforward for a considerable time the islands bore that name. In 1684 the charter of the Bermuda Company of London was annulled and government passed to the Crown.
Constitution

Representative government was introduced into the Colony in 1620. Since 1684 the Governors have been appointed by the Crown and the laws of the Colony have been enacted by a local legislature. Until 1888 this legislature consisted of the Governor and the House of Assembly and since then of the Governor, the Legislative Council and the House of Assembly.

The Governor is assisted by an Executive Council consisting of three official members (Colonial Secretary, Colonial Treasurer and the Attorney General and six unofficial members (increased from five in November, 1962) appointed by the Crown.
The Legislative Council consists of three official members (The Chief Justice as President, the Colonial Secretary and the Attorney General) and eight nominated members.

The House of Assembly consists of thirty-six members elected for a term of five years. On the 12th January, 1963 a new Bill entitled The Parliamentary Election Act, 1963 , received the assent of His Excellency. Under the provisions of this Act the nine parishes, which have always been represented by four members, have now been divided into two electoral districts, each of which will now be represented by two members. The new Act requires that voters must be British Subjects of twenty-five years of age or over at the time of registration, and if not possessing Bermudian status, they must have been ordinarily resident in the Colony for the whole of the period of three years immediately prior to registration, registration being held every year. Candidates for election must qualify as electors and, in addition, must possess Bermudian status. Electors who own the freehold estate in land in Bermuda of an area of not less than 2,000 square feet will now

qualify for an additional vote. Formerly the franchise had been limited to those persons possessing freehold property of not less than £60 in value and, prior to 1944, only males were permitted to vote or to seek election.

At the General Election held on the 16th May 1963 86.5% of the registered electors voted. The Progressive Labour Party, the first political party to emerge in Bermuda, was successful in getting six of its nine candidates elected.
Members of the Executive and Legislative Councils and of the House of Assembly are paid 24s. for each day's attendance.

A number of Government Departments are controlled by Executive Boards which consist of unofficials appointed by the Governor. The head of the Department acts in an advisory capacity. The chairmen of most of the major Boards are members of the House of Assembly.
Taxation

There is no direct taxation. The revenue of the Colony is mostly raised from Customs duties. The revenue of the corporation of Hamilton is derived from wharfage, shed tax, rents and local taxation assessed at the rate of 16s. on every £100 of real estate and personal property. The corporation of St. George's obtains its revenue in like manner, the rates being 13s. 6d. on each £100. The nine parishes, which administer the general parochial affairs of the poor law, levy rates, and under the Liquor Licence Act licensees pay a certain sum which varies according to the class of licence held.
Public Finance
In 1962 £3,388,743 of revenue was derived from Customs duties.

Currency

The coins in circulation are British currency, which is legal tender. There is no limit to the legal tender of British silver. Bermuda Government £5, £1, 10s. and 5s. currency notes to the value of £1,565,945 10s., were in circulation on 31st December 1962.
Development

Agriculture.—The ever increasing density of the population and the corresponding increase in housing to accommodate it continued to encroach on the land available for agriculture which, in 1962, was a mere 1,062 acres.

The re-afforestation scheme, started in 1949 with the object of replacing the dead Bermuda cedars, continued with some 10,000 trees being felled and some 7,000 trees, shrubs and palms being planted in their place.

The Parks Division, formed in 1956, continued to care for the many small wayside parks bordering public roads, gardens, school playing fields and grounds, Government House Grounds and the Botanical Gardens, in all comprising some 295 acres.
Public Works.—The Government Hardstone Quarry output during 1961–62 amounted to 67,000 tons.
Maintenance was carried out on all public roads and buildings, etc., and late 1961 work was completed on the Casemates Prison.

Construction commenced in 1961 on a new wing for the Mental Hospital which was completed in 1962 and officially opened by His Excellency the Governor and named St. Brendan's Hospital. The wing has accommodation for over 100 patients.
In 1962 work also commenced on the new Hospital and on an extension to the Nurses Home.
Considerable improvements were made to the Civil Air Terminal and an extensive school building programme was pursued.

Libraries, Museums, etc.

The Bermuda Library, with adult, children's and music departments in Hamilton, was greatly enlarged during 1957 and now accommodates the interesting collection of the Bermuda Historical Society. It has a branch in St. George and a distribution service to the schools of the Colony.

The Bermuda Government Aquarium with adjoining live zoological collection, which was also considerably enlarged and much improved, has a museum of local natural history and other exhibits. The Bermuda Historical Society has a second museum in St. George containing items of local historical interest.

Health

The level of health in the Colony continued to be high and Government services are chiefly engaged in preventive measures. The Public Health Department is responsible for the inspection of milk and meat, garbage collection and disposal, rat control, mosquito control, etc. It is also responsible for pre-natal and pre-school health services.

The King Edward VII Memorial Hospital is privately run, but receives an annual Government grant-in-aid, amounting in 1962 to £256,000. The Mental Hospital is attended by Government Medical Officers and Health Officers. On the 1st October, 1960, a Health Insurance Scheme for Government employees and their dependants was inaugurated. The scheme is compulsory for Government employees and optional for their dependants. Contributions are at the rate of £4 per annum for an officer only, £8 per annum for an officer and one dependant and £12 10s. for an officer and two or more dependants. The Government contributes equal amounts into the fund.
During 1962 total government expenditure on Health Services was £383,512 compared with approximately £41,000 in 1937.
Communications
The air terminal is at Kindley Field, St. George's Island.

Five airlines, i.e. B.O.A.C.-Cunard, P.A.A., Eastern, T.C.A. and Iberia, provide Bermuda with scheduled services to Britain, Spain, Canada, the United States, the Bahamas, the Caribbean and West Indies, and South America.

Direct and indirect seaborne passenger and cargo services are maintained with varying frequency and regularity with all parts of the world by Bermuda Shipping Company, Booth-Lampot, Cunard, Furness-Bermuda, Holland-America, Independent Golf, Isbrandsten, MANZ, Pacific and Oriental, Pacific Steamship Navigation, Royal Mail and Saguenay.

The Colony has 132 miles of well surfaced roads with $8\frac{1}{2}$ miles in Hamilton and $4\frac{3}{4}$ miles in St. George's. On the 31st January, 1963, there were 20,576 vehicles licensed in the Colony comprising 7,205 private cars, 595 taxis and buses, 1,144 motor trucks, 11,071 auto and auxiliary cycles and 561 miscellaneous vehicles.
There is no railway in Bermuda.
Broadcasting

There are two broadcasting stations, both commercial, one operated by the Bermuda Broadcasting Company Limited, using the call sign Z.B.M., the other operated by Capital Broadcasting Company Limited, using the call sign Z.F.D.

The first named company was formed in 1943 and started broadcasting in 1946 on 1240 kc/s with a power output of 250 watts. In 1953 a second station, Z.M.B.2 was added to the network and in 1962 power on both stations was increased to 1,000 watts and an F.M. station Z.B.M.-F.M. was inaugurated.
The Company have also operated since January, 1958, a television service known as Z.B.M.-T.V.
The latter named company was formed in 1962 and commenced broadcasting the same year with a power output of 1,000 watts.
Neither radio nor T.V. receiving licences are required.
Principal Occupations

The tourist trade provides employment for a majority of the population. Estimated numbers in some significant occupations are: Hotels/Guest Houses 2,713; commercial 3,305; construction 1,748; agriculture and gardening 670; general labourers (male) 1,365; transport 927; teachers 426.

Marketing and Co-operative Organizations

The only marketing scheme in operation in Bermuda is the "Planned Production and Marketing Scheme" introduced in 1949 to assist the growers of vegetables to find a suitable outlet for their produce. The growers taking part in the scheme contract to grow a specified acreage of certain vegetable crops. The Department of Agriculture agrees to purchase any produce from the acreage specified at guaranteed minimum prices. In addition to the produce grown on contract the Government Wholesale Agricultural Marketing Centre buys other vegetables, citrus fruit and bananas. This scheme has undoubtedly assisted the farming community as more farmers are applying to join. During 1962 purchases of agricultural produce at the Wholesale Marketing Centre amounted to £50,691. Receipts from the resale of this produce were nearly £51,769.

BRITISH ANTARCTIC TERRITORY
Area

The British Antarctic Territory was created on the 3rd March 1962 and lies bonded between 20 and 80 degrees of west longitude and south of the 60th parallel of latitude. The territory consists of the island groups of the South Shetlands and South Orkneys, all Islands adjacent to the mainland of the Graham Land Peninsula, the Graham Land Peninsula itself and all land masses within the bonded area to the South Pole.
Population

The British population of the Territory consists solely of male scientific officers and their assistants, the total number of these during the winter of 1963 was 85. This figure can be expected to double during the summer months on arrival of relief personnel and summer field workers. No exact numbers are known of other nationalities working in the Territory.
Principal Towns
There are no towns in the Territory. Scientific and Field Work bases are situated on various islands and parts of the mainland.
Geographical Features

The majority of the islands in the Territory are of a wild and rugged feature with many glaciers. The Graham Land Peninsula is mainly very mountainous, with plateau features of heights from 4,000 to 7,000 feet, running the entire length of the Peninsula. The main continental masses are covered by ice caps and fringed by floating ice shelves.
Climate
The Territory has a rigorous climate of Antarctic nature.
History
The main island group of the South Shetlands were discovered and taken possession of by Captain W. Smith in 1819, whilst the South Orkney Islands owe their discovery to Captain G. Powell in 1821.

The Graham Land Peninsula was discovered in 1820 by Edward Bransfield, R.N., and taken into the possession of Great Britain by John Biscoe in 1832. The penetration of what is now known as the Weddell Sea and the final discovery of the Continental Land Masses to the South and East of Graham Land Peninsula can be attributed to many great explorers—Cook, Weddell, Ross and Bruce to name but a few. Many of the Islands within the Territory were used as temporary bases for the early sealing and whaling expeditions, but at Deception Island of the South Shetlands Island Group a more permanent and more active whaling station was in operation from 1906 to 1931. During this time a magistrate was in permanent residence for the summer months.

BRITISH ANTARCTIC TERRITORY : BRITISH GUIANA

In the 1943–44 season, under the code name of "Operation Tabarin", the first of what are now known as the British Antarctic Survey bases were established at Deception Island and Port Lockroy. Since then the occupation of bases has been continuous and the number so occupied now stands at 8; their locations are as follows:—
SIGNY ISLAND—South Orkneys 60.43° South 45.36° West.
HOPE BAY—Trinity Peninsula, Graham Land 63.24° South 56.59° West. (There is a small field hut at View Point, Duse Bay which is manned periodically by sledging parties in transit from Hope Bay.)
DECEPTION ISLAND—South Shetlands 62.59° South 60.34° West.
GALINDEZ ISLAND } West Coast of Graham ARGENTINE ISLANDS } Land 65.15° South 64.16° West.
ADELAIDE ISLAND—67.15° South 68.30° West. West Coast of Graham Land.
FOSSIL BLUFF—King George VI Sound.
HALLEY BAY—Caird Coast 75.31° South 26.36° West.
STONINGTON—68.11° South 67.00° West.
Other bases have been established, but are at present unoccupied; they were known as—
Base A—Port Lockroy, Wiencke Islands, Palmer Archipelago.
Base C—Cape Geddes, Laurie Island, South Orkneys.
Base G—Admiralty Bay, King George Island, South Shetlands.
Base J—Prospect Point, Graham Coast.
Base N—Anvers Island, Palmer Archipelago.
Base O—Danco Coast, Graham Land.
Base W—Detaille Islets, Loubet Coast, Graham Land.
Base Y—Horeshoe Island, Marguerite Bay.
Constitution
The Territory is administered by the High Commissioner resident in Stanley, Falkland Islands (see page 102).
Land Policy
The policy is guided by the Antarctic Treaty.
Taxation
All British Scientific officers and their assistants are taxed under the Falkland Islands Income Tax Ordinance.
Public Finance
For the years 1960–61 (see under Falkland Islands ). Until the year ended 30th June 1962 British Antarctic Territory finances were embodied in the Dependencies.
Dependencies
Revenue Expenditure
(a) Includes H.M.G. grants.

In 1962–63, the revised estimate of revenue from the Dependencies totalled £109,864. In addition, the sum of £540,694 was expected to be received from Her Majesty's Government as a contribution towards the cost of running the British Antarctic Survey (ex Falkland Islands Dependencies Survey) Antarctic Bases and Ships.
Currency
Currency (when used) is British coinage and Falkland Islands £5 notes, £1 notes and 10s. notes which are interchangeable with sterling on par.
Communications

Communications between the Territory, the Colony of the Falkland Islands and the United Kingdom are maintained by the British Antarctic Survey's Royal Research Ships the 'John Biscoe' and the 'Shackleton' during the months of November to April/May only.

Between these months the Antarctic Bases are relieved, resupplied and restaffed. Radio communication for the passing of scientific and administrative data is linked between Antarctic Bases and the British Antarctic Survey in Stanley, Falkland Islands.

BRITISH GUIANA
Area
British Guiana lies on the north-east coast of the South American Continent. Its area is 83,000 square miles, divided into three counties as follows :

Principal Towns

The City of Georgetown, Demerara, is the capital and with its environs has a population of approximately 162,000. Population in the city proper numbers about 78,000. New Amsterdam, Berbice, has a population of 14,300 approximately.
Geographical Features

The Colony may be divided into three zones, viz., the low-lying coastland, the mountain region and the intermediate region. The coastland comprises a low-lying, swampy strip of marine alluvium which varies in width from about ten miles in the west to about 40 miles in the east. Its flat, grassy savannahs would mostly be inundated during the rainy season but for artificial drainage and sea defences. Almost the whole of the population and agricultural activity of the Colony is concentrated between the Pomeroon River and Corentyne River along this strip. The mountain region, known as the Pakaraima Range, lies along the western boundary of the Colony.
