Geographical Features

The islands comprising the Colony straddle the $180^\circ$ meridian and lie about 1,200 miles south of the equator. Most of them are of volcanic origin but some are of limestone formation. There are no active volcanoes though hot springs occur in isolated places. All the larger islands are surrounded by coral reefs. Mountain ranges of moderate height—the highest peak is 4,341 feet above sea level—lie athwart the prevailing easterly trade winds and there is consequently a marked difference in the heavy forest vegetation on the windward and the light reeds and grasses on the leeward slopes of most islands. The country is well watered and rivers are numerous. The total forest area is approximately 3,486 square miles.
Climate

The climate is oceanic and tempered by cool east or south-east breezes which blow between May and October. During the rest of the year winds are variable and tropical storms, known locally as hurricanes, may occur. High humidity, which sometimes reaches saturation point, causes unpleasant living conditions while it lasts. Rainfall varies from an annual average of about 120 inches in Suva to 75 inches on the leeward coast of Viti Levu. The diurnal range of temperature at Suva, a few feet above sea level, is about $11^\circ$ , but recordings between $60^\circ$ and $90^\circ$ are not unusual.
History

The first navigator to discover islands in the Fiji archipelago was Tasman in 1643. Cook charted a small island in 1774 and Wilson discovered others in 1797. Bligh of the Bounty recorded most of the Group in 1789 during his memorable voyage in an open boat after being cast adrift by the mutineers.
Christianity was introduced into Fiji by Wesleyan missionaries from Tonga in 1835.

Cakobau (or Thakombau), an influential Fijian chief, offered to cede the sovereignty of the islands to Great Britain in 1858 in exchange for support against his rivals and help in meeting his debts, but the offer was refused. After a further unsettled period, Cakobau renewed his offer of cession. The Deed of Cession was signed at Levuka on the 10th October 1874. In 1882 the seat of government was moved to Suva because of its better harbour and greater facilities for expansion.

Rotuma was discovered by Captain Edwards in H.M.S. Pandora in 1791 during his search for the Bounty mutineers. In the nineteenth century the principal Rotuman chiefs repeatedly offered to cede the island to Great Britain, in order to prevent the continuance of a succession of religious wars. Rotuma was formally annexed in 1881.
Constitution

The Government of the Colony is provided for under the Fiji (Constitution) Order in Council, 1963 and Royal Instructions. The Executive Council, presided over by the Governor, consists of the Colonial Secretary, the Attorney-General, the Financial Secretary, as ex officio members and six nominated members, including a European, a Fijian and an Indian from the unofficial members of the Legislative Council. The Legislative Council is presided over by the Speaker (first appointed in 1956) and consists of nineteen official members and eighteen unofficial members, of whom six are Europeans (four constituency members and two nominated), six Indians (four constituency members and two nominated) and six Fijians (four constituency members and two elected by secret ballot by the Council of Chiefs, itself consisting of elected and nominated members).

A local government system, first established in 1876, and since 1945 known as the Fijian Administration, has jurisdiction over all Fijians in the Colony, a system based on indigenous institutions. At the apex is the Council of Chiefs, presided over by the Secretary for Fijian Affairs. In executive control is the Fijian Affairs Board, consisting of the Secretary for Fijian Affairs as chairman, the Fijian members of Legislative Council, a Legal Adviser, and a Financial Adviser. The Secretary for Fijian Affairs is the executive officer of the Board and he is assisted, in each of the three administrative divisions into which the Colony is divided, by a Commissioner, who is in charge of the division and responsible to the Governor, through the Colonial Secretary, for the efficient administration of his division, including Fijian affairs. District Officers act as inspectors of the local government units but do not intervene in their management.

In Rotuma, where language and customs differ from those in Fiji, the District Officer is the chief executive authority and he also has limited magisterial powers. A Rotuman Council, consisting of the District Officer and a number of Rotumans, makes regulations of local application. Such regulations shall not have effect unless and until they have been approved by resolution of the Legislative Council.
Land Policy

Soon after the date of the cession a commission investigated the title to lands claimed by foreigners and as a result freehold titles to some 400,000 acres of Fijian land were granted. The Fijians now own 84% of the land, the unit of ownership being the mataqali or extended family. Surveys of mataqali holdings have been made following determination of boundaries by Native Land Commissions and a complete record of these family units is maintained. Under the Native Land Trust Ordinance of 1940 the control of all Fijian-owned land is in the hands of an independent Board administered on behalf of, and for the benefit of, the Fijian owners. The Board has power to set aside certain areas of land as reserves for the exclusive use of the Fijian owners and to lease land outside such reserves without reference to the owners. The sale of Fijian-owned land is prohibited except to the Crown and then only where it is required for a public purpose. The sale of Crown Land is prohibited except in very special circumstances. Subdivision of land in specified areas is subject to control by a statutory board and development in and around the ten principal urban centres is regulated by Local Authorities in conjunction with a Central Town Planning Board under a Town Planning Ordinance.

Taxation

Customs duty and port and customs service tax are levied on most imports. Income tax rates range from 1s. 3d. to 6s. 3d. on each £ of taxable income. Provision is made for personal, married, dependent children and widows' allowances. Companies other than non-Fijian shipping companies are liable for tax at the rate of 6s. 3d. in the £ on profits. Non-Fijian shipping companies are taxed at the rate of 4½d. in the £.
Surtax is chargeable on individual incomes at varying rates. Death and gift duties are also payable.

Fiji currency notes are issued by the Fiji Currency Commissioners in denominations of £20, £10, £5, £1, 10s. and 5s.; silver coins in denominations of 2s., 1s., and 6d.; and smaller denominations of 3d., 1d. and ½d. The Fiji £ is linked to sterling at the rate of £F.111 — £100.

Other Development

During 1951 at the request of various Fijian Provincial Councils, a Fijian Development Fund was established into which is paid an amount not exceeding £10 a ton from the proceeds of the sale of Fijian-produced copra. Such deposits are credited to the producing group or individual and bear interest at 2%. The money thus accumulated may be expended only on works or schemes which will benefit the Fijians. The fund is administered by the Fijian Development Fund Board and is being used to provide funds for housing, water supplies, schools, domestic appliances, etc.
Education
At the end of the school year 1962 there were 87,144 pupils enrolled in schools, 6,780 of them in post-primary institutions. Both primary and

post-primary education was carried on by missions, private bodies and Government. The roll of the Government Training College for Teachers was 226; of the Roman Catholic Training College, 17, and that of the Teacher Training Department of Fulton Missionary College, 29.
There were 573 registered schools where staffing and accommodation were satisfactory and 71 "recognised" schools of a lower standard.
In 1962 there were 2,780 teachers of whom 2,263 were registered. 2,073 of these registered teachers were in Government service.
The number of students studying abroad under scholarship and bursary schemes was 143 :

During 1962 estimated gross expenditure on education from general revenue amounted to £1,263,144 compared with £43,306 in 1935. When local contributions by school authorities and expenditure from Colonial Development and Welfare funds are taken into account, total expenditure on education during 1962 is estimated to have exceeded £1,700,000.
Libraries and Museums

The Fiji Museum at Suva contains a fine historical and ethnological collection relating to Fiji and Western Pacific Territories. The Carnegie Library and British Council Library at Suva and the Shri Ramkrishna Library at Nandi are the main lending and reference libraries in the Colony. The Fiji Society is concerned with subjects of historic, scientific and antiquarian interest to Fiji.
Health

The principal infectious diseases in Fiji are : influenza, pertussis, infantile diarrhoea and pulmonary tuberculosis. Malaria does not occur. The total bed capacity of hospitals in the Colony is 2,232, of which 2,189 are in Government institutions; there are also 45 dispensaries in the rural areas in charge of Assistant Medical Officers. Admissions to Government Hospitals in 1962 numbered about 27,399 and the number of outpatients, including those treated at dispensaries, was 697,412. Leprosy is treated at the Fiji Leprosy Hospital, Makogai, with the most modern methods.

The Fiji Medical School (formerly the Central Medical School), founded in 1888, provides a five-year course in medicine and a three-year course in dentistry, together with shorter ancillary courses. In 1962, 168 students from 16 territories enrolled in these courses.

The actual expenditure from general revenue on medical services in 1962, inclusive of expenditure on Pacific Medical services, such as the Medical School and the Fiji Leprosy Hospital, Makogai, was £1,024,759 compared with £78,052 in 1935.

FIJI
Communications

The Colony's main airport is the international airport at Nandi, 135 miles by road from Suva. Aircraft on the trans-Pacific route from Canada and the United States to Australia and New Zealand call at Nandi on both northbound and southbound flights. Services on these routes are operated by Canadian Pacific Airlines, Pan American World Airways and Qantas. Qantas operate a seven times weekly service in each direction. During 1962 Overseas aircraft arriving in Fiji numbered 1,868. Tasman Empire Airways with Electra aircraft operated a six times weekly service between Nandi and New Zealand.
The Compagnie de Transport Aeriens Intercontinentaux operate a twice weekly service from Paris to New Caledonia and Tahiti via Nandi Airport.

Services within Fiji and to neighbouring territories are operated by Fiji Airways with a fleet of three D.H. Heron and one D.H. Drover aircraft based at Nausori (Suva) Airport. There are scheduled services to Nandi and the islands of Vanua Levu and Taveuni; and to Tonga, New Hebrides, the British Solomon Islands and Western Samoa.

There are 440 miles of permanent line on the light railway operated by the Colonial Sugar Refining Company for bringing sugar cane to the crushing mills. The average annual tonnage transported is about 980,000. A free passenger service is run twice weekly on a section of track 120 miles long. The Colony is also served by a widespread and efficient Bus Transport System.
There are some 1,260 miles of road, 1,003 miles of which have an all-weather surface.

The chief ports are Suva and Lautoka. Overseas vessels calling at Fiji ports during 1962 numbered 427, the majority being cargo vessels some of which also carry passengers. A monthly passenger and cargo service is maintained by the Union Steam Ship Company and connects Suva with Auckland, Tonga and Western Samoa. There is a monthly cargo service from the United Kingdom to New Zealand calling at Suva. Regular inter-island cargo, passenger and mail services are maintained by a small number of motor vessels and there are numerous craft up to about 120 tons operating on irregular itineraries to and from the main centres.

At the end of 1962, there were 137 post offices and agencies, 19 telephone exchanges, and 70 telegraph offices operated by the Posts and Telegraphs Department. Telephone and telegraph facilities are available between the principal towns on the main islands of Viti Levu, and Levuka on the island of Ovalau, with 8,636 telephone subscribers.

Radio-phone and telegraph services are maintained to 71 fixed shore stations, mainly on individual islands throughout the group, and Suva Post Office radio (VRO) operates a 24-hour watch for both fixed shore and ship stations. External telegraph and telephone communications are provided and operated by Cable and Wireless Ltd. to all important overseas countries.
Broadcasting

A broadcasting service is undertaken by the Fiji Broadcasting Commission from Stations VRH, VRH2, VRH4, VRH5, VRH6 and VRH7 at Suva operating on frequencies of 930, 840, 4,756, 4,785, 3,980 and 3,935 kilocycles with powers of 2,000, 400, 10,000, 10,000, 250, and 250 watts respectively.
Station VRH3 broadcasts from Lautoka 890 kilocycles on 2½ kilowatts. There were 26,443 licensed radio receivers in 1962.
Principal Occupations

The economy of the Colony is based on agriculture and to a smaller extent on mining. The cultivation of sugar cane is almost entirely in the hands of Indian peasant farmers. Fijians produce about three-fifths of the second important export crop, copra, and about 70% of the employees at the mines are Fijians. The bulk of the population is not, however, a wage-earning one.
At the end of 1962 there were 57 registered Industrial Associations.
Main Crops and Products

Mining is the only substantial non-agricultural industry and the Colony is otherwise wholly dependent upon primary production plus associated industries for coconut oil extraction and processing, cigarette manufacture and butter manufacture. Export income is secured from the sugar industry, from gold and manganese mining and from copra and bananas. Almost the whole of the gold and silver output is produced at Vatukoula, where 1,657 persons were employed in 1962. Production was 87,354 ozs. gold and 38,985 ozs. silver. There were 14 manganese mines in production during that year, the total output being 3,457 tons. Copper production totalled 128 tons and iron ore 6,229 tons.

Production for local consumption only, which normally closely approaches local needs, includes rice, root crops, such as taro, yams and cassava; fish and livestock products including whole milk and butter, beef, pork, goat flesh and eggs, and miscellaneous fruits and pulses.
The livestock population is estimated at: horses 16,164; cattle 112,986; pigs 22,000; goats 24,000; poultry 170,000.

Timber plantation formation continues on a limited scale with some 1,560 acres planted during 1962, mainly with pines and mahogany. Considerable experimental work is being carried out in the satisfactory establishment of plantations. Four large long-term concessions are operating over some 120,000 acres. 48 sawmills were in operation during the year and timber production during 1962 was 12.3 million super feet, compared with the previous record production figure of 10.6 million in 1961. A steady changeover to other sources of fuel continues, particularly in the larger centres.
Reef and coastal fishing is undertaken in a small way by Fijians and Indians but the supply of local fish does not nearly meet the demand.

A geological survey is being undertaken to determine in detail the areas of possible economic mineralization. Upon completion a geological map of the Colony will be issued. The cost of the survey is being met from Colonial Development and Welfare and local funds.

Marketing and Co-operative Organizations

At the close of 1962 there were 111 registered co-operative societies (1 Chinese, 1 Ellice Islanders, 1 European, 54 Fijian, 6 Gilbertese, 25 Indian, 11 Multi-racial and 12 Rotuman). 65 probationary co-operative societies (1 Ellice Islanders, 60 Fijian, 1 Multi-racial and 3 Rotuman) were also operating under the guidance and supervision of the Co-operative Societies Department. By type the registered societies comprised 4 agricultural machinery, 39 consumers', 1 marketing, 41 consumer-marketing, 11 credit societies, 3 grazing societies, 7 landholding societies, 1 agricultural colonization society, 1 better-living society, 2 multi-purpose unions and 1 landholding union. Probationary societies by type comprised 14 consumers' and 51 consumer-marketing. The total membership of the registered societies was 5,825 persons and 49 societies and their total subscribed capital was £94,478. The total membership of the probationary societies was 2,096 persons and their total subscribed capital was £28,713. The principal crop handled was copra and the greater part of it was sold to local crushing mills. The total turn-over of registered and probationary societies in copra, other produce marketed and goods sold in stores was £671,897. Sugar cane which is not handled by co-operative societies, is sold to the South Pacific Sugar Mills Ltd. The export of bananas is controlled by the Banana Marketing Board which issues licences to various shippers including the former licensed buyers, individual growers, co-operative societies and Fijian village organisations.

GAMBIA
Area
The total area, including the water area of River Gambia and its creeks and the adjoining marshes is slightly over 4,000 square miles.
Population

At the census of population taken in April 1963 the total population was 315,600, of which 27,800 in Bathurst town, 12,400 in Kombo St. Mary (the suburban area of Bathurst) and 275,400 in the rural areas. Apart from some hundreds of Europeans, Lebanese and Mauretanians residing in or near Bathurst, the population is African, mainly of the following tribes:—Mandingoes, Fula, Woloff, Serahuli and Jola. Figures of the tribal distribution are not yet available.
Principal towns

The only town is Bathurst (27,800) which is the seat of Government, the commercial centre and the chief port. Other centres, none of which exceeds 5,000 inhabitants, are the four divisional headquarters shown below and the river ports of Kaur and Kuntaur.

Division Population Headquarters
Western .. 55,400 Brikama
Lower River .. 97,200 Mansakonko
MacCarthy Island 64,800 Georgetown
Upper River .. 58,000 Basse
Geographical Features

The River Gambia, some 700 miles in length, flows into the Atlantic by a wide estuary which narrows to two and a half miles between Barra Point and Bathurst. There are 27 feet of water at the entrance of the river at low tide, and ocean-going steamers drawing not more than 17 feet can at present proceed without trouble to Kuntaur 150 miles up-river. There is no bar to the River Gambia. The country consists of the Island of St. Mary (Bathurst) at the mouth of the River, the adjoining districts of Kombo, and strips of territory on each bank of the River, about seven miles wide for most of their length.
Climate

Gambia has a long dry season, normally from November to May, which is pleasant with low temperature and humidity due to the effect of the harmattan. Rainfall is confined to the period from June to October and averages about 40 inches on the coast, decreasing inland. The dry season climate near the coast is the best in West Africa. Up-river it is much hotter than on the coast especially during the period February to May, after which the heat gradually decreases with the onset of the rains. The annual mean temperature is about 80°F at Bathurst and 82°F at Georgetown. Rainfall at Bathurst in 1963 to end October was 37½ inches.
History

The banks of the River Gambia have been inhabited for many centuries. The first known written report is a reference in the account of Hanno, the Carthaginian, of his voyage down the West African coast in B.C. 470, and there are stone circles in the neighbourhood of Kuntaur which may date from the same century.

During the 5th to 8th centuries A.D. most of the Sene-Gambian area was part of the empire of Ghana, whose rulers were of the Serahuli tribe, still strongly represented in Gambia. Ghana was superseded by the kingdom of the Songhais, who were also of the Serahuli tribe.

About the 13th century Mandingoes established themselves in what is now Mali and assumed overlordship of the whole Gambia basin. The Mali rulers' names Keita and Sonko are still prominent names among Gambian Mandingoes.
In the latter half of the 18th century the area was penetrated by Fula invaders, part of the movement which went on to found the Emirates of Northern Nigeria.
European exploration had begun in 1455 when the Gambia estuary was discovered by a Portuguese expedition commissioned by Prince Henry the Navigator. The Portuguese made no lasting settlement.

Between 1588 and 1752 Royal Charters were granted to various English merchants to trade with it. French merchants also plied during that period but the right of trade was abandoned to England by France under the treaty of Versailles in 1783. From 1807 to 1816 the general commerce between Great Britain and the Gambia fell off, in part due to the abolition of the slave trade. In 1816 a new settlement was founded on the
Digitized by Google
THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST
Island of Banjul by English merchants from Senegal. Banjul was renamed St. Mary's and its town named after Lord Bathurst the then Secretary of State.
The settlement was subject to the Government of Sierra Leone and was annexed to that Colony by Act of Parliament in 1821.

In December 1829, the Secretary of State appointed the first Lieutenant Governor "of the settlement of Bathurst and its dependencies in the Gambia". The laws of Sierra Leone remained in force but the Lieutenant Governor was not accountable to the Officer Administering the Government there.
In 1843 the Gambia was created an independent Colony with a Governor and Executive and Legislative Councils for the administration of its affairs.
By the charter of 1866 it became a portion of the West African Settlements and continued to be so under the charter of 1874 until it was made a separate Government in 1888 with its own Legislature.

MacCarthy Island was purchased in 1823; the Ceded Mile was granted by the King of Barra in 1826; and Kombo St. Mary was granted by the King of Kombo in the various cessions of 1840, 1850 and 1853. In 1897 the countries of Foni and Jarra, on the south bank of the river, came under British protection. The rest of Kombo was also annexed by the British Government and is now divided into North, South and Central Kombo.

By 1902 the boundaries of Gambia had been delimited under agreement with the French and the whole country had been pacified. Bathurst and its environs, with some small outlying areas, remained "the Colony"; and the remainder of the country was administered in four Divisions, embracing thirty-five traditional chiefdoms. Latterly six Area Councils have been created as the units of local government in the rural areas.
Constitution
Under the Gambia (Constitution) (Amendment) Order in Council, 1963, Gambia attained full internal self-government on 4th October 1963.

Executive authority is exercised on behalf of the Queen by the Governor, but in the exercise of his functions the Governor is normally required to obtain and act in accordance with the advice of the Cabinet or of a Minister acting under the general authority of the Cabinet. The Governor retains "special responsibility" for external affairs, internal security and defence, but under the provisions of the Order-in-Council current responsibilities under the first two of these headings have been delegated to the Prime Minister.
The Governor is assisted by the Deputy Governor, who performs the functions of Governor during his absence or incapacity.

The Cabinet consists of the Prime Minister and a number of other Ministers, at present eight, the Prime Minister is the elected member of the House of Representatives who leads the party which commands the support of the majority of members of the House. Ministers are appointed in accordance with the advice of the Prime Minister.

The House of Representatives consists of a Speaker, elected by the House; the Attorney-General, who is a public officer, ex officio ; thirty two Elected members, seven from the Colony and twenty-five from the Protectorate; four Elected

Head Chiefs elected by the head chiefs; and two Nominated members appointed by the Governor after consultation with the Prime Minister. The Attorney-General and the nominated members have no vote. A Deputy Speaker is elected by the House from among its own members. The Governor may attend and address the House at any time.
The Governor retains certain legislative reserved powers in respect only of his "special responsibilities".
Land Policy

Individual ownership of land is recognized in Bathurst and there is no restriction on the acquisition of land by non-natives. Freehold interests in Crown lands are not disposed of except in special circumstances. A grant of Crown lands for a term of years may be made by the Governor. The normal term is 21 years with an option of renewal for a like term.

Land in the Protectorate is held on native customary tenure from the District Authority for the district in which it is vested. In general, land may not be alienated or leased to non-natives without the consent of the Governor. Except in a few cases, non-native occupation of land is confined to leases for not more than 21 years. Leases of small plots suitable for commercial or industrial purposes may be obtained from the District Authority for the district, with the approval of the Governor.
Taxation
The main source of tax revenue is the customs tariff, which is at present supplemented by a "purchase tax" of 4%.
Company income tax is at the rates of 45% (9/- in the £).
Personal income tax is levied either on total income or on "chargeable" income under the following schedules, tax being paid on whichever schedule yields the higher tax:—

Local rates are payable to the Bathurst Town Council and to Area Councils in the rural areas.
There is a direct tax on inhabitants of the Protectorate known as the District Rate which accrues to the Area Treasuries. The rate at present varies from 40s. in the more rural villages

to 55s. in the larger towns. It is based on the "yard" of not more than four houses with thatched roofs. Houses roofed with corrugated iron carry a higher tax in most areas. There are additional rates for lodgers in the "yard" and "strange farmers".

Currency

The currency in circulation is issued by the West African Currency Board in London. It consists of notes of £5, £1 and 10s.; yellow metal coins of 2s., 1s., and 6d.; cupro-nickel coins of 3d.; bronze coins of 1d. and £d. The currency is interchangeable with sterling at par.
Development Plans
Allocations totalling £3½ million have been made available from time to time under the Colonial Development and Welfare Acts 1945 to 1959.
The major works carried out under these Acts include:

(i) The Royal Victoria Hospital, Bathurst; (ii) Bathurst town drainage and land reclamation schemes; (iii) Bathurst new water supply; (iv) Bitumenised roads in Bathurst; (v) New High School, Bathurst; (vi) Armitage School, Georgetown; (vii) New primary and post-primary schools; (viii) New health centres and rural dispensaries; (ix) New veterinary headquarters; (x) New staff quarters; (xi) Oyster Creek Bridge, length 720 ft.; (xii) Ferry terminals at Bathurst and Barra; (xiii) First section of the Southern Trunk Road.

Other schemes include research into pest infestation and agronomic problems; agricultural, forestry and veterinary improvements; the rice farm at Sapu; sociological and medical research and reconstruction of roads up country.
A V.H.F. Radio Telephone and Telegraph System was installed between 1961 and 1963. The runway of Yundum airport was completely reconstructed in 1963 under a free gift from the U.K.
A programme of further development schemes was prepared in 1963, and is to be published during 1964.
Education

Education policy provides for a six-year basic primary course, after which about half the pupils are selected by examination either for a Secondary Grammar course leading to School Certificate or a Secondary Modern course of four years.

Primary education. —There were about 9,800 pupils attending primary schools in Gambia in 1962/63. In Bathurst there are eleven schools run by Government. Eight are former Mission schools, taken over by Government under a Local Agreement of 1945, and are administered by Management Committees on which the former religious authority is represented. There are also a Catholic school with senior primary classes and two preparatory and Kindergarten schools, one Catholic and one Methodist.
In the suburban areas there are two Government and one Catholic primary schools.
In the rural areas there were fifty-five schools in the 1963/64 year, of which 16 are Mission schools. The remainder, formerly owned by District Authorities, are now Government institutions.

Secondary Modern education is provided at Crab Island School in Bathurst and at Latrikunda in the suburban area. Enrolment is 1670. In 1964 it is expected that a rural post-primary school with agricultural bias will be opened.

Secondary Grammar education is concentrated in Bathurst, where there are three schools. The Gambia High School, opened in 1959, is a mixed school which absorbed the former Methodist High Schools and the Bathurst School of Science. It is an independent non-denominational school administered by its own Board of Governors. Education is given up to the Advanced Level of the General Certificate of Education. The Catholic Boys' and Girls' Secondary Schools continue to operate separately. Total enrolment in 1962/63 was about 750, and about 60 pupils presented themselves for the West African School Certificate. Armitage School at Georgetown provides post-primary boarding education for boys and girls. It is to be raised to full secondary grammar standard in 1964/65 with some 300 pupils.

Vocational Education is provided in the Technical Trade School at Bathurst, training carpenters and metal workers, and in the Government Clerical classes, which it is planned to expand into a commercial school.
Yundum Teacher's Training College provides a three-year course for 150 students, preparing for posts as teachers in Government and Mission schools.

THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST

Scholarships. —There are 41 Gambians holding Government scholarships in the United Kingdom or in West Africa, and about 100 private students following part-time or whole-time courses in the United Kingdom.
Expenditure. —Government recurrent expenditure on education in the 1963 estimates was £298,000, representing about 11% of total recurrent expenditure.
Health

The most important diseases are malaria, leprosy, tuberculosis, trypanosomiasis, ankylostomiasis, ascariasis, filariasis, trachoma, yaws, tetanus, measles, whooping cough, anaemias. The Health Service includes mosquito control units at Bathurst, Cape St. Mary, and Yundum Airport. Health Inspectors are posted throughout the country.

Medical facilities are based upon two hospitals, at Bathurst and at Bansang, in MacCarthy Island Division. The Royal Victoria Hospital at Bathurst is a well-equipped general hospital, opened in 1953 and having 175 beds. Also in the Bathurst area are a small Sanatorium, a mental hospital, and a Home for the Infirm, also used for long stay cases. Bansang Hospital is a rural hospital of 75 beds, and nearby is a small leprosy village settlement.
There are eight Health Centres and 41 dispensaries and sub-dispensaries scattered throughout the country.
Maternity and Child Welfare Clinics are held in Bathurst and at twenty places outside Bathurst.
A dentist is on the staff of the Royal Victoria Hospital at Bathurst.

A nutrition project is being assisted by W.H.O. and UNICEF. It consists of the issue through the M.C.W. clinics of supplementary rations of skim milk and vitamin capsules (vitamins A and D) to pre-school children, pregnant women and nursing mothers.

A Leprosy Control Project is being implemented by the Government with the assistance of W.H.O. and UNICEF. Treatment is on an out-patient basis and is available at 50 clinics throughout the country. Mobile treatment circuits were introduced in 1961. At the end of the year over 4,000 patients were under treatment.
The estimated recurrent Government expenditure on the Medical and Health Services in 1963 is £258,000, representing about 10% of total recurrent expenditure.
Communications

The international aerodrome is at Yundum, 17 miles from Bathurst, a completely new runway was constructed during 1963 from a gift of £150,000 from the United Kingdom. Airport management services are provided on behalf of Government by International Aeradio Limited and Meteorological services are under the Controller of Civil Aviation. Direct scheduled flights to England are provided once weekly by British United Airways, Ghana Airways (twice weekly) and Nigeria Airways (once weekly) run flights to Dakar and down the West African Coast, connecting with services to all parts of the world. Senegal airways planes also call on their Dakar-Ziguinchor service.

There are approximately 730 miles of road in Gambia, of which 270 are to an all weather standard. During 1963 the trunk road linking Bathurst with the Dakar-Ziguinchor "Trans-Gambian" Highway was completed, but beyond the Highway all season communication has not yet been established. Roads in Bathurst and its environs are bitumenised.

The port of Bathurst is served regularly by Elder Dempster and Palm Lines and, as opportunity offers by ships of several Continental and West African lines. In 1962 import cargoes totalling 60,000 tons were handled at Bathurst and export cargoes amounted to 62,000 tons, mainly decorticated groundnuts, plus over 10,000 transit cargoes. Groundnuts are loaded into ocean-going ships direct from the river ports of Kaur and Kuntaur.
Internal river services are maintained by the Government vessel Lady Wright . The second vessel Fulladu went out of commission during the latter part of 1962.
There are no railways.
Broadcasting
A broadcasting transmitter station "Radio Gambia" was inaugurated on 1st May 1962, operating on the 60 metre band. It transmits at present on five days a week from 6 p.m. to 8 p.m.
Principal Occupations
Most of the population is engaged in agriculture. The estimated numbers in wage employment are:

Government .. .. .. .. 2,500
Others .. .. .. .. 6,000
There are two general Trade Unions—the Gambia Labour Union and the Gambia Workers Union.
Main Crops and Products
The main crops are groundnuts for export (75,000 tons of undecorticated nuts were purchased in 1962–63) rice, sorghum and millet; some maize and a little cotton for village weaving are also grown.
The estimated livestock population is: cattle 182,000; sheep 54,000; goats 94,000; pigs 2,000; horses 50; donkeys 2,000.

A fair amount of fish is caught in coastal waters and some in the lower river; very little however is done farther up-river, though fish are fairly plentiful. The value of smoked fish exported to Sierra Leone in 1962 was £35,000.
Trade

In 1938, 45.8% of the total imports came from the United Kingdom, 19.4% from the Commonwealth countries and 34.8% from foreign countries. The corresponding figures in 1962 were 39.7%, 13.2% and 47.1%.

Co-operative and Marketing Organizations

The Gambia Oilseeds Marketing Board handles the export of all groundnuts and other oilseeds. Its aims are to ensure orderly marketing and to improve the quality of exports. The progressive development of Farmers' Marketing Co-operatives continues. The first society was registered in 1956; there are now 78 registered societies which handled 18% of the 1962-63 groundnut crop.

THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST
GIBRALTAR
Geographical Features and Area

Gibraltar is a narrow peninsula running southwards from the south-west coast of Spain, with an area of $2\frac{1}{4}$ square miles. It consists of a long, high mountain called The Rock and a sandy plain to the north of it, raised only a few feet above the level of the sea, called the North Front. Its greatest elevation is 1,396 feet, its length $2\frac{3}{4}$ miles and its greatest breadth nearly 1 mile.
Population
The population is European and at the Census of October 1961 numbered 24,075. At the end of 1962 the estimated population was 24,151.
Climate

The climate is mild and temperate; snow or frost is extremely rare. The mean minimum and maximum temperatures in winter are $45^\circ$ and $65^\circ$ respectively. In summer a warm breeze laden with moisture, known as the "Levanter", strikes the eastern face of the Rock, condenses in the sky above and causes a cloud pall to hang over the city and bay. During this period the climate is humid and relaxing. The mean minimum and maximum temperatures in summer are $55^\circ$ and $85^\circ$ respectively. The rainy season is spread over the period September to May; the average annual rainfall is 35 inches.
History

Gibraltar was possessed successively by the Phoenicians, Carthaginians, Romans and Visigoths, but remained uninhabited till the Mohammedan invasion of Spain. It was held alternately by Moors and Spaniards until 1704 when, during the war of the Spanish succession, it was captured by the British forces under Admiral Sir George Rooke and ceded to Great Britain by the Treaty of Utrecht in 1713. The cession was renewed by the Treaty of Versailles in 1783. Many attempts have been made to take Gibraltar, especially during the great siege in 1779-83, when General Eliott (afterwards Lord Heathfield) defended it against the united forces of Spain and France, but all have been unsuccessful and it has remained in British hands since its capture in 1704.
Constitution

The Gibraltar (Legislative Council) Order in Council, 1950, as amended by further Orders in Council, provides for the establishment of a Legislative Council consisting of the Governor as President, the Speaker, three ex officio members, two nominated members appointed by the Governor and seven elected members. The ex officio members are the Chief Secretary, the Attorney-General and the Financial Secretary. The Order in Council empowers the Governor to make laws for the peace, order and good government of the Colony with the advice and consent of the Council. The Governor possesses the usual reserved power to pass into law any measure necessary or expedient in his opinion in the interests of public order, public faith or good government (including defence).
The Gibraltar Letters Patent and the Royal Instructions provide that the Governor shall be advised in the exercise of his powers by an Executive Council consisting of four ex officio members
and four unofficial members. The ex officio members are the Deputy Fortress Commander, the Chief Secretary, the Attorney-General and the Financial Secretary.

In September 1962, the Secretary of State for the Colonies approved a proposal that much of the day-to-day work of the Executive Council should be referred by the Governor in Council to a Council of Members, on the understanding that it would be in the discretion of the Governor to decide what matters should be referred to the Council of Members and that the recommendations of the Council of Members would be referred back to the full Executive Council.
The Council of Members is composed of the four unofficial members of Executive Council under the Chairmanship of the Chief Member.
Taxation

This is mainly indirect. An Income Tax Ordinance came into force in 1953; the standard rate of tax is 3s. in the £, but rates for individuals range from 6d. to 4s. in the £. There are no excise duties.
The main heads of taxation are :
Customs. Income Tax. Estate Duties. Stamp Duties. Licences. Fuel Oil Tax.

The items subject to import duty are alcoholic beverages, perfumed spirits, motor spirits, tobacco, coffee, mechanical lighters, motor vehicles, motor vehicle spares and accessories, photographic cameras and cinema projectors, radio sets and spares, razor blades, watches and clocks, fountain pens, including ball-point pens, binoculars, telescopes, sound recorders, sound reproducers and recording media.
The Government lottery yielded £143,556 in 1962.
In 1962 Customs accounted for £764,298 and licences, excise duties and internal revenue for £316,255 of the Colony's revenue. In the same year the main heads of expenditure were :

£
Social Services ..967,000
Public Works ..282,000
Justice ..165,000

The Public Debt of the Colony at 31st December 1962 consisted of £1,000,000 of 3% Debenture Loan, 1967-72, £400,000 of 3$\frac{1}{2}$% Debenture Loan, 1970-75, £250,000 of 5% Debenture Loan, 1967-72, £53,050 of 6% Debenture Loan, 1981-86, and £283,050 of 6% Debenture Loan, 1971-82. In addition there was a balance of £87,500 outstanding on an interest-free loan of £250,000 from Colonial Development and Welfare funds.

GIBRALTAR
Currency

Gibraltar Government notes and United Kingdom silver and copper coins are legal tender in the Colony. Notes to the value of £1,397,447 were in circulation at 31st December 1962. The issue is governed by the Currency Note Ordinance and is of the following denominations : £5, £1, 10s.
Development Plans
The new Customs, Police and Immigration buildings at the Frontier with Spain and the 10-acre rain water catchment as well as the new road to Catalan Bay were completed in 1963.

Work on two of the three hotels under construction as well as an extension to another are now well advanced and should be completed by mid-1964. The Air Terminal is being enlarged to deal with the increased tourist traffic. With the assistance of Colonial Development and Welfare funds, progress in the construction of new flats is being maintained. Work on the construction of the Casino is now well in hand and three new Homes for the Aged and the Mackintosh Hall are due to be completed shortly. A concession has been granted to a local firm to set up an Aerial Ropeway to Signal Hill on the top of the Rock where in addition to having an observation terrace it is also proposed to have a restaurant. With the clearance of a site at North Front formerly used for temporary housing a new area is being opened up for industrial purposes.
Education

There are eight secondary and fourteen primary and infants' schools conducted by the Department of Education, with a total of 3,925 pupils at 31st December 1962. In addition there are three unaided schools with an enrolment of 655. Government expenditure on education in 1962 amounted to £175,021 compared with £9,863 in 1935.
Approximately 40 students of both sexes are undergoing courses of higher education in the United Kingdom.
Libraries and Museums

A lending library is maintained by the British Council. At the Gibraltar Garrison Library, established in 1793, an extensive reference section and a well-supplied lending library are available to members. The Museum contains exhibits of historical interest connected with Gibraltar.
Health
Total expenditure on medical and public health services in 1962 was £228,861 compared with £22,966 in 1935. The number of beds available in hospitals was :

St. Bernard's Hospital (General) including King George VI Memorial Wing .. 120
King George V Hospital .. 60
Infectious Diseases Hospital .. 10
St. Joseph's Hospital .. 60
The number of in-patients at the St. Bernard's Hospital during 1962 was 2,506 and out-patient attendances numbered 10,387.
Communications
There is no civil airfield in the Colony, but restricted use of the R.A.F. aerodrome at North Front by civil aircraft is permitted, subject to termination if military considerations so require.
British European Airways operate daily services between Gibraltar and the United Kingdom both direct and via Madrid. Gibraltar Airways operate a daily service (two flights) to and from Tangier.

The length of road open for traffic is 5$\frac{1}{4}$ miles in the city, 4 miles in the south district and about 3$\frac{1}{4}$ miles in the area of North Front and Catalan Bay. All roads are suitable for motor traffic.

The total arrivals of merchant vessels during 1962 were 4,389 with a net tonnage of 10,765,623. The majority called for bunkers, the discharge of cargo and passengers, provisions and repairs. The following passenger services were in operation:

Orient Line, United Kingdom/Australia .. 2 monthly
Union Castle Line, United Kingdom/East Africa .. 2 monthly
P. & O. Line, United Kingdom/Australia .. 2 monthly
Anchor Line, United Kingdom/India .. 2 monthly
British India Line to United Kingdom only .. monthly
Italia Line, Italy/New York.. 2 weekly
National Hellenic America Line, Piraeus/Italy/New York .. 2 monthly
Zim Israel Line, Haifa/Italy/New York .. 3 monthly

Approximately one cargo vessel a week arrived from the United Kingdom. A daily passenger and car ferry service to and from Tangier is operated by the Bland Line. There is also a daily passenger service between Gibraltar and Algeciras.
Broadcasting

A Government Broadcasting Station (Radio Gibraltar) broadcasts in English and Spanish for approximately 12 hours daily, an average of 30 hours weekly being devoted to commercial broadcasting. In addition to live and locally recorded programmes, use is made of B.B.C. transcriptions and relays. The power of the station is $\frac{1}{2}$kW and it operates on a frequency of 1,484 kc/s, 202.2 metres. Its coverage is about 50 miles. There is also a local wired broadcasting service operated by the Military Authorities for military personnel, which relays programmes from the B.B.C. During 1962, 4,604 radio receiving licences were issued.
Television

A licence to establish a commercial television station was granted to Gibraltar Television Co. Ltd., and test transmissions began in 1961. On the 1st October, 1962, the Company started to operate for approximately three hours daily on the standard European system of 625 lines on Channel 6. There were over 2,400 licensed T.V. sets at the end of September, 1963.
Principal Occupations

Almost half of the male wage-earners in Gibraltar, of whom a large proportion are Spaniards entering for work daily, are employed in one or other of the Service Departments, the Colonial Government, or the City Council. Commercial enterprises provide employment for a substantial number of clerical workers in shipping offices and trading agencies. Hotels, catering services, retail distributing trades and stevedoring are the other main sources of private employment.

Tourist Trade
The value of the tourist trade is estimated at about £2,000,000 per annum.

The number of visitors passing through the Colony continues to increase. To meet this increase two luxury hotels are in course of construction. In addition every possible facility is being given. Places of interest such as the Caves, Galleries, Moorish Castle and the Upper Rock may be visited without permits.

GRENADA
Area

The total area of the Colony is 133 square miles. In addition to the island of Grenada it includes certain of the small islands known as the Grenadines, the largest of which, Carriacou, has an area of about 13 square miles.
Population

The population at the census on 7th April 1960 of 88,677 is for the most part coloured. There is a small white population, a number of Indians and a small community of the descendants of early white settlers. The estimated population at the end of 1962 was 91,000.
Principal Town
The capital, St. George's, has a population of 7,305.
Geographical Features

In structure, Grenada is a moderately eroded volcanic pile. In general it consists of a somewhat disordered topography of mountains and fairly deep, steep-sided valleys. A backbone of mountains traverses the whole length, closer to the western than the eastern coastline. The highest points are Mt. St. Catherine (2,749 feet), Fedon's Camp (2,512 feet) and Morne Quaqua (2,412 feet). From the north-east end and along the east and south-east the mountains slope gradually towards the sea. The south coast is deeply indented by numerous natural harbours. The island is well-watered by quick-flowing streams. Approximately 10,000 acres are under forest. A small lake, the Grand Etang (approximately 36 acres), lies at a height of about 1,800 feet in the centre of the Island.
Climate

Rainfall is low in the coastal areas and high in the mountains of the interior, with an average range of 60 to 150 inches per annum. The months January to May are relatively dry, June to December relatively wet, August being the driest month and November the wettest.
History

Grenada was discovered by Columbus in 1498. It was originally inhabited by Caribs. A company of London merchants attempted to form a settlement in 1609 but were so harassed by the Caribs that the attempt was abandoned. Grenada was, however, included in the Carlisle Grant of 1627. In 1650, Du Parquet, Governor of Martinique, purchased the island from the French West India Company to whom it had been granted by the King of France and established a settlement at St. George's. This venture did not pay and Du Parquet sold the island to the Comte De Cerillac from whom it was in turn purchased in 1665 by the French Company and passed to the Crown of France in 1674.

Conquered by the British in 1762, the island was formally ceded to the British Crown by the Treaty of Paris in 1763. In 1779, England being preoccupied with the American War of Independence, Grenada was retaken by the French, but restored to Britain by the Treaty of Versailles in 1783 and re-occupied in 1784. In 1795-96 a rebellion against British rule was instigated by a French Republican from Guadeloupe. The rising was suppressed in 1796 and since then there has been peace in Grenada.
Constitution
The constitutional arrangements are the same as in Antigua (see page 64).
Taxation

Forms of direct taxation include income tax, estate duties and property tax. Income tax is based on a graduated scale, varying from 5 cents to 65 cents in the $ for individuals (on chargeable income for the year immediately preceding the year of assessment). Companies pay at the rate of 40%. There is a land tax of 25 cents per acre; and house tax is levied on a sliding scale varying from $1.20 per house of rental value between
Digitized by Google
THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST
$24 and $28.80 per annum to 8% in the case of houses with rental value over $144 per annum. Other forms of taxation include import, export and excise duties and motor vehicles tax.
At the end of 1962 the Public Debt amounted to $12,615,618 (including Hurricane Rehabilitation Loan from the United Kingdom Government of $7,108,768) and accumulated Sinking Funds to $1,120,640.
Currency
The currency of the Colony is the West Indian dollar which is linked to sterling at the rate of $4.80 to the £.

Notes issued by the Eastern Caribbean Currency Board were introduced in 1951 when the Windward Islands joined with the other British territories in the Eastern Caribbean (Barbados, British Guiana, the Leeward Islands and Trinidad) in a common currency.

Notes issued by the Government of Trinidad and Tobago, Barclays Bank (Dominion, Colonial and Overseas) and the Royal Bank of Canada continue as legal tender but are being withdrawn and the banks' powers of note issue have been terminated. Meanwhile United Kingdom coinage has also largely been replaced by the Board's own dollar coinage, though the diminishing United Kingdom coinage continues as legal tender.
Development Plans

Under the 1945, 1950, 1955 and 1959 Colonial Development and Welfare Acts Grenada received allocations totalling £1,736,121; a further allocation of £150,000 has been made under the Commonwealth Development Act 1963.
Other Development

Progress has been made in the direction of attracting external investment for the development of the tourist industry which the Colony is not in a position to finance. A step forward has also been made in setting out Government's policy regarding Morne Rouge development in the interest of tourism.
Education

There were 56 Primary Schools in the Colony with rolls totalling 24,074 pupils at 31st December, 1962. Average attendance in 1962 was 19,566 or 81.2% of the total enrolment. Compulsory attendance is not at present enforced. Eleven of the primary schools are maintained entirely by Government, the remainder are under denominational control and receive grants-in-aid from public funds. Government is at present pursuing a policy for gradual replacement of dilapidated buildings by Government-owned buildings of an approved standard and model. The actual operation of such schools, however, will remain under denominational control by written agreement.

There are four handicraft centres. In addition five schools have well-equipped handicraft rooms. There are also four domestic science centres, with adequate facilities provided at five other schools. A visual education unit serves the whole colony. Six Secondary schools (one Government and five grant-aided) have a total roll of 1,777 (end 1962). Two of the schools are for boys only, three for girls only and one is co-educational. All secondary schools teach up to the School Certificate and G.C.E. standard and the Grenada Boys' Secondary School, the Presentation Boy's College, the St. Joseph's Convent, St. George's and the Anglican High School also prepare pupils for the Cambridge Higher School Certificate examination. The actual cost to Government of primary and secondary education during 1962 was $1,115,375.83.

The Government awards, among others, two biennial island scholarships one tenable at a University in the British Commonwealth, open to both male and female candidates; and one tenable at the University of the West Indies Faculty of Agriculture.
Libraries, etc.
There is a free public library in St. George's and branch libraries in each parish. In addition there is an island-wide book mobile service.
Health

A WHO Campaign for the eradication of Aedes Aegypti was started in 1953 and completed at the end of March 1962 with great success and with only the island of Carriacou still positive. In March 1961 Dr. Van Seventer, a WHO Entomologist, conducted an Aedes Aegypti survey in Carriacou for the purpose of finding a proper insecticide to complete the Eradication Phase. Since then regular perifocal treatment has been conducted. There has been no reported case of malaria either from this island or the mainland and Grenada has now been declared free of Malaria.

The WHO-UNICEF also assisted with the B.C.G. campaign in 1954, with follow-up in 1955 and 1956 and subsequent incorporation of the programme into the normal activities of the Health Department; also with a scheme for the supplementary feeding of school children, expectant and nursing mothers: the last is still in progress.

In 1956 a treponematosis campaign was undertaken with the object of eradication of yaws and a reduction in the incidence of venereal diseases. Evaluation and surveillance which have been continuing since then have shown that yaws has been eradicated.

The Colony Hospital of 240 beds is at St. George's. The Princess Alice Hospital, St. Andrew's, is a modern building, recently constructed and provides 32 beds. Addition of a Children's Ward has been completed. The district hospital in Carriacou which was completely destroyed by the hurricane of 22nd September 1955 has been rebuilt and was opened on 16th July 1958. It has been named the Princess Royal Hospital. A Children's Ward will soon be constructed.

There are 26 district Medical Visiting Stations throughout the Colony with resident nurse-midwives in charge. Maternity and child welfare work is carried on in all these stations. There are three main Health Centres at St. George's, Gouyave and St. David's, and in four of the Visiting Stations accommodation has been improved to provide subsidiary Health Centres.

GRENADA
Expenditure on medical and sanitary services in 1962 amounted to $1,160,128 compared with $97,987 in 1935.
Communications
Pearls Airport is located at the North Eastern Corner of Grenada (12°09' N.; 61°37' W.).
Runway—Direction 082/262.
Dimension 4,000 feet × 150 feet (Colas). Stopway 1,200 feet × 150 feet (Grass).
Tower Frequency. 118.1 mc/s.
Non-Directional Beacon. "ZGT". 362 kc/s.

British West Indian Airways Ltd. operates daily scheduled services through Pearls Airport, linking Grenada with other West Indian Islands. These schedules vary with the seasons and are adjusted to ensure regular connections at Trinidad and Barbados to other parts of the world.

There are approximately 476 miles of good roads divided into four classes. Class I—188½ miles, Class II—102½ miles, Class III—90½ miles Class IV—94½ miles. About 356 miles have an oiled surface. There are no railways.

The St. George's Harbour at the south-west of the island is Grenada's chief port. Its outer harbour is an open roadstead with 5-10 fathoms, sand bottom, good holding ground. The inner harbour or carenage is a well sheltered natural harbour located between Fort George Point and a point about a quarter of a mile to the south-eastward. Berths for two ocean-going vessels with length of 400-500 feet are available on the eastern side of the inner harbour alongside an 800 ft. long pier with a minimum depth of 30 feet alongside. Berths for small craft are available on the Western side of the carenage and also along 400 feet on the eastern face of the pier.

Entrance to the inner harbour is through a channel with minimum width of 600 feet and minimum depth of 45 feet. The channel and entrance are marked with buoys. Leading marks located on shore lead to the pier. Turning basin in inner harbour is 900 ft. × 600 ft. approximately.

No tugs are available or necessary. Pilotage is not compulsory but advisable. Pilots are available and require prior notice. Transit shed on wharf. Cargo is usually handled by ships' gear but two 6 ton mobile cranes are available. Lighters with total capacity of 350 tons are also available. Good potable water is laid on to the wharf. Bunkers are not available except light diesel oil for small craft.
The other ports, Granville and Hillsborough in Carriacou are mostly used by small craft.

Regular cargo services are provided by Harrison Line Steamers and by vessels chartered by Geest Industries Limited from United Kingdom ports; by the Saguenay Shipping Company from Canadian ports; by the Booth, Lamport and Holt and American Defence Lines from the United States ports and by the Royal Netherlands Steamship Company from European ports. The Federal Shipping Service together with several small motor vessels, auxiliary and sailing vessels take care of the cargo services between the islands and also provide a useful passenger service.
Several foreign ships make frequent calls at this port to take immigrants to the United Kingdom. Passenger travel to the U.S.A. and Canada, however, has to be made through Trinidad or Barbados.
During 1962, 660 motor and steam and 497 sailing and auxiliary motor vessels with a total net tonnage of 884,534 and 17,655 respectively called at Grenada.
Broadcasting

A Broadcasting Service for the Windward Islands, with headquarters in St. George's, Grenada, was inaugurated in 1955. Transmissions take place on short waves from a 5 kW transmitter and cover the Eastern Caribbean.
The capital outlay on these stations, and the running costs for the first few years, have been provided from Colonial Development and Welfare funds.
Principal Occupations
The following is a summary of gainfully-employed labour for 1962:
Agriculture—
(a) Field Workers .. .. .. 4,425
(b) Spice Curing Stations .. .. 131
(c) Sugar Production (factory): In season .. .. .. 104 Out season .. .. .. 40
Government—
P.W.D. Labour Force .. .. 704

There are twelve registered trade unions.
Main Crops and Products

The major export crops on mainland Grenada are cocoa, nutmeg, mace, and banana. Appreciable quantities of raw sugar and copra are produced for local consumption. Limes as lime oil, and cotton are the main crops exported from Carriacou, a dependency.
The approximate livestock population is:
Sheep and goats .. .. 7,000

There is a cigarette factory, a soap and edible fats factory and a sugar factory producing unrefined sugar for local consumption. The sugar factory also operates a continuous rum-distilling plant and together with a number of estates equipped with pot stills, supplies the demand for uncured rum. A lime factory and two estate equipped lime stills deal with the Island's lime crop. There is also a Government-operated cotton ginney in Carriacou. One estate in Grenada manufactures and exports nutmeg oil. In addition there are two large and three small soft drink factories and a brewery.

Marketing and Co-operative Organizations

A Government-operated Central Marketing Organization was initiated in 1951 for the purchase, sale and, where necessary, storage of surplus quantities of locally grown foodcrops. The nutmeg industry is controlled by the Grenada Co-operative Nutmeg Association which purchases, processes and markets all nutmegs and mace produced in the Colony.
In 1955 a statutory body, the Grenada Banana Co-operative Society, was set up to control the production and marketing of bananas.
One farmers' society and eight fishermen's unions were registered under the Friendly Societies Ordinance in 1954.

A Co-operative Department was established in 1957. It now consists of a Registrar of Co-operatives, a Co-operatives Officer, 2 Junior Co-operatives Officers and two clerks. The Department is responsible for promotion work as well as registration, supervision and audit.

THE HIGH COMMISSION TERRITORIES
THE HIGH COMMISSION TERRITORIES (BASUTOLAND, THE BECHUANALAND PROTECTORATE AND SWAZILAND)
Basutoland (a Colony) is administered on behalf of Her Majesty's Government in the United Kingdom by a Resident Commissioner.

The Bechuanaland Protectorate and Swaziland (also a Protectorate) are each administered by an officer holding the title of Her Majesty's Commissioner, responsible directly to the Secretary of State. In all three Territories a general power to give directions is vested in the High Commissioner who since the advent of the South African Republic in May, 1961, has also held the office of Her Majesty's Ambassador to South Africa.
Each of the three Territories is divided into districts in charge of District Commissioners.

BASUTOLAND
Area
Comprises an area of 11,716 square miles of which about one quarter in the west is lowland country varying in height above sea level from
5,000 to 6,000 feet, the remaining three quarters being highlands rising to a height of 11,425 feet in the Drakensberg Range, which forms the eastern boundary with Natal.
Population
In 1956 (the date of the last census) the population figures for Maseru township were: Africans 5,091, Europeans 590, Mixed Race 57 and one Asian.

A census of all races held in April 1956 gave the following figures for the population present in the Territory: Africans 638,857; Europeans 1,926; Persons of Mixed Race 644; and Asians 247. The census also gave the additional figures of 154,782 as an estimate of the number of Africans temporarily absent from the Colony for work and other purposes. The estimated total population of Basutoland in July 1961 was 697,000 with an additional 130,000 absent from the Territory. (By way of comparison, a census of 1875 gave a total of 469 Europeans and 127,707 Africans in Basutoland.)
Principal Towns
The capital is Maseru, which has an estimated population of about 10,000. The other principal towns are: Butha Buthe, Leribe, Mafeteng, Mokhotlong, Qachas Nek, Mohale's Hoek and Quthing.
Geographical Features

The Colony of Basutoland is an enclave within the Republic of South Africa, its boundaries running with those of Natal to the east, the Cape Province to the south and the Orange Free State to the north and west. It lies between latitudes $28^{\circ} 35'$ and $30^{\circ} 40'$ south and longitudes $27^{\circ} 00'$ and $29^{\circ} 30'$ east. The mountain ranges run from north to south and those in the central area, named the Maluti, are spurs of the main Drakensberg which they join in the north, forming a high plateau varying in height from 9,000 to 10,500 feet. In this area two of the largest rivers in the Republic, the Orange and the Tugela, and tributaries of the Caledon, have their source.
Climate

Rainfall is variable and averages approximately 29 inches a year over the greater part of the country. Most of it falls between October and April, but there is normally no month which has less than half an inch. Unfortunately, river discharge statistics show that most of this water is lost to Basutoland in the form of run-off. The deciding factors from an agricultural point of view are whether the rain comes in steady soaking showers at intervals suited to the growth of the staple crops or whether it comes in the form of short and heavy storms, running to waste and eroding the soil. Serious droughts, like the one of 1948-49 are rare and there are a large number of perennial streams, though not so many as existed some years ago. In the lowlands the temperatures vary from a maximum of $90^{\circ}$ or more in summer to a minimum that rarely drops below $20^{\circ}$ in winter: in the highlands the range is much wider and temperatures below zero are common.

History

The Basotho as a nation did not come into prominence until 1818 when Moshesh, a minor chief of the Bakoena tribe in Northern Basutoland, gathered together the remnants of the various clans that had been scattered by the raids of the Zulu and Matebele.

The new nation went through a period of struggle, first with the emigrant Boers of the Great Trek, then with the British. From 1856 until 1868 there was a series of wars with the Orange Free State burghers, Moshoeshoe lost a large portion of his country and repeatedly sought the protection of the British Government.
In 1868 the Basotho became British subjects and the boundaries of the Territory were defined by the Cape of Good Hope Notice of 13th May 1870.

The country remained in a very unsettled condition until it was annexed to the Cape Colony in 1871. The people never took kindly to the Cape Colonial rule and in 1880, after various disturbances, the Governor of the Cape extended to Basutoland the provisions of the Cape Peace Preservation Act of 1878, which included a clause for the general disarmament of the Basotho. They refused to accept the terms, and, after a war lasting nearly a year, the Act was repealed, several chiefs would not submit, and it was decided to hand over the administration to the Imperial Government. This was effected by Order in Council of 2nd February 1884.
Constitution

Before the introduction of constitutional reforms in March 1960 Basutoland was governed under a loose-knit system by which the British Administration combined with an hereditary Chieftainship. The Basotho owe allegiance to a single Paramount Chief under whom there is a hierarchy of Chiefs and Headmen. These Chiefs and Headmen are the authorities with whom the ordinary people deal in their daily affairs, and are responsible for the day to day administration of the Territory.
The Head of the Administration is the Resident Commissioner, who works under the direction of the High Commissioner for Basutoland, the Bechuanaland Protectorate and Swaziland.

In 1910 an advisory body known as the Basutoland Council consisting of the Resident Commissioner as President, the Paramount Chief as Chief Councillor and 99 Basotho nominated members, was constituted by Proclamation. The Council evolved gradually and an elected element, 42 in number, was introduced in 1957.
In 1943 District Councils, consisting in the main of nominated members and a small number of elected members, were established as advisory and consultative bodies.

In September 1955 the Basutoland National Council passed a motion requesting "that the Basutoland Council be given power to make laws in all internal matters, such laws to be confirmed by the Paramount Chief". In May 1956 the Secretary of State invited the Council to submit detailed recommendations for an advance in constitutional status. Agreement on a new constitution was reached at the end of 1958 and this was brought into operation in March 1960.
The new constitution provides for an Executive Council, which is advisory to both the High Commissioner and the Paramount Chief, and a Legislative Council known as the Basutoland National Council.

The Executive Council consists of the Resident Commissioner, who acts as Chairman, the Government Secretary, Finance Secretary, Assistant Attorney-General, three persons chosen by the Basutoland National Council from among their own number and one person nominated by the Paramount Chief.

The Basutoland National Council consists of 80 members, divided equally between elected and non-elected members; the former being elected from among the members of District Councils by the District Councils sitting as electoral colleges for that purpose. The non-elected element is made up of the three official members of the Executive Council (excluding the Chairman), the Commissioner for Local Government, the 22 Principal and Ward Chiefs and 14 nominees of the Paramount Chief. The Council has power to legislate for all persons in Basutoland in respect of all matters, save the following, which are the High Commissioner's matters:

External Affairs and Defence; Internal Security; Currency, Public Loans, Customs and Excise; Copyright, Patents, Trade Marks and Designs; Posts (including Post Office Savings Bank), Telegrams, Telephones, Broadcasting and Television; the recruitment, appointment, conditions of service, promotion, discipline and retirement (including pensions) of officers in the Public Service.
In respect of legislation within the High Commissioner's field, the Basutoland National Council will continue to act as a consultative body and all matters will be referred to it.
National elections under the new constitution were held in January 1960 to elect the 162 members of the nine District Councils. The results were:—

Basutoland Congress Party 72
Independents 52
Basutoland National Party 22
Marema-Tlou 16
Basutoland Progressive Association 0
Taxation
The main forms of direct taxation are Basuto tax, income tax and poll tax (payable by all male adult persons who do not pay Basuto tax).

The principal sources of indirect revenue are customs and excise duties at varying rates. Basutoland is in a customs union with the Republic of South Africa and receives a fixed percentage of the gross revenue collected on imports into the Republic. Licensing exists for many trades and businesses. There are also death duties, stamp duty and a wool and mohair export duty.
