THE HIGH COMMISSION TERRITORIES
As revenue was expected to be substantially below expenditure and the Territory had no central balance the United Kingdom Government approved grant-in-aid of £1,663,000 for 1963–64.
Currency

The currency is the same as in the Republic of South Africa. During February 1961 the Territory followed the South African change from Sterling to a decimalized system of Rands and Cents (£1 Sterling = R.2; 1 shilling = 10 cents).
Development Plans

Under the Colonial Development and Welfare Act 1959 Basutoland received an allocation of £1.2m. in grants and exchequer loans towards development over the period 1959–64. The programme provides for both social and economic projects, the main headings being:—

£
Soil conservation and agriculture 400,000
Medical services .. .. .. 143,000
Education .. .. .. .. 119,000
Roads and airfields .. .. 104,000
Water and electricity supplies .. 95,000
A further allocation of £418,000 was made in 1961 for development to March 1963. This was to be used principally towards Education, Medical, Agriculture and Veterinary services.

A further allocation of £1.2m. was approved in September 1963, and with the balances available under the earlier allocations, this provides some £1.703m. for development in the period ending March 1966.
Education

African education is largely in the hands of three missions (Paris Evangelical, Roman Catholic and Anglican), under the direction of the Education Department. Grants-in-aid are paid by Government to the three missions to cover the salaries of all teachers on the approved grant-in-aid list. In many aided schools of the Roman Catholic mission, assistance is given by unaided teachers, particularly Sisters. Small grants are paid from territorial funds to improve the equipment in primary schools. Estimated expenditure on grants-in-aid during financial year 1963–64 is £536,750.

There are 1,102 schools and institutions in the Territory of which all but 13 are under mission control, and of these mission schools 985 are either wholly or partly aided. The total enrolment in primary schools is 151,719, with 2,672 teachers of whom 1,205 have no professional certificate. In secondary schools the enrolment is 2,209, with 104 teachers all with professional training. The enrolment in the teacher training schools is 584, in schools offering a technical course 171 and in housecraft schools 372.

The University of Basutoland, Bechuanaland Protectorate and Swaziland formed by Royal Charter, formally came into existence on the 1st January 1964, and is the independent successor to the Roman Catholic Pius XII College at Roma. It will award its own degrees, and is intended by 1967 to have some 300 students primarily, but not exclusively, from the three High Commission Territories. Students are to be enrolled regardless of race, sex, nationality or religion. The immediate aim of the University is to meet the High Commission Territories' needs for qualified administrators in the Civil Service and the Judiciary, and in the trading profession, and to produce graduates
qualified in Agriculture, Law, Economics and Commerce.
Tuition in primary schools is free, and bursaries are available for the most deserving cases at all stages of secondary, teacher training and University work.
The number of known Basotho students abroad on full time courses of a year's duration or more at the end of 1963 was 87, an increase of some 30 on the figure for the end of 1962.
Health

The Government Medical staff of the Territory consists of a Director of Health, 22 Medical Officers, one Medical Officer of Health, one Medical Officer for Mental Health and one Surgical Specialist. There are nine Government hospitals, staffed by European Nursing Sisters with subordinate African staff. There is accommodation for 664 patients in the Government hospitals. A 230 bed Government hospital in Maseru, which with the gracious consent of Her Majesty has been named the Queen Elizabeth II Hospital, was officially opened on the 25th July 1957. There are six mission hospitals subsidized by the Government, with a total accommodation of 467 beds. Health centres in mountain dispensaries provide out-patient medical facilities, and, where a trained African nurse is a member of the staff, maternity services to people living in remote areas. During 1962 19,480 patients were admitted to Government hospitals and 7,366 to mission hospitals. During the period 1937 to 1962 the outpatients at both Government and Mission dispensaries and clinics increased from 110,807 to 738,499. The Leper Settlement, four miles out of Maseru, had 326 patients at the end of 1962.

The principal diseases are venereal disease, chronic rheumatism, malnutrition, infections of the respiratory tract and dyspepsia. The heaviest toll of lives in children is due to tuberculosis, malnutrition, diphtheria, whooping-cough and gastro-enteritis. The incidence of nutritional and deficiency diseases is comparatively high and is allied to maize being the staple food. Typhus, plague and smallpox occur only rarely, and then in sporadic form, while diphtheria, typhoid fever, measles and whooping cough are endemic.
Communications

The Territory is linked with the railway system of the Republic of South Africa by a short branch-line from Maseru, the capital, to Marseilles on the Bloemfontein-Natal main line. One mile of this line is in Basutoland. The South African Railway runs near the boundaries of the Territory and goods are transported by road to the nearest railway station across the border.

The Road Motor Transport Department of the South African Railways runs regular services between Fouriesburg and Butha-Buthe, Zastron and Quthing, Zastron and Mohale's Hoek, and Quthing and Sebapala, and within the Territory in the lowland areas there are a number of bus services owned and operated by Basotho.

The greater part of Basutoland is very mountainous and in this area all transport is by pack animals. A new mountain motor road running eastwards from Maseru into the mountains has been constructed in order to open up the interior and facilitate its development and administration. The road has reached a point 80 miles from Maseru.
Digitized by Google
THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST

On the western side of Basutoland a fairly good road system has been developed in the strip of agricultural country running from north to south. The main roads traverse the whole of this area, with branches leading west to the principal points of exit. From the main roads a number of feeder-roads lead towards the foothills.
Principal Occupations

Basutoland is a pastoral country without factories or industrial undertakings, and since the produce of the land is insufficient to support the total population, many able-bodied Basotho leave the Territory each year to take up employment in the Republic of South Africa. The Government protects labourers by forbidding recruitment for any mine or other concern where the conditions of work are not good. All labourers recruited by agents in Basutoland for work outside the Territory have to be attested before an official and the contract must be according to Government Regulations.
Main Crops and Products

So far there has been little evidence of any exploitable mineral deposits in the Territory, but recent prospecting for diamonds has shown encouraging results. For the present, agriculture and animal husbandry are Basutoland's main sources of wealth. The chief exports at present are wheat, wool and mohair. It is estimated that 1,000,000 acres are under cultivation.

The soils in the mountain areas are of basaltic origin, while those in the lowlands are derived mainly from underlying cave sandstone. In the lowlands the soil has been cropped continuously for upwards of 80 years. Due to the absence of fuel, practically all cattle manure is burnt, so that little or no organic matter is returned to the land. Thus with increasing population, both human and livestock, excessive demands have been made on the soil, which has lost its structure and become subject to serious erosion. The soils in the mountains which have been brought into cultivation comparatively recently are rich though shallow. Controlled grazing has brought back a grass cover and has effectively halted the loss of soil by storm water run off in the mountain areas. In 1961 some 1,252,000 trees were planted as part of soil conservation measures.
In 1962 there were 376,740 head of cattle and 1,227,386 sheep in the Territory. Wool, followed by mohair, hides and skins, continue to be the main source of cash income from the animal population.
Trade

Basutoland has no industries and imports the consumer goods and capital items it needs as well as a certain amount of agricultural produce and livestock. Exports consist almost entirely of agricultural commodities and livestock, the main exports being wool and mohair, followed by wheat, sorghum and cattle. The value of imports usually exceeds the value of exports but the adverse balance is partly offset by the export of labour to the Republic of South Africa.

THE BECHUANALAND PROTECTORATE
Area

The Protectorate has not been surveyed as a whole, but its area is estimated at 222,000 square miles. The headquarters of the Protectorate Administration is at Mafeking, across the border in the Cape Province of the Republic of South Africa.
Population
The last census was taken in 1956 and showed a population of 3,173 Europeans, 676 Coloured, 248 Asians and 316,578 Africans. A further census is planned during the first half of 1964.

The population is not evenly distributed, the bulk of the people living in the eastern part of the Protectorate with one fairly large tribe occupying the north-western corner. The central, western and southern areas of the territory, consisting for the most part of the Kalahari Desert, are extremely sparsely inhabited.
The Bechuana tribes tend to live in large villages or towns. About one-half of the population lives in towns of more than 1,000 inhabitants.
Principal Towns
Francistown, Gaberones, Kanye, Lobatsi, Maun, Molepolole, Mochudi and Serowe.
Geographical Features

The Bechuanaland Protectorate is bounded on the south by the Province of the Cape of Good Hope, on the south-east by the Transvaal, on the north-east by Southern Rhodesia, on the north by the Caprivi Zipfel and on the west by South West Africa. It lies between latitudes $18^\circ$ and $37^\circ$ south and longitudes $20^\circ$ and $29^\circ 50'$ east. The Kalahari Desert extends over most of the Protectorate west and south of Ngamiland as far as

latitude $27^\circ$ S. This is not a desert in the commonly accepted sense of the word, but consists of a vast expanse of undulating sand-belts with outcrops of limestone. In these limestone belts water is sometimes found at depths from 30 to 100 feet and it is possible that under the deep overburden of sand there may exist untapped reservoirs. The northern and south-western corners of the Kalahari have the least vegetation and most sand dunes, but elsewhere the grasses are excellent, and this is the natural habitat of the aboriginal Bushmen. Large areas are wooded like parklands with camel-thorn and other indigenous trees.

In the north-western part of the Protectorate the Okovango River, flowing inland from Angola, widens into a delta from which flood-waters, in seasons of heavy rainfall, flow as far south-east as the Makarikari salt-lake and south and south-west into Lake Ngami. The Chobe River, also flowing from Angola, inundates a large area in the north before flowing into the Zambesi 60 miles west of the Victoria Falls.
The mean altitude of the Protectorate is 3,000 feet and in the eastern portion occasional elevations, e.g. at Kanye, Serowe and Ghanzi, reach 4,000 to 5,000 feet.
The Protectorate as a whole is a natural game-reserve for most species of the fauna of Africa and Government policy aims at their preservation.
Climate
The climate is sub-tropical (latitude $22^\circ$ S. passes through the centre of the Protectorate) but varies with latitude and altitude.
The average annual rainfall is 18 inches, varying from 25 inches in the north to 9 inches or less in the western Kalahari.

In the higher parts, days are pleasantly warm and the nights cold during the winter, but the summer is universally hot. In August seasonal winds blow from the west coast and, desiccated by the Kalahari, carry sand and dust across the whole country.
The atmosphere throughout the year is very dry and this helps somewhat to mitigate the high temperature.
History

In 1884 the British Government appointed a Deputy Commissioner to the Bechuana tribes and in 1885 the whole of the Bechuanaland was, with the concurrence of Khama and other principal chiefs, proclaimed to be under the protection of Her Majesty The Queen. The southern part of the territory, which included Mafeking, Vryburg and Kuruman, was at the same time constituted a Crown Colony with the title British Bechuanaland. In 1885 British Bechuanaland was annexed to Cape Colony, subsequently the Cape Province of the Republic of South Africa. The northern part, known as the Bechuanaland Protectorate, has continued to be administered by the Government of the United Kingdom.

The administration of the Bechuanaland Protectorate is governed by various Orders in Council and Proclamations, of which the most important is the Order in Council of Her Majesty Queen Victoria dated 9th May 1891. That Order empowered the then High Commissioner for South Africa to exercise on Her Majesty's behalf all the powers and jurisdiction which Her Majesty had or might have within the Protectorate and to that end authorized him to 'do' or cause to be
E 2
THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST

taken all such measures as he might think expedient, subject to such instructions as he might from time to time receive from Her Majesty or through a Secretary of State. Other provisions of the Order empowered the High Commissioner to appoint administrative and judicial officers and to assign their functions to them subject to the preservation of his own powers in their entirety and to provide, by proclamation from time to time, for the administration of justice, the raising of revenue and the peace, order and good government of all persons within the Protectorate, including the prohibition and punishment of acts tending to disturb the public peace.

In issuing these proclamations the High Commissioner was instructed by the Order in Council to respect any Native laws and customs by which the civil relations of any Native chiefs, tribes or population under Her Majesty's protection were at that time (viz., in May 1891) regulated, except in so far as the same might be incompatible with the due exercise of Her Majesty's power and jurisdiction or which were repugnant to humanity.

The Order required the High Commissioner to publish his proclamations in the Gazette and reserved to Her Majesty the right to disallow any such proclamation. The Order also provided that, subject to any proclamation lawfully issued by the High Commissioner, any jurisdiction exercisable otherwise than under this Order in Council of 1891, whether by virtue of any Statute or Order in Council, or of any treaty, or otherwise, should remain in full force.
Her Majesty reserved the power to revoke, alter, add to or amend this Order at any time.
Constitution

The Protectorate is administered by Her Majesty's Commissioner, subject to the overriding power of the High Commissioner for Basutoland, the Bechuanaland Protectorate and Swaziland and is divided for administrative purposes into ten districts under District Commissioners who are assisted in the maintenance of law and order by a force of police.
The territory comprises Crown lands, blocks of European farms and tribal Reserves.

In the African areas the method of administration is that generally known as "indirect rule". Tribal administrations were formally established and the powers and rights of African authorities were defined in the African Administration and African Courts Proclamations of 1943 (Caps. 56 and 5 of the Laws, Cap. 56 having now been replaced by Proc. No. 24 of 1956) which replace the original Proclamations 74 and 75 of 1934. In 1938 Tribal Treasuries were successfully established in all Tribal Territories. Government pays 50% of each Tribal Administration's collection of native tax to the tribal treasury concerned; other sources of revenue are a graded tax and standrents. The Chiefs and Finance Committees, under the guidance of District Commissioners, are responsible for the preparation of estimates and general management of their treasuries.

On 28th July 1960 the Secretary of State for Commonwealth Relations announced his approval of proposals for a new constitution in the Protectorate. These proposals were promulgated by the Bechuanaland Protectorate (Constitution) Order in Council, 1960, and provide for a Legislative Council with power to legislate for the Protectorate, subject to certain reserve powers remaining with the High Commissioner. The

Legislative Council has an elected unofficial majority; there are 21 elected members, of whom 10 are European, 10 African and one Asian; there are also 10 Government officials and four nominated unofficial members. The European elected members are elected by European voters, subject to certain qualifications of age, residence and property or income; the Asian elected member is elected by Asian voters similarly qualified. The African elected members are elected by an African Council consisting of Chiefs of the eight principal tribes, 32 members elected by the tribal or district councils together with the official and appointed members. The Executive Council consists of six officials and four unofficials two of whom are European and two African.

The Bechuanaland Protectorate (Constitution) (Amendment) Order, 1963 created the post of Her Majesty's Commissioner in place of the former post of Resident Commissioner. The new post is equivalent in status to that of a Governor. It also empowered the Commissioner to assent to legislation and made him directly responsible to the Secretary of State.

During the second half of 1963 Her Majesty's Commissioner held discussions with the leaders of political parties and important groups in the Protectorate for the purpose of revising the Constitution. A local White Paper, recording the outcome of these discussions, was published in November 1963. The White Paper proposed a Ministerial form of Government, with a Legislative Assembly constituted on the basis of universal adult suffrage but with a small number of special members elected by the Assembly itself. Her Majesty's Commissioner would retain responsibility for defence, external relations, internal security, the Public Service and finance, as well as general reserve executive and legislative powers. The White Paper envisaged elections for the new constitution if possible in the Autumn of 1964, but in any case not later than the first quarter of 1965. It was envisaged that these proposals would in due course be submitted to the Secretary of State.
Taxation

The main forms of direct taxation are African tax and African graded tax, income tax, personal tax and cattle export tax. The principal sources of indirect taxation are customs and excise duties at varying rates. The Bechuanaland Protectorate is in a customs union with the Republic of South Africa and receives a fixed percentage of the gross revenue collected on all imports into the Republic. Licensing exists for many trades, businesses and professions. There are also death duties.

Education

There are eight main tribes in the Protectorate. Communications are primitive, distances great and financial resources far from commensurate with urgent needs. Consequently the administration and promotion of African education are unusually difficult.

Most of the administrative work has devolved upon tribal school committees and in three remote areas education is administered directly by the District Commissioners concerned and the Department of Education.

Primary Education. Enrolled in the 235 African primary schools in the Protectorate during 1962 were 46,536 pupils of whom approximately 56% were girls. The disparity between the sexes, caused by the absence of boys at cattle-posts and on the land, has had a retarding influence ever since schools were first established.
Secondary Education. Three of the eight secondary schools in the Territory are co-educational secondary boarding institutions offering the Cambridge School Certificate course.
These are Moeng College, founded by the late Mr. Tshekedi Khama, a Catholic Mission school, St. Joseph's College, near Gaberones and the
London Missionary School, Moeding College, which was opened in 1962. All are grant-aided by Government.

The remaining five are day secondary schools which offer three-year courses leading to the Junior Certificate. Three are administered by Tribal School Committees, one is a grant-aided Catholic Mission school in Francistown and one is an independent unaided school in Serowe.
Total enrolment in 1962 was 764.
Trades Training. A Trades School, built at a cost of £65,000 from Colonial Development and Welfare funds was opened in 1962.
It offers courses in building, carpentry and motor mechanics.

Teacher Training. One Teacher Training College is situated in Lobatsi and a second has been opened in Serowe. Capital cost of the latter, paid for from C.D. & W. funds amounted to approximately £100,000.
Total output from both Colleges will rise to about 100 per annum in about two years time.

Youth Movements. There are 4,000 Boy Scouts, divided into 92 groups and 6,106 Girl Guides, in 103 groups. Monthly bulletins are issued to all Scout and Guide officers. A government grant of £150 per year to the Girl Guides and £200 per year to the Boy Scouts is applied chiefly to training and travelling expenses.

The total estimated expenditure on educational service from funds controlled by Government, by Tribal Administrations and by missions during the year 1962 was £559,892. Of this total £96,775 was contributed by Colonial Development and Welfare schemes.
Health

There are seven Government hospitals and five Government Health Centres, together with 41 dispensaries. Various missions administer six hospitals and two Health Centres and 28 out-station dispensaries. These hospitals, health centres and dispensaries provide 1,081 beds and 295 cots. During 1962 21,730 patients were admitted to hospitals, and attendances for treatment as out-patients numbered 389,850, plus 73,189 attendances for vaccinations, inoculations, etc., making a total of 484,769. Maternity work, including ante-natal and post-natal care, is mainly carried out in the hospitals but there is a special maternity centre administered by the London Missionary Society and supervised by the Government Medical Officer of the District.

The technical staff consists of the Director of Medical Services, Deputy Director of Medical Services, two Medical Officers of Health, fourteen Medical Officers, three Health Inspectors, one Matron, four Sisters-in-Charge, one Sister Tutor and 11 Sisters. There is a subsidiary African staff, trained, semi-trained and untrained. In addition there are six medical missionary centres with European and African staff.

African nurses are trained at both Government and Mission hospitals. Midwives are trained at the Government Hospital, Serowe, at the Maun Maternity Centre and the Molopolole Mission. Nurses undergo a three-year course in general nursing, at the end of which they receive a year's training in midwifery.
African Sanitary Inspectors are trained under the supervision of the Health Inspectors.

Communications

The main railway line from Cape Town to the north runs through the Protectorate, entering it at Ramathlabama, 886 miles from Cape Town, and leaving it at Ramaquabane, 398 miles farther north. The line runs roughly parallel to the eastern boundary of the territory at an average distance from it of about 55 miles. The railway within the territory is owned by the Rhodesia Railways and the section Mahalapye/Ramathlabama is operated by the South African Railways Administration as agents. The gauge is 3 feet 6 inches.

The roads are almost exclusively of earth or sand, the only exceptions being short lengths of gravel, and, in two townships, bitumen surfaces over short distances. There is limited bridging over the main rivers.
Thirty-two landing-grounds and emergency strips are maintained in the Protectorate.
Principal Occupations

Over 95% of the population is engaged in stock raising. A few Africans are employed as drovers and herdsmen by European farmers but the great majority are themselves owners of livestock, which are cared for on a family or tribal basis. The principal occupations of the small number of African wage earners are:

Government service 3,300*
Agriculture 3,500
Building 500
Trade and industry 3,500
Domestic service 2,000
* This figure includes approximately 1,700 casual labourers.
Main Crops and Products

The main occupation of the Bechuana is cattle raising. The number of cattle in 1962 was 1,351,778, of which 18,777 animals and 90,252 carcases were exported. There were also 430,016 sheep, goats and pigs. Seasonal lack of water and grazing and indifferent animal husbandry are the chief handicaps to the expansion of the industry.

Irregular rainfall and primitive agricultural practices, aggravated by the migration of able-bodied men to work in the Republic are the cause of occasional crop failures. The principal crops are sorghum and beans.
A little gold and some kyanite are mined in the north-east, asbestos near Kanye in the Banwaketse Reserve and manganese in the Bangwaketse and Bamalete Reserves.

SWAZILAND
Area

Swaziland, the smallest of the three High Commission territories is bordered on the north, west and south by the Transvaal, on the north-east by Mocambique and on the south-east by Natal. It lies between latitudes $24^\circ 50'$ and $26^\circ 40'$ south and longitudes $30^\circ 25'$ and $32^\circ 5'$ east. With a total area of 6,704 square miles, it is a country of many diversities and many distinctive characteristics.
Population

In mid-1962 it was estimated that the total population was 280,300 comprising 270,000 Africans (an approximation) and, shown by a census of the two groups, 8,040 Europeans and 2,260 Euroafricans. This figure includes about 10,300 residents employed temporarily outside the Territory. It is estimated that 26,810 residents were born in countries other than Swaziland (South Africa 19,020; Mocambique 4,070 and British Isles 760). The 1956 census showed that the population of Swaziland was 240,511.
Principal towns

The capital is Mbabane (population 8,400) and the next two largest proclaimed townships are Manzini (7,800) and Stegi (3,400). In addition, four new industrial centres have populations between 4,600 and 7,000. They are: Tshaneni/Mhlume (sugar), Big Bend/Ubombo (sugar), Bunya/Mhambanyati (wood pulp) and Havelock (asbestos).

Geographical Features

Geographically and climatically, it is divisible into four physiographic provinces—the broken mountainous high-veld in the west, with altitudes averaging over 4,000 feet, the middle-veld which is mostly mixed farming country about 2,000 feet lower and the low-veld, a hot woodland region typified by aloes, acacias and pterocarpus, and bounded on the east by the Lubombo mountains, with an average altitude of 1,500 feet. The Lubombo mountains form the fourth physiographic province. Four rivers, the Komati, Usutu, Mbuluzi and Ngwavuma, flow from west to east, cutting their way through the Lubombo mountains to the Indian Ocean. The exploitation of these rivers is particularly important to the agricultural development of the middle and bush veld, where irrigation projects are giving the scenery a different aspect.
Climate

75% to 80% of the rains come in the summer half of the year from October to March. Year to year variability of totals is extremely large, especially in the low-veld. Drought hazard is also most serious in the low-veld. The average rainfall at Mbabane in the high-veld is 54.6 inches, at Manzini (formerly Bremersdorp) in the middle-veld 35.6 inches and at Big Bend in the low-veld 20.8 inches. The mean maximum and minimum temperatures are respectively 72.8°F. and 51.6°F. at Mbabane; 79.2°F. and 56.4°F. at Manzini; and 84.5°F. and 59.8°F. at Big Bend.
History

Swaziland remained nominally independent until 1890. From 1890 to 1894 the United Kingdom and the Transvaal (South African Republic) Governments established a species of condominium which was replaced in 1894 by an arrangement under which the South African Republic was given powers of protection and administration without actual incorporation. After the Anglo-Boer war, the administration of Swaziland was transferred to the Governor of the Transvaal, who was also the High Commissioner of South Africa, but in 1907 responsibility for Swaziland was transferred from the Governor to the High Commissioner for South Africa. Swaziland therefore remained under the control of the United Kingdom when the Transvaal became a province of the Union of South Africa in 1910.
Constitution

In 1902, at the end of the Anglo-Boer war, the British Special Commissioner with a small force of the South African Constabulary was sent into Swaziland to establish a provisional Administration. Shortly afterwards, an Order in Council dated the 25th of June 1903, was issued which established the relationship between Great Britain and the Swazi and constitutes the basic authority under which the Administration has been conducted by Her Majesty's Government.

Swaziland is administered by Her Majesty's Commissioner, subject to the overriding power of the High Commissioner for Basutoland, the Bechuanaland Protectorate and Swaziland. Her Majesty's Commissioner exercises such administration and control and is vested with all such powers, authorities and jurisdiction as are conferred upon him by the Swaziland Administration Proclamation of 1904 and other laws, or by the terms of his Commission. He is assisted and advised by the Chief Secretary and by the heads

of the various Government departments. The Territory is divided into six districts which are administered by District Commissioners stationed at Mbabane, Manzini, Hlatikulu, Piggs Peak, Mankaiana and Stegi. The District Commissioners are the principal executive officers of Government within their districts and directly responsible to Her Majesty's Commissioner for their efficient administration. The Judiciary falls under the Chief Justice of the High Commission Territories.

Her Majesty's Commissioner is advised by the European Advisory Council on matters directly affecting the European residents of Swaziland and on any other matters specifically referred to the Council by Her Majesty's Commissioner. The Territory is divided into ten electoral divisions and each returns one member to the Council, which usually meets twice a year. Urgent matters are, however, dealt with by the Council's Standing Committee. On matters concerning the African population Her Majesty's Commissioner consults the Paramount Chief and through him the Swazi National Council.

A great deal of the structure of the traditional Swazi political system has been retained in the modern pattern of the Swazi National Administration. The Ngwenyama (the Paramount Chief) is the constitutional ruler who is advised by his kinsmen and his chosen Councillors and cannot initiate action without the approval of two formally constituted Councils. The Liqoqo, comprising the more important of Ngwenyama's kinsmen and a number of chosen advisers, and the Libandla, which at its widest extension is a Council of every adult male in the nation. It is recognised as the final body from which approval for any contemplated act or legislation should be obtained.
From the central institutions of the Swazi National Administration, responsibility for the routine administration of the country devolves upon the Chiefs and Indunas.

In May 1963, the Secretary of State published a White Paper outlining a constitution for Swaziland. This constitution was later promulgated in the Swaziland Order in Council, 1963, the main provisions of which came into operation on 3rd January, 1964. It provides for an Executive Council of eight members (four official and four unofficial) and a Legislative Council with four official members, eight Swazi certified by the Ngwenyama-in-Council as elected by traditional methods, eight Europeans (four of whom will be elected on a European roll and four on a national roll), eight persons of any race elected by voters on the national roll, and finally up to three members nominated by Her Majesty's Commissioner. Election on the national roll will virtually be by universal adult franchise. The elections for the first Legislative Council are due to take place in mid-1964.

There are proclaimed townships at Mbabane, Manzini, Stegi, Hlatikulu and Goedegun. In each of these townships and in Piggs Peak village, an Urban Area Advisory Committee functions under the chairmanship of the District Commissioner and advises him on the administration and welfare of the township and surrounding urban area. These Committees consist of five elected members and Government officials nominated by Her Majesty's Commissioner. There are, in addition, African Advisory Committees which advise the District Commissioner on the needs of the urban African population. Attention is now being given to the question of granting municipal status to the townships of Mbabane and Manzini, and to the establishment

THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST

of district councils in the administrative districts of the Territory. As a preliminary step to the establishment of district councils, the Government is reorganizing district administration by reducing the number of administrative districts from six to four with a view to providing a better distribution of population and industrial development, a more efficient administration and a division of boundaries which take better account of communities of common interest. Whilst no formal machinery has been established to effect joint consultation between the different communities in the Territory, Standing Committees of the Swazi Nation and the European Advisory Council, as well as representatives of the Euroafrican Welfare Association, meet together to discuss matters of territorial importance. In terms of the Swaziland General Law and Administration Proclamation—Chapter 3 of the Laws of Swaziland—Roman Dutch Law is the common law of the Territory, except where modified by statute. In civil matters Swazi law and custom may be followed where necessary. Statute law consists of Transvaal laws in force at the date of the General Law and Administration Proclamation and all subsequent laws promulgated by the High Commissioner. A Special Court, now the High Court, having the full jurisdiction of a Superior Court, was also established together with Courts of District Commissioners with a limited jurisdiction. A Court of Appeal for Basutoland, the Bechuanaland Protectorate and Swaziland was established in 1955, but the right of further appeal to the Judicial Committee of the Privy Council was retained.
Taxation

The main forms of direct taxation are African tax, European poll tax and income tax, towards which mining companies contribute the major share. The principal sources of indirect taxation are customs and excise duties at varying rates. Swaziland is in a customs union with the Republic of South Africa and receives a fixed percentage of the gross revenue collected on imports into the Republic. Licensing exists for many trades. There are also death duties.

Development Plans

Under the Colonial Development and Welfare Act, 1959, Swaziland received an allocation of a little over £4m. in grants and exchequer loans towards development over the period 1959-64. The programme provides for both social and economic projects, the main headings being:

A further allocation of £280,000 was made in 1961 for development to March 1963. This is being used principally for Education, Hospital extensions and Electricity supplies.

Under the Commonwealth Development Act, 1963, Swaziland received a further allocation of £2m. towards development in the period 1963-66, which, together with the unspent balance of previous allocations, will permit spending in this period at a markedly higher rate than the average rate of Colonial Development and Welfare expenditure in the territory over the last few years.
A new territorial development plan for the period 1963-66 is in course of preparation.

The construction of the first railway in Swaziland began in mid-1962 and the line should be open for traffic by the end of 1964. The railway will run a distance of 140 miles to the Mozambique border and will eventually link Swaziland with the port of Lourenço Marques. Its construction will make possible the development of large iron ore deposits at Ngwenya and will facilitate the export of wood pulp and sugar.
Education

African Primary Education is largely the responsibility of a number of Church Missions which receive substantial financial assistance from the Government in the form of salary and equipment grants. Apart from 236 schools falling under the direct superintendence of the Missions there are 20 Government maintained schools, three National schools financed by the Swazi Treasury and 36 tribal schools. Enrolment which stood at 13,797 in 1950 reached 44,802 Swazis in 1963. In addition to the primary schools there are six schools proceeding to G.C.E. "O" level and 19 junior secondary schools, two Teacher Training Centres, a Housecraft Training Centre and a Trade Training Centre which offers courses in building, carpentry, motor mechanics, and electrical wiring and maintenance.

Many bursaries are granted each year to assist students taking secondary school courses leading to matriculation, teacher training courses, post-matriculation courses at University level and various forms of industrial training.

For European children, education is compulsory up to the age of sixteen or the successful completion of Standard VIII. There are two high schools and 11 primary schools with a total enrolment of 1,545 in 1963.

The Coloured, or Euroafrican, community is numerically the smallest group in Swaziland and comprises a wide range of types with varying material and cultural standards. The educational requirements of coloured children are met by five Government-aided Mission boarding-schools,

THE HIGH COMMISSION TERRITORIES
two of which offer secondary education up to G.C.E. "O" level. Total enrolment in 1963 was 840.
There are two Teacher Training Centres offering Primary Lower and Primary Higher training. In addition there is a Housecraft Training Centre at Mbuluzi.
In 1963 the hitherto exclusively European schools admitted African and coloured children to the grades.
Health

Posts are provided in the Medical Department for the Director, nine Medical Officers, two Medical Officers of Health, two Pharmacists, four Dispensers, two Matrons, thirteen Nursing Sisters, four Health Inspectors, eighty-eight Nurses as well as auxiliary medical staff. Seven Doctors and thirteen Nursing Sisters as well as thirty-seven Nurses are employed at Mission Hospitals, subsidised by Government.

There are four Government hospitals, at Mbabane, Hlatikulu, Mankaiana and Pigg's Peak. At Mbabane there are 150 beds, Hlatikulu 137 beds, Mankaiana 24 beds and Pigg's Peak 39 beds. The Raleigh Fitkin Memorial Hospital (Nazarene Mission) at Manzini has accommodation for 246 patients, the Roman Catholic Mission Hospital at Stegi for 35 patients and the Mahamba Methodist Hospital for 45 patients.

There are 10 Government, 17 Mission and three other clinics, financed by the Swazi National Treasury, in the Territory. The headquarters of the public health staff under the Medical Officer of Health is at Manzini where there is a well-equipped laboratory; in addition a Tuberculosis control administrative building and laboratory has been erected.
Communications

The road motor-transport services of the South African Railways Administration and the Portuguese East African Administration carry both goods and passengers and operate between Swaziland and the railheads in the neighbouring territories. A railway line to the Mozambique border is under construction.
The Territory is served by a network of roads which are being rapidly brought to good standards. There is an extensive telecommunications system.
Main Crops and Products

Cattle form the main wealth of the Swazis and the total number owned increased from 146,542 in 1921 to 420,874 in 1962. The number of cattle owned by others increased from 63,748 in 1921 to 122,519 in 1962. In 1962 there were 229,049 goats, 42,775 sheep and 99,604 pigs.

Slaughter cattle are exported to markets in Johannesburg and Durban which are controlled by export quotas allocated by the South African Meat Industry Control Board. The number of cattle exported is increasing. In 1962 it was 21,213. Total production of butter in 1962 was 459,221 lb.

Mining is an important part of Swaziland's economy. The Havelock asbestos mine in the north-western corner of the Territory is one of the largest asbestos mines in the world and is apparently assured of a life of at least 30 years at present rates of output. Rich deposits of iron ore north-west of Mbabane will be developed in 1964 with the completion then of the railway link to

Mozambique. Other deposits of iron ores are known to occur in the Pigg's Peak, Mankaiana and Hlatikulu districts. There is a small tin-mining industry and barytes is being worked in the Mbabane district. There are extensive deposits of low volatile bituminous and anthracitic coal in the lowveld. The bituminous coal deposits will be worked to provide coal for the railway and the sugar mills in 1964. The refractory minerals diaspore and andalusite are being worked in the Mankaiana district, where pyrophyllite is also being exploited. In the same district considerable tonnages of high grade kaolin are mined. There are three small gold mines in production in the Mbabane and Pigg's Peak districts. Recently further rich gold values have been intersected in exploratory work in the Hoho area.

The agricultural potential of Swaziland is considerable and most crops can be grown. There has been an immense improvement in agricultural methods among the Swazi due to the stimulus of the rural development schemes which were inaugurated in 1949.

During the last ten years much has been done to lay the foundations for the development of this potential, both on Title Deed and Swazi Nation land. One of the most important aspects of this work has been the establishment of an agricultural research service in the Territory.
Maize, which is the staple food of the Swazi, is the most important dryland crop. But because of drought in 1962 170,000 bags of maize had to be imported to meet the food requirements of the country.

The most important dryland cash crop is still cotton, although there was a substantial fall in production in 1962, compared with a record crop in the previous year. The 1962 figure was 2,963 short tons valued at £207,000. In 1961 production was 5,297 tons valued at £359,300. Other important dryland cash crops produced in 1962 were: pineapples (2,900 tons worth £20,000); tobacco (592,102 lb. worth £33,900) and bananas (2,064 tons worth £30,000).

Irrigation agriculture is centred on three main schemes; that on the Komati River, developed by the Colonial Development Corporation, commands 30,000 acres of irrigable land; the other two privately run projects extract water from the Great Usutu River and command a total of 27,000 irrigable acres. There are numerous minor irrigation schemes throughout the middle and low veld regions. Principal crops grown on these schemes during 1962 were sugar cane, citrus, rice and tomatoes; the production of other irrigated crops such as cotton and maize is being investigated by the research service. During the 1961-62 season sugar production amounted to 76,889 tons of milled sugar valued at £2,355,000 from 665,000 tons of cane delivered to mills; citrus fruit marketed through the local co-operative amounted to 66,500 export cases and 97,000 pockets, worth a total of £96,400. There were over 410,000 citrus trees in orchards by the end of the 1961-62 season. The 1962 rice crop, worth £320,000, totalled 6,360 short tons and the winter-grown tomato crop for the same year amounted to 2,311 tons—worth £17,300.

The Republic of South Africa is the principal market for the Territory's agricultural produce, viz.: all cotton, tobacco, rice, bananas and canned tomato and some of the sugar produced find a ready market in that country. Rice, citrus and tobacco are marketed through co-operative organisations, the last two named being affiliated

Considerable development has taken place in the forestry industry during the last two decades. The total area afforested by the end of 1962 was 222,000 acres, of which 180,000 acres were pines. The largest of the three main forestry projects, the Usutu Pulp Company, entered the production stage towards the end of 1961. This company, which was initiated by the Colonial Development Corporation aims to export 100,000 tons of unbleached wood pulp annually. Timber and timber products worth £487,000 were exported during 1962.

HONG KONG
Area

The area of land is approximately 398½ square miles (Hong Kong Island 29 square miles, Kowloon 3½ square miles, Stonecutters Island ¼ square mile, New Territories (leased) 365½ square miles). It includes all islands within a rectangular area of some 738 square miles, containing the leased and ceded territory. British waters are bounded on the north by the shores of Deep Bay and Mirs Bay, between which lies the land frontier with China.
Population

The population of the Colony at the end of 1962 was 3,526,500. It is not practicable to divide these by race, but over 98% speak the Chinese language or have traditional connexions with China, though more than half are British subjects by virtue of birth in the Colony. At the 1961 Census, out of 3,129,648 residents including Service personnel and their families, only 49,747 gave their place of origin as outside Hong Kong and China; 33,140 of these were of Commonwealth and 16,607 of non-Commonwealth origin.
Principal Towns
The overwhelming majority of the population lives on Hong Kong Island or in Kowloon. The capital is Victoria, on Hong Kong Island.

The principal centres of population in the New Territories are Tsuen Wan, which has recently grown into an important industrial centre with large textile, enamel and rubber factories, iron works, etc.; Cheung Chau, a small but densely populated island, important as a market and
Digitized by Google
HONG KONG

fishing centre; Yuen Long, a mainland market town; Tai O, a fishing and market centre on Lantao Island; Tai Po and Shek Wu Hui, both mainland market towns; and Peng Chau, an island fishing port, with some industries.
Geographical Features

The Colony consists of the island of Hong Kong and a portion of the mainland to the North, together with 235 adjacent islands ranging from Lantao with an area of about 58 square miles, to uninhabited rocky islets. A peninsula, on which Kowloon stands, juts southward from the mainland towards Victoria on Hong Kong island. Between these two lies the harbour, one of the finest natural ports in the world. Much of the built-up area surrounding the harbour has been reclaimed or levelled.

The greater part of the Colony consists of steep, unproductive hillside, in some parts covered with dense scrub, in others seriously eroded as a result of indiscriminate felling of trees during the Japanese war-time occupation. Cultivation is confined mainly to the narrow valleys. The coastline is sharply indented.

A steep range of hills divides Kowloon from the New Territories to the North, in the centre of which is the highest mountain—Tai Mo Shan, 3,140 feet; Lantao Peak is 3,061 feet and Victoria Peak on Hong Kong Island 1,805 feet high.
Climate

The climate is sub-tropical and governed by monsoons, the winter being cool and dry, the summer hot and humid. The mean monthly temperature varies from $59^{\circ}$ F. in February to $82^{\circ}$ F. in July. The actual temperature rarely rises above $95^{\circ}$ F. or falls below $40^{\circ}$ F. The average annual rainfall is 84.76 inches, three-quarters of which falls between May and September. The mean relative humidity exceeds 80% during the summer but in early winter sometimes falls as low as 20%.
History

Hong Kong was founded as a British Colony in 1841, the cession of the Island to Great Britain being confirmed by the Treaty of Nanking in 1842. The area on which the main urban part of Kowloon now stands, together with Stone-cutters Island in the harbour, Ap Lei Chau and Green Island, was ceded by the Convention of Peking in 1860; and in 1898 the New Territories, which consist of the rural area north of Kowloon and the islands around Hong Kong, were leased to Great Britain for 99 years.
The Colony was occupied by the Japanese from 1941 to 1945, and in the following four years made a remarkably rapid recovery.
Constitution

The Government is administered under Letters Patent of 14th February 1917 by a Governor assisted by an Executive Council and a Legislative Council. The Executive Council, which is consulted by the Governor on all important administrative matters, consists of six officials and six unofficials, three of whom are Chinese and one a member of the Colony's Portuguese community. The Legislative Council consists of nine officials and eight unofficials, five of whom are Chinese and one Indian. There is an Urban Council
with power to make by-laws relating to public health, sanitation and other subjects. It consists of six officials and 16 unofficials, eight of whom are elected.
Taxation

(1) Earnings and Profits Tax. —This tax, introduced for the first time in 1947 by the Inland Revenue Ordinance (Cap. 112), is a substitute for the more orthodox type of income tax. Instead of one comprehensive tax there are four separate taxes, as follows:
(a) Profits Tax (subdivided into Corporation Profits Tax and Business Profits Tax).
(b) Salaries Tax.
(c) Interest Tax.
(d) Property Tax.

The standard rate for 1963–64 is $12\frac{1}{2}\%$ and this rate is applied in full in the case of Profits Tax and Interest Tax (subject to an exemption limit under Business Profits Tax where tax is normally only charged if the profits exceed $7,000). Salaries Tax is levied on net income from employment after deducting a personal allowance of $7,000, and allowances for a wife ($7,000) and for up to nine children. The rates of tax charged vary from one-fifth of the standard rate on the first $5,000 of chargeable income to double the standard rate on chargeable income exceeding $45,000. There is a provision, however, whereby the maximum charge for Salaries Tax shall not exceed tax at the standard rate on the total assessable income without the deduction of any allowances. Property Tax is charged on the net rateable value of all land and buildings situated in the Colony other than in the New Territories. Effective 1963/64 the charge is at one-half of the standard rate (i.e. $6\frac{1}{4}\%$ ) if the rent receivable is in fact controlled by reference to 1941 rental but at the full standard rate (i.e. $12\frac{1}{2}\%$ ) on all other properties. However, property wholly occupied by the owner exclusively for residential purposes is exempt from Property Tax.

Under all four taxes the charge is limited to profits or income arising in or derived from the Colony. As an alternative to these separate taxes, a resident of the Colony may elect to be personally assessed on his total Hong Kong income. A single assessment is then made by allowing similar allowances and charging similar rates of tax, as is the case under Salaries Tax. A set-off is then allowed for any amounts already paid under the four separate taxes.

(2) Estate Duty. —Estate Duty is levied on conventional lines at rates varying between 2% in the case of estates valued between $50,000 and $100,000, and 40% in the case of estates valued at over $15 million.

(3) Other Sources of Revenue. —Other sources of revenue are Rates, Stamp Duties, Entertainments Tax, Betting Tax and Business Registration Fees. Excise Duties are also payable on tobacco, hydrocarbon oils, liquor and table waters.

Currency
The value of the Hong Kong dollar is maintained at approximately HK $1 = 1s. 3d. sterling.

The currency in circulation consists of $500, $100, $10 and $5 notes issued by three banks of issue: Hong Kong and Shanghai Banking Corporation, The Chartered Bank and Mercantile Bank, Ltd. respectively. Subsidiary currency consisting of $1 and 1 cent notes and nickel alloy coins in denominations of $1, 50 cents, 10 cents and 5 cents are issued by the Hong Kong Government. The $1 note is gradually being withdrawn from circulation.
Development Plans

Over the next four years it is proposed to expend about $2,300 million on development projects, $200 million of which will be provided from the Colony's Development Loan Fund (mainly on housing), and the balance from general revenues.

Education

In 1963 education was provided in 2,099 schools for over 771,000 pupils. This figure represents approximately one in five of the estimated total population. School enrolment may be divided as follows :

Primary .. .. .. 557,000
Secondary .. .. .. 131,000
Post-secondary .. .. 19,000
Other types of Education .. 64,000

Private schools provided places for some 412,000 children; grant-aided and subsidized schools for 242,000; while 120 Government institutions catered for 117,000. In 1962 there were about 1,800 students in the United Kingdom for higher

studies including those doing technical and vocational courses, while a number pursued their studies elsewhere. Government expenditure on education in the Colony was approximately $161 million as compared with $2½ million in 1938 and $1 million in 1928.

The University of Hong Kong has 1,679 students in the following faculties—arts, science, medicine, engineering and architecture. The annual Government grant to the University totalled £11 million for 1962–63.
Libraries

Local libraries include an English and Chinese library at the University, a British Council Library and a small educational library at the Education Department. Various clubs have well-stocked libraries for the use of their members.
Medical and Health

The Health division of the Medical and Health Department undertakes the control of anti-epidemic measures, the care of expectant and parturient mothers, infant welfare work and the inspection and treatment of school children. It is also responsible for port and airport health work, anti-malarial measures, social hygiene, tuberculosis control and a B.C.G. campaign, food and drug control, public health propaganda and pathological, chemical and biochemical laboratory work. The general state of health is good.

The Government maintains and operates fourteen general and special hospitals and provides financial assistance to eleven other hospitals run by voluntary organizations. In addition, there are in the Colony ten private hospitals which do not receive financial help from the Government. The numbers of beds in the three groups (as on 31st March 1963) are 3,428, 4,473 and 1,361 respectively.

In-patients treated in Government and Government-aided hospitals during 1962 totalled over 158,000 and out-patients in the three main Government hospitals were over 1,025,000. There are also a large number of dispensaries and clinics in both urban and rural areas.
Government expenditure on these services during the year 1962–63 amounted to approximately $94.9 million, compared with $89.0 million in the previous year.
Capital expenditure on medical projects under the Public Works Non-Recurrent head accounted for another $28.2 million.

Following on the outbreak in 1961, cholera El Tor reappeared in August 1962. 11 cases with 1 death were recorded, the last case being confirmed on 13th October, 1962; 20 contact carriers without symptoms were discovered amongst 536 contacts. Cholera appeared again in June 1963.

The most serious health problem is tuberculosis; 14,263 cases were notified in 1962 with 13.2% fatalities. Pneumonia (all forms) occasioned over 2,400 deaths; infant mortality was 36.9 per 1,000 live births.
Communications

Air.—Hong Kong (Kai Tak) Airport with its 8,350 feet of runway continues to expand its role in air travel. Since the opening of the runway in September 1958 together with its modern airport lighting system, regularity of aircraft movements has improved considerably.
Digitized by Google
HONG KONG

An instrument landing system, together with precision approach radar and long and medium range surveillance radar, has been installed and is operating most successfully and being used continually by airlines.

The increase of air traffic is maintained, both aircraft and passenger handling being higher than the previous year. The principal aircraft types are now Boeing 707 and 720, Comet, Convair 880 and 990, Britannia and Electra. During 1962-63, international flights by commercial aircraft carried 606,404 passengers, 7,222.7 tons of freight and 1,601.6 tons of mail, in addition to transit traffic.

Rail.—There is a railway 36 kilometres long connecting Kowloon with the Chinese frontier, but there has been no through passenger traffic since October, 1949. Through goods traffic is conveyed in Chinese section wagons. Total goods carried during 1962 amounted to 385,671 metric tons and 7,004,345 passengers were carried.
Roads.—There are 526 miles of roadway, 97.5% of which is of metalled construction.

Shipping.—43,155 vessels entered and cleared during 1962-63, their net registered tonnage being 44,799,852; 1,399,509 passengers landed or embarked by sea during the same period. Frequent ferry services cross the harbour and link the principal islands to the westward.
Sound and Television Broadcasting

Radio Hong Kong is the Government-owned broadcasting service. It transmits programmes on medium wave (2 kW) and VHF/FM (5 kW) from two separate stations, in English and four Chinese dialects. In addition Radio Hong Kong operates a short-wave station of 2½ kW. There were 142,546 licence holders at the end of 1962. Rediffusion (Hong Kong), Limited, operate a wired broadcasting service on a commercial basis with programmes in English and Chinese. At the same time, this organization is required to relay a certain proportion of programmes from Radio Hong Kong. At the end of 1962 there were some 38,507 subscribers to the service. Rediffusion Limited also operate a wired television service. A commercial broadcasting organization, the Hong Kong Commercial Broadcasting Co., Ltd., transmits programmes on medium wave (1 kW) in English and two Chinese dialects.
Principal Occupations

Before the war most of the urban population was engaged in commerce but, since 1948, there has been a significant growth of local industries. In June 1963, there were 313,358 employees in 7,851 registered and recorded factories and industrial undertakings: 128,160 of them were women. More than 150,000 were estimated as being employed in smaller cottage-type industries and as out-workers. About 90,000 were engaged in agriculture and fishing and 100,000 in building and construction.

The report of the 1961 census recorded an economically active population of 1,211,999. Only 15,802, mostly men, claimed that they were unemployed and 5,098 stated that they were seeking their first jobs. The main groups of occupations were as follows:

Main Crops and Products
The main crops are paddy, vegetables, sweet potatoes and various fruits.

The estimated number of cattle is 17,190 head and of pigs 429,046 head; there are some 2,592,000 head of poultry, including breeding stock and birds for egg production. There are more than 578,600 head of other types of bird, including ducks, pigeons, geese and quail.
In 1962 55,904 tons of fish were landed, as against 50,854 tons in the previous year.

Marketing and Co-operative Organizations

There are twenty-six Vegetable Marketing Co-operative Societies with a membership of more than 9,000. Twenty-five of these Societies have formed a Federation of Vegetable Marketing Co-operative Societies. In addition, two Irrigation Societies, five Salaried Workers' Thrift and Loan Societies, seven Consumers' and Credit Societies, one Fish Pond Society, 207 Housing Societies, nine Agricultural Credit Societies, nine Better Living Societies and 46 Pig Raising Societies have been formed. Many of the last named have also federated and formed a Federation of Pig Raising Societies. Fishermen are becoming more aware of the benefits of co-operation and there are 52 Fishermen's Thrift and Loan Societies, two Fishermen's Thrift Societies, three Fishermen's Credit and Marketing Societies and two Fishermen's Credit and Housing Societies. Thirty-nine Fishermen's Co-operative Societies have formed themselves into four Federations. The total number of members of Co-operative Societies is more than 20,000.

The Vegetable Marketing Organization transports vegetables to the Central Wholesale Market in Kowloon from co-operative societies, collecting centres and depots in the New Territories. The collecting centres which are run by the farmers have, with the exception of two centres, now developed into co-operative societies.

Five Wholesale Fish Markets are operated by the Fish Marketing Organization. A revolving loan fund with a present temporary ceiling of $3,300,000 has existed since 1946 and is of great benefit to fisherfolk; over $13,600,000 has been loaned from it. The Organization also operates schools for fishermen's children and provides education for nearly 3,000 students.

THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST
KENYA

The Constitution providing for internal self-government in Kenya was introduced on the 1st June, 1963. It was agreed in June that, subject to the necessary steps being completed in time, Kenya would become independent on 12th December. This was confirmed during the Conference held in London in September and October, 1963, under the chairmanship of the Secretary of State for the Colonies, and Kenya attained independence and became a member of the Commonwealth on 12th December, 1963.
The Secretary of State for Commonwealth Relations is now the channel of communication between Her Majesty's Government in the United Kingdom and Her Majesty's Government in Kenya.
Basic information about Kenya can be found in the 1963 edition of the Colonial Office List .
MALAYSIA

In May, 1961, the Prime Minister of the Federation of Malaya, Tunku Abdul Rahman, raised the possibility of an association between the Federation, Singapore, North Borneo, Sarawak and Brunei. The British Prime Minister, Mr. Macmillan, invited the Tunku to London for discussions with the object of reaching an understanding on the broad issues and to prepare the way for consultation with North Borneo and Sarawak, without which no commitments about their joining could be entered into by the British Government. The discussions were held on the 20th to 22nd November, 1961, and a joint statement by the British and Malayan Governments was issued immediately afterwards. This inter alia announced (1) the view of both Governments that the inclusion of North Borneo and Sarawak (with the other territories) in the proposed federation was a desirable aim in the interests of the peoples of the territories, and (2) the decision to set up a commission to ascertain the views of the people of North Borneo and Sarawak and in the light of its assessment to make recommendations. The joint statement also announced that the views of the Sultan of Brunei were being sought.

The Commission was appointed in January, 1962, and comprised a Chairman (Lord Cobbold), two members nominated by the British Government and two members nominated by the Malayan Government. The Commission visited North Borneo and Sarawak in February, 1962, and submitted its report (Cmnd. 1794) to the two Governments in June. The two Governments after considering the report conferred in London in July. In a joint public statement on the 1st August it was announced that they had decided in principle that, subject to the necessary legislation, the proposed federation should be brought into being by the 31st August, 1963.
The two Governments proposed to conclude a formal agreement which, among other things, would provide for:—
(a) the transfer of sovereignty over North Borneo, Sarawak and Singapore by the 31st August, 1963;
(b) provisions governing the relationship between Singapore and the new federation, as agreed between the Governments of Malaya and Singapore;
(c) defence arrangements as set out in the joint statement by the British and Malayan Governments dated the 22nd November, 1961, Cmnd. 1563;

(d) detailed constitutional arrangements, including safeguards for the special interests of North Borneo and Sarawak, to be drawn up after consultation with the legislatures of the two territories. (These safeguards would cover such matters as religious freedom, education, representation in the Federal Parliament, the position of the indigenous races, control of immigration, citizenship and the State constitutions.)

An Inter-Governmental Committee under the Chairmanship of Lord Lansdowne was established in Jesselton, North Borneo, on which the British, Malayan, North Borneo and Sarawak Governments were represented, to work out the future constitutional arrangements and the form of the necessary safeguards referred to at (d) above.

The British and Malayan Governments informed the Sultan of Brunei of the agreement they had reached and made it clear that they would welcome the inclusion of the State of Brunei in the new federation. The Brunei Legislative Council accepted Malaysia in principle subject to the negotiation of satisfactory terms of merger.
The unanimous Report of the Inter-Governmental Committee was published in February, 1963 (Cmnd. 1954), and the Committee's recommendations were accepted by all the Governments concerned.

Following meetings in London of representatives of the Governments concerned an agreement was signed on the 9th July, 1963, to form a Federation to be called Malaysia on the 31st August, 1963, comprising the States of the Federation of Malaya, the State of Singapore and the Colonies of North Borneo (to be known as Sabah) and Sarawak. The Sultan of Brunei decided that Brunei would not join Malaysia for the present.

The Malaysia Agreement was published as a Command Paper on the 11th July, 1963, (Cmnd. 2094). Under the Agreement, the Federation of Malaya undertook to secure in the Malayan Parliament the enactment of a measure to amend the Malayan Constitution to provide for the admission of the new States. Similarly, the British Government undertook to submit Orders in Council before Malaysia Day to give the force of law to the Constitutions of Sabah, Sarawak and Singapore. Britain also undertook to secure the enactment of legislation providing for the relinquishment of sovereignty over North Borneo, Sarawak and Singapore on Malaysia Day. These measures were duly taken. However, as a result of an Agreement between the Heads of the Governments of Malaya, the Philippines and Indonesia at a meeting in Manila at the beginning of August, the Secretary General of the United Nations, with the co-operation of the British, North Borneo and Sarawak Governments, sent a team of representatives to North Borneo and Sarawak later that month to report whether the people truly wished to enter Malaysia. In consequence it was announced on the 28th August that the Governments of Britain, Malaya, North Borneo and Sarawak had agreed that Malaysia Day would be postponed from the 31st August to the 16th September, 1963. The Secretary-General's report, confirming the wish of the majority of the people of North Borneo and Sarawak to enter Malaysia, was published on the 14th September and Malaysia was duly formed on the 16th September.
The Secretary of State for Commonwealth Relations is the channel of communication between the British Government and the Government of Malaysia.

MAURITIUS
MAURITIUS
Area

The area of Mauritius is approximately 720 square miles, that of Rodrigues 40 square miles and the area of the Lesser Dependencies, viz., the Chagos Archipelago (the largest islet is Diego Garcia), Agalega and the Cargados Carajos or St. Brandon Isles, approximately 47½ square miles.
Population

The estimated population at 31st December 1962 was: Mauritius 692,350, Rodrigues 18,650, Lesser Dependencies 1,100; divided approximately into: (1) general population, comprising Europeans and descendants of Europeans and people of African and mixed origin, 31%; (2) Indo-Mauritians, made up of immigrants from the Indian subcontinent and their descendants, 66%; and Chinese, consisting of immigrants from China and their descendants, 3%.
Principal Towns
At the *31st December 1962 the estimated population of the principal towns was:—

Port Louis (capital) .. 90,375
Curepipe (new limits) .. 35,900
Beau Bassin-Rose Hill .. 38,250
Quatre Bornes .. 28,775
* Based on the 1962 Population Census.
Geographical Features

The island lies in the Indian Ocean, 550 miles east of Madagascar. It is chiefly of volcanic formation but almost entirely surrounded by coral reefs. There are several small chains of mountains, the principal peaks reaching about 2,700 feet. There are numerous rivers and streams; none of them are navigable beyond a short distance from the sea. Some of the larger waterfalls have been harnessed to supply hydro-electric power. The land rises gradually from the coasts and the centre of the island is composed chiefly of a fertile plateau varying in altitude from 1,000 feet to 2,000 feet. Remnants of the native forests of the island cover some 15,000 acres.
Climate

The south-east trade winds blow during most of the year and the climate is generally humid. The mean temperature varies from 23°C. at sea level to 19°C. at 2,000 feet. The highest and lowest values ever recorded at Pamplemousses at 180 feet are 35°C. and 10°C. and at Vacoas, at 1,394 feet on the western slopes, are 37°C. and 8°C.

Rainfall differs considerably according to the height above sea level. On the highest part of the central plateau an average of 200 inches falls each year but amounts decrease rapidly to 60 and 35 inches on the windward and leeward coasts. There is no marked dry season but in the three months from September to November rainfall is about one quarter of the fall in the three wettest months, January to March.

Tropical cyclones may occur between September and May but by far the greatest frequency is in January and February; March and April are also important months owing to the greater susceptibility to damage of the grown sugar cane. In about 10% of all years, mean wind speed over an hour reaches 50 m.p.h. and gusts 100 m.p.h.; gusts of 80 m.p.h. may be expected about 25 times every century.

History

Mauritius was probably first visited by Arab sailors and by Malays during the Middle Ages. During the early sixteenth century Portuguese sailors visited the island several times and the first European to discover it is believed to have been Domingos Fernandez. The Portuguese used it merely as a port of call without making any settlement. The first settlers were the Dutch who landed in 1598 and gave the island the name of Mauritius in honour of their ruler, Prince Maurice of Nassau. Settlements were established from 1638 onwards but did not prosper and the island was abandoned in 1710. In 1715 the island was formally taken possession of by the French. A small first contingent of colonists was sent in 1721 from the neighbouring island of Réunion (then called Bourbon), but it was not actually occupied until 1722 when a small party of colonists was sent out on behalf of the French East India Company. The Island was then named Isle de France. From 1722 till about 1767 it was governed by that Company. From 1767 to 1810, apart from the brief period of independence under the Colonial Assembly during the French Revolution, it was in charge of officials appointed by the French Government.

During the long war between England and France, French men-of-war and privateers based on the Isle de France were a source of great mischief to the English merchant vessels. The British Government decided to capture the island and in 1810 a strong British expedition was eventually successful. The former name of Mauritius was then restored to it, and, with its dependencies, including Rodrigues, it was ceded to Great Britain by the Treaty of Paris in 1814.
Constitution

The Government is vested in a Governor with a Council of Ministers and a Legislative Assembly. The Mauritius (Constitution) Order 1964 provides for a Legislative Assembly consisting of the Speaker (elected by the Assembly from among its members, other than members who are members of the Council of Ministers or Parliamentary Secretaries) one ex-officio member (the Chief Secretary), 40 elected members and such nominated members not exceeding 15 in number as the Governor may appoint.
The Colony is divided into 40 single-member constituencies and every person (male or female) has the right to vote who:—
(a) is a British subject of the age of 21 years or upwards; and
(b) has resided in the Colony for a period of at least two years immediately before the date of registration or is domiciled in the Colony and is residing therein at that date; and
(c) has resided in the constituency in which he is entitled to be registered as a voter for a period of at least six months immediately before the date of registration.
Digitized by Google
THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST

The Council of Ministers consists of the Premier, the Chief Secretary and not less than ten nor more than thirteen appointed members. The Governor acting in his discretion appoints as Premier a member of the Legislative Assembly who appears to the Governor likely to command the support of the majority of members of that Assembly. The appointed members of the Council are persons who are elected or nominated members of the Legislative Assembly and are appointed by the Governor after consultation with the Premier. The Governor may, after consultation with the Premier, appoint such persons from among the elected or nominated members of the Legislative Assembly as he may deem expedient to be Parliamentary Secretaries. The members of the Council and the Parliamentary Secretaries are responsible to the Governor and to the Legislative Assembly for all matters affecting the department with which they are associated.
Land Policy

Roughly one-sixth of the area of the island is owned by the Crown and consists of (a) Crown lands proper made up of unconceded lands and lands re-acquired by the Crown, and (b) "Pas Geometriques" being reserved land lying on the sea coast between the high water mark in spring tides and a line drawn parallel to the general contour of the coast at a distance of 81.21 metres. Alienable Crown lands may be sold only by public auction, except in the case of lands required for religious, charitable or educational purposes, or for public utility, when a nominal price is charged. The deeds of sale do not alienate the right of the Crown in respect of any mines of precious stones or metals or any layer of mineral oil or the right of the Crown of entering the property for the purpose of mining or of boring and collecting mineral oil, but no other restrictive clause is inserted in the deed of conveyance. Crown lands and "Pas Geometriques" may be leased by private contract or public auction.
The remaining five-sixths of the area of the island are privately owned lands of which the holders are free to dispose as they think fit.
