Education

On 31st October 1962 there were 834 educational establishments in Mauritius. Of this total, 367 were pre-primary schools, 257 primary schools, 105 secondary, two post-secondary and two Central Schools, one Technical Institute and Trade Centre, with a total of 165,093 pupils. The Government institutions comprised 124 primary schools, two central schools, four secondary schools, one training college for teachers, one post-secondary College of Agriculture and one Technical Institute. Thirteen secondary and 75 primary schools within the total given were State-aided; eight of the State-aided secondary schools had primary sections. There were also a number of private day and evening schools teaching such subjects as the Koran, Urdu and Hindi. The Government awarded six scholarships in 1962 for higher education overseas. Mauritian students overseas numbered 1,080, of whom 855 were in the United Kingdom and Republic of Ireland Universities, 100 in France and 95 in India and Pakistan, ten in the United States and Canada, eight in Switzerland and twelve in other countries.

Adult education courses provide for post-primary and post-secondary education in Science, Handwork, Art, Typewriting, Book-keeping, Secretarial Training, "Builders Quantities", Economics, Local Government, Telecommunications Mathematics, Telecommunications Practice, Technical Drawing, English Language and Electrical Engineering courses for apprentices.

In the financial year 1962–63 expenditure on education is estimated at Rs. 22·6 million (excluding capital expenditure on new buildings and other development works which cost Rs.2·2 million). In 1935 expenditure was Rs.1,347,201.
Libraries and Museums

There are seven Libraries :—(i) Police Library, Port Louis, (ii) Municipality of Port Louis Library, (iii) Town Council of Beau Bassin/Rose Hill Library, (iv) Carnegie Library, Curepipe, (v) Mauritius Institute Library, Port Louis, (vi) British Council Library, Rose Hill, (vii) Urban Council of Quatre Bornes Library.
The Natural History Museum, Port Louis, and the Historical Museum, Mahebourg, are both run
by the Mauritius Institute. A small art gallery is also annexed to the Port Louis Museum.
Health
The vaccination of babies with D.P.T. Polio (Quadruple Vaccine) from Canada, inaugurated in 1960, has become a regular feature.
Alterations and improvements to hospitals have continued throughout the year.
The infantile mortality rate (60·1), and the death rate (9·3) are the lowest on record.
Help from the W.H.O. continued in respect of the scheme for the eradication of Malaria and Tuberculosis.

The health inspectorate works from health offices of which there are 13 throughout the island. The total number of beds available in 1962 was 1,566 in general hospitals, of which 205 were in Orthopaedic wards, 141 in Maternity wards and 213 in Tuberculosis wards. There were 780 beds at the Mental Hospital, 61 at the Leper Asylum, 46 in the Prison hospitals and 55 at the Emergency T.B. Hospital. In addition, 25 sugar estates provide each a small hospital with a total of 500 beds for the sole benefit of their employees. There were also five private nursing homes, 50 static dispensaries and out-patient departments of hospitals (excluding two out-patient departments of hospitals and two dispensaries in the prisons) and four mobile dental clinics. The Maternal and Infant Welfare Service provides 29 static centres and one mobile ante-natal clinic. Admissions to hospitals numbered 37,054; attendances at static dispensaries numbered 1,260,307 and at mobile dispensaries 118,817.
The expenditure on medical services during the financial year 1961–62 amounted to Rs.17,426,995·94 against an actual expenditure of Rs.1,964,369 in 1929–30.
Communications

The airport (Plaisance) is operated and managed by the Colonial Government. Air France with "Super Constellation" aircraft operates a scheduled service thrice weekly between Reunion and Madagascar whence connections to Europe and other points can be made. British Overseas Airways Corporation provide a weekly Comet IV service between London and Mauritius via Rome—Khartoum—Nairobi. A weekly service between Australia and South Africa is operated on alternate weeks by Quantas Empire Airways with "Electra" and South African Airways with DC7B aircraft, the route being Sydney—Melbourne—Perth—Cocos—Mauritius—Johannesburg. (S.A.A. do not serve the first two points.) Rhodesian Air Services operate a fortnightly direct service between Salisbury and Mauritius with DC 4 aircraft. No internal services exist.
The railway carried 470,365 metric tons of freight in 1961–62. Track mileage totalled 68 miles on 30th June, 1962.

There are in the Colony about 525·5 miles of main roads, 232 miles of branch roads and 63 miles of streets in villages and extra-urban areas; 543·5 miles of main and branch roads, streets in villages and extra urban areas have been asphalted.

Port Louis, the capital of the island, is also the only port; it can accommodate ten ocean-going vessels at any one time and four small vessels. Regular cargo services are provided from the United Kingdom by the British and Commonwealth Shipping Company and from continental
Digitized by Google
THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST

ports by the French companies Messageries Maritimes Limited and Nouvelle Cie. Havraise Peninsulaire; the Scindia Steamships Company provide a regular cargo service from Bombay and the O.S.K. Line provide a regular monthly cargo service from Japan and the Far East. Passenger services from the United Kingdom direct to Mauritius are infrequent; the French vessels direct from Marseilles to Mauritius provide the better service. The Dutch Royal Interocean Lines vessels call at Port Louis regularly on their Far East-South Africa-South America service, to and from South Africa. During 1962, 519 vessels entered Port Louis giving a total of 1,543,336 net tons.
Broadcasting

Government runs the Mauritius Broadcasting Service, which has a monopoly in the Colony. The station at Malherbes operates on 439 metres, 683 kc/s medium wave and 30.90 metres and 61.86 metres, 9,710 and 4,850 kc/s with powers of 10 kW in both cases. In June 1963 there were 53,452 wireless licences in force in the Colony with an estimate of five listeners per receiver.
Principal Occupations

Cane plantation and sugar manufacture give employment to approximately 5,300 skilled and 54,000 semi-skilled and unskilled workers. Other Agricultural industries are cultivation of tea, employing some 2,100 workers; tobacco 1,300; aloe fibre 450. About 6,300 workers are in known employment in manufacturing, 4,700 in construction, 2,600 in electricity services and communications, and 22,500 in services, including central and local government but not domestic and hotel services.
In 1962 there were 75 registered trade unions including ten associations of employers and two federations of trade unions with 36 members. Total membership of the employees' unions was 39,526.
Main Crops and Products

The main crops are sugar-cane, "aloe" ( Furcraea ) fibre, tea and tobacco. Sugar is produced in 23 factories which have been to a large measure re-equipped with up-to-date machinery in the recent years. Practically all the aloe fibre produced is spun locally and turned into bags for the sugar industry. Tea is being encouraged and the production was roughly 2,800,000 lbs. in 1962, about 1,000,000 lbs. of which were sold locally and the rest exported.

The tobacco produced is blended with imported leaves in the proportion of three to one for the manufacture of cigarettes for local consumption. Of the by-products of sugar cane 110,253 metric tons of molasses were exported and 16,534 hectolitres of high strength alcohol were produced.

The Livestock population is estimated to be roughly: 'creole' milch breed cattle, 44,000; Zebu type, beef herd cattle, 1,500; goats, 54,000; pigs, 5,000; sheep, 100; rabbits, 8,000; poultry and other birds, 415,000.
The catch of fish in territorial waters is approximately 2,000 tons a year of which 1,500 are landed at Controlled Stations.
Trade
In 1938, 34.4% of total imports came from the United Kingdom, 41.4% from other Commonwealth countries and 24.2% from foreign countries. The corresponding figures in 1962 were 27.9%, 42.2% and 29.9%.
In 1938, 96.9% of total exports went to the United Kingdom, 1.6% to other Commonwealth countries and 1.5% to foreign countries. The corresponding figures in 1962 were 83.8%, 9.4% and 6.8%.

Marketing and Co-operative Organizations

The whole of the sugar produced is marketed by the Mauritius Sugar Syndicate. In 1962 the exports of sugar totalled 492,296 long tons. On the 28th February 1962 there were 330 registered co-operative societies with a membership of 32,042, paid-up share capital amounting to Rs.2,050,925 and General Reserves totalling Rs.3,264,273. The turnover in goods and services for the year was Rs.40,244,230, including Rs.14,393,981 worth of sugar sold through 153 credit societies and Rs.3,251,491 as compensation from Cyclone and Drought Insurance and Rs.50,888 worth of green tea leaves sold through six marketing societies and Rs.63,998 worth of fish sold through six fishermen's societies. MONTSERRAT
Area and Geographical Features

The area of the island is about 32 square miles. It is entirely volcanic, very mountainous and comprises three main hill masses—Silver Hill in the north (1,285 feet), Central Hills (2,450 feet) and Soufrière Hills (Chance Peak 3,002 feet) in the south. Forest and woodland cover about 10 square miles in the two main ranges and streams are plentiful. The northern hills, however, are almost without trees and in that part of the island there is little cultivation.
Population
The population numbered 12,108 at the 1960 census, and was estimated to be 12,945 at the end of 1962.
Principal Town
Plymouth, the only town, had a population of 1,911 at the 1960 census.
History

The island was colonized in 1632 when a number of Irishmen of Sir Thomas Warner's company made their homes there. It was captured by the French in 1667, restored to England at the Peace of Breda later in the same year, capitulated to the French again in 1782 and was finally ceded to Britain in 1783.
Constitution
The constitutional arrangements are the same as for Antigua (see page 64) except for the following differences:—

(i) In Executive Council there are four unofficial members (the Chief Minister, two other Ministers and a Member without Portfolio) and two official members (the Crown Attorney and the Financial Secretary). These three Ministers are appointed from the elected members of the Legislative Council.
(ii) The present composition of the Legislative Council is the Administrator, who presides, two official members, one nominated unofficial member and seven elected members.

(iii) Montserrat shares a joint Police Force with Antigua and the Virgin Islands. The head of this joint Force is appointed by the Administrator of Antigua acting in his discretion and gazetted ranks of the Force are appointed by the Administrator of Antigua after consultation with the Police Service Commission.
Land Policy
Crown land comprises the Government Land Settlement, where occupation is confined to an annual lease, and a small area acquired for public purposes. All other land is privately owned.
Taxation
The following forms of direct taxation are in force:—
Income Tax (ranging from 6d. to 15s. per £ of chargeable income; companies 6s. 8d. per £ of chargeable income).
Land and House Tax.
Motor Vehicles Tax.
Bicycles Tax.
General Taxes.
The other main forms of taxation are import and export and excise duties.

Development Plans
For the period 1960–64 an allocation of £200,000 was made. The 1960–64 Development Plan includes soil conservation, improvement and expansion of the livestock industry, land

MONTSERRAT : NORTH BORNEO

clearing, vegetable production and marketing, banana and sugar-cane cultivation, fisheries development, forestry, co-operative development, public works (for example, road construction), social services (for example, health and hospital improvement, sanitation, housing and water supplies). For the triennium 1963–66 a further £75,000 has been allocated and a new development plan is being prepared.
Education

Primary and secondary education are both controlled by Government. There are 12 Government elementary schools, one aided and one unaided denominational school and two private elementary schools. There is one Government secondary school preparing students up to the Higher School Certificate and a preparatory private school for children between the ages of 5 and 12 years. At the end of 1962 3,025 children were enrolled in Government, aided and private primary schools, with an average attendance of 91%. The enrolment of the secondary school at the end of the same period was 186.
Government expenditure on the Education Department in 1962 was $251,145.
Library
The Montserrat Public Library has a membership of 1,583. It has adult, juvenile and reference sections with a total stock of 11,579 books.
Health

Expenditure on medical and allied services in 1962 was $229,734. There were 69 beds available at the Glendon Hospital and in-patients totalled 786. There were three health centres and eight outposts at which antenatal and infant welfare clinics were held.
Communications
There are three main public roads extending over 60 miles and about 58 miles of secondary unsurfaced roads.

Blackburne airfield was opened in 1956. The runway is 3,600 feet long with clear approaches and take-off. Prior notification of arrival is required in order that Customs and other facilities may be provided.

Ships of the Harrison Steamship Company call regularly. The island is linked with other islands of the Leewards group and with Dominica and Barbados, by a locally owned vessel. The West Indies Shipping Corporation also maintains a regular inter-island service by two motor vessels.
Broadcasting
The local broadcasting service is Government-owned. The transmitter (250W, 1,550 k/cs.) and the studio are located at the Botanical Gardens.
Principal Occupations

The main occupation is agriculture, chiefly the growing of bananas and sea island cotton. Increased attention is being given to the cultivation of fruit and vegetables for local consumption and export.

There are three employees' trades unions in the Colony: The Trades and Labour Union (membership 312); The Seamen's and Waterfront Workers' Union (membership 200); and The Union of Teachers (membership 78).
Main Crops and Products
The main crops are sea island cotton, bananas, limes, sugar-cane, sweet potatoes and tomatoes.

THE FEDERATION OF RHODESIA & NYASALAND
History and Constitution

A short account of the events leading up to the establishment of the Federation in September, 1953, and a summary of the Federal Constitution, are to be found in the 1954 and 1955 editions of the Colonial Office List . In January, 1962, the British Government granted the right of secession from the Federation to Nyasaland, and in April, 1963, to Northern Rhodesia and Southern Rhodesia. A conference to discuss the problems involved in dissolution was held at Victoria Falls in June, 1963, and dissolution took place on 31st December, 1963.

NORTHERN RHODESIA
(See also section on The Federation of Rhodesia and Nyasaland and page 6).
Area
The Protectorate of Northern Rhodesia lies on the plateau of Central Africa. Its area is 288,130 square miles, including more than 3,000 square miles of inland water.

Geographical Features

With the exception of the valleys of the Zambezi, the Luapula, the Kafue and the Luangwa Rivers the greater part of Northern Rhodesia has a flat to rolling topography with elevations varying from 3,000–5,000 feet above sea level, rising in the north-eastern districts to occasional altitudes of over 5,000 feet. The highest group of hills is the Muchinga Mountains which form part of the great escarpment running down the western edge of the Luangwa River Valley, and of these, individual peaks rise to about 8,000 feet. There are three great natural lakes: Lake Bangweulu in the northern district and Lakes Mweru and Tanganyika on the northern boundary of the Territory. Lake Bangweulu and its swamps cover an area of about 3,800 square miles. On the southern boundary lie the Victoria Falls, the largest waterfall in the world and, Lake Kariba, the largest man-made lake in the world; and on the northern boundary the second highest waterfall in Africa, the Kalambo Falls, 726 feet high. A large proportion of the Territory is covered with deciduous woodlands.
Climate

There are three distinct seasons—the rainy season from November until April, the winter season from about May to September and the hot season between September and November. The rainfall averages from 25 inches a year in the southern areas to over 50 inches in the north. In the hot season day temperatures may run from 80°–100° but at night there is a very distinct drop in the temperature.
History

Two early travellers in Northern Rhodesia deserve notice. The first was the Portuguese governor of Sena, Dr. de Lacerda, who led an expedition to Chief Kasembe's capital on the eastern shores of Lake Mweru in 1798. Though he died before reaching his goal, his diaries provide the first authoritative account of what is now north-eastern Rhodesia. The second was Dr. Livingstone who travelled through Barotseland to

NORTHERN RHODESIA

Angola in 1851 and returned via Tete to Portuguese East Africa in 1855, discovering the Victoria Falls en route . He died at Chitambo, south of Lake Bangweulu, in May 1873. In the early part of the nineteenth century the Territory, sparsely occupied by Bantu tribes, was invaded by the Arabs from the north, the Ngoni branch of the Zulus from the south and the Kalolo who migrated from Basutoland. The Kalolo conquered what is now Barotseland but the indigenous Lozi later revolted and under Lewanika imposed their rule again.

In 1891 Lewanika was informed that the protection of Her Majesty's Government had been extended to his country as he had requested that it should be and on 17th October 1900 the Barotse Concession was signed by him and his indunas and representatives of the Chartered Company. The concession was confirmed in due course by the Secretary of State for the Colonies, and under its terms the Company acquired certain trading and mineral rights over the whole of Lewanika's dominion, while the paramount chief was to receive, among other advantages, an annual subsidy.

The first task of the administration was the eradication of the slave trade conducted by the Arabs. In 1898 the last caravan was intercepted and the slaves freed and by 1906 Lewanika had agreed to the emancipation of the conquered tribes, whose status had been a mild form of slavery.

Before 1899 the whole Territory had been vaguely included in the Charter granted to the British South Africa Company but in that year the Barotseland-North-Western Rhodesia Order in Council placed the Company's administration of the western portion of the country on a firm basis. It was closely followed by the North-Eastern Rhodesia Order in Council of 1900 which had a similar effect. The two territories were amalgamated in 1911 under the designation of Northern Rhodesia and the administration of the Company (subject to the exercise of certain powers of control by the Crown) continued until 1924. The capital was at Livingstone. In that year the administration of the Territory was assumed by the Crown in terms of a settlement arrived at between the Crown and the Company and the first Governor, Sir Herbert Stanley, was appointed on 1st April 1924. In 1935 the capital was moved to Lusaka.

From September, 1953, the Protectorate was one of the three territories comprised in the Federation of Rhodesia and Nyasaland ( see page 144). This Federation was dissolved on December 31st, 1963. During the ten-year period of the Federation certain Departments and services were taken over by the Federal Government. These included: Audit, Civil Aviation, Military, Prisons, Customs, Income Tax, Posts and Telegraphs, European Education, Health and European Agriculture. During this period an independent Corporation was also set up to control Broadcasting.
Constitution
A new Constitution for Northern Rhodesia under which the territory will proceed to internal self-government came into effect on 1st January 1964.

The Governor retains responsibility on behalf of Her Majesty's Government for defence and external affairs and can reserve for the signification of Her Majesty's pleasure any Bill which in his view is inconsistent with any obligations imposed on Her Majesty by any treaty, convention or
agreement with any country or international organisation, or is likely to prejudice the royal prerogative.
The Governor is assisted by a Deputy Governor appointed by the Governor on Her Majesty's instructions.

The Executive Council has been replaced by a Cabinet consisting of a Prime Minister and 13 other ministers holding portfolios assigned by the Governor in accordance with the advice of the Prime Minister who presides over the Cabinet. All Cabinet papers and minutes of Cabinet meetings are circulated to the Governor.
The Attorney General continues to be the official legal adviser to the Government, but is not a minister or a member of the Cabinet.
Parliamentary Secretaries are appointed by the Governor on the advice of the Prime Minister.

The Legislative Assembly totals 75 members, 65 elected in main roll (African) constituencies and 10 in reserved roll (European) constituencies. (Asians and coloured persons were allowed to opt on which roll they wished to vote.) Members of any race were eligible to stand as candidates in all constituencies providing they possessed the necessary qualifications to be a voter on either roll.
There is a House of Chiefs.
The Governor retains responsibility for public order and for the use and operational control of the police force, but he may delegate such responsibilities if he thinks fit.

The Constitution contains a Bill of Rights which sets out the fundamental rights and freedoms of the individual and provides protection from discrimination on grounds of race. There is a Constitutional Council whose functions will include that of considering whether Bills referred to it by Members of the Legislature are inconsistent with the Bill of Rights.

The Constitution provides for the establishment of a High Court and of a Court of Appeal. A Judicial Service Commission deals with appointment, disciplinary control and removal from office of the magistracy, and also advises the Governor on the appointment of Judges.

The Public Service Commission and the Police Service Commission will become fully responsible during the course of 1964 for appointments, disciplinary control and removal from office of members of the civil service and the senior members of the police.
At the general election on 20th January 1964, there were 1,401,325 registered voters of whom 1,377,349 were registered on the main roll, and 23,976 on the reserved roll.

Land Policy

Land in the Territory, apart from Barotseland, is divided into (i) Native Reserves vested in perpetuity in the Secretary of State in trust for the African population; (ii) Native Trust Land also vested in the Secretary of State for the benefit of Africans but which may on certain conditions be temporarily alienated to non-natives; (iii) Crown Land. Crown Land in the City of Lusaka and in the Municipal Townships is alienated by Head Lease to the Local Authority which in turn sub-lets to an approved applicant. Leaseholders may
Digitized by Google
THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST

obtain freehold on completion of the development required under their leases. Crown Land in the smaller townships is alienated by direct lease from the Crown but subject to the recommendation of the Local Authority. Freehold conversion is not obtainable. Crown Land for agricultural purposes is alienated on leasehold tenure to applicants who prove that they have the necessary finance and farming experience to make full use of the land. Under the provisions of the Agricultural Lands Ordinance the lessee of declared agricultural land may obtain a conditional freehold title to his farm on the fulfilment of the conditions laid down by the Ordinance. There is in certain areas freehold land which was granted in the early days.
Taxation
At the time of going to press responsibility for the assessment and collection of income tax and customs and excise duties is reverting to the Northern Rhodesia Government.
Public Finance
The dissolution of the Federation of Rhodesia and Nyasaland on December 31, 1963, will result in changes in the Territory's financial responsibilities and arrangements.

The main sources of revenue in the financial year ended the 30th June, 1963, were the payments received from the Federal Government, representing 18% of the collections within the Federation on account of taxes on income, and the Territorial Surcharge on Company Income Tax assessments collected by the Federal Government on behalf of the Northern Rhodesia Government. These two taxes represented 56.2% of the actual revenue for 1962-63 and a further 12.2% was derived from mineral royalties. Customs and Excise duties were collected by the Federal Government and, in accordance with the existing fiscal arrangements, these were not shared with the Territorial Governments of the Federation.

During the 1962-63 financial year, the Federal Government continued to have full financial responsibility for the Departments and services it had taken over following the establishment of the Federation of Rhodesia and Nyasaland on the 23rd October, 1953. The principal Departments and services taken over were as follows:—
Audit Broadcasting Civil Aviation Customs and Excise European Agriculture European Education Health Income Tax Military Posts and Telegraphs Prisons.
With the dissolution of the Federation, the financial responsibility for these Departments and services, together with their attendant revenues, revert to the Northern Rhodesia Government.

Currency

Up to the 23rd April 1957 the currency normally used was issued by the Southern Rhodesia Currency Board and was common to Northern Rhodesia and Nyasaland. The denominations were 10s., £1, £5 and £10 notes, cupro-nickel or copper halfpennies and pennies and cupro-nickel or silver threepenny pieces, sixpences, shillings, florins and half-crowns.

On the 23rd April 1957 however, the Bank of Rhodesia and Nyasaland, which took over the assets of the Central Africa Currency Board on the 1st April 1956 commenced the issue of new currency notes of the denominations of 10s., £1, £5 and £10. The two types of currency now circulate together, but the new will gradually replace the old as the latter becomes unfit for circulation. As from the 1st January 1955 British coins ceased to be legal tender in Northern Rhodesia and Nyasaland.

Education
Non-African education and all higher education became the responsibility of the Federal Government on 1st July 1954 and only reverted to the Territorial Government at the end of 1963.

In July 1961 there were 58 Government schools and 12 Government-aided private schools providing education for European children. Ten of the Government schools and one private school provided education up to the Cambridge School Certificate standard.

The enrolment in European Government schools was 15,194; in Government-aided private schools, 2,302. Technical education for apprentices is provided at Broken Hill, Lusaka and Livingstone. Government assistance in the form of scholarships, bursaries, grants and loans is available under certain conditions for higher education. European, Asian and Coloured students from Northern Rhodesia attend the Teachers' Training College at Heany, near Bulawayo, and the University College of Rhodesia and Nyasaland in Salisbury.
There were 13 Government primary and secondary schools and one Government-aided private school for Asian and Coloured children. The total enrolment of Asians was 1,712; and of Coloureds, 525.

No exact figures are available for the number of African children in each age group. For the first time a territorial population census was carried out in 1963, but the final figures are not yet available. Of the primary school age group (7–14 years inclusive) the total number in schools was 348,342. There are 8,581 pupils pursuing secondary or undergoing some form of vocational training at schools or colleges aided or maintained by the Ministry of African Education.

Rhodes-Livingstone Institute, Museum, etc.

The Institute, with headquarters at Lusaka, has conducted social research in British Central Africa since 1938. While emphasis is placed on the study of the reaction of African peoples to modern social and economic conditions, research into the problems of the immigrant races is also undertaken. Since 1st April 1962 the Institute has been incorporated into the University College of Rhodesia and Nyasaland.

The Rhodes-Livingstone Museum at Livingstone is Northern Rhodesia's National Museum and tells the story of the Territory from the Stone Age to modern times. The library contains approximately 6,000 volumes which are available to members of the Northern Rhodesia Society.
The aims of the Northern Rhodesia Society are to promote the study of ethnology, history, geography, natural history and kindred sciences in their relation to Northern Rhodesia.
Health
Arising from the dissolution of the Federation of Rhodesia and Nyasaland, the former Federal Health Services reverted to the control of the Northern Rhodesia Government on 1st December, 1963.
Government medical facilities in the territory include two central hospitals situated at Lusaka and Kitwe and General Hospitals at Livingstone,
Digitized by Google
THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST
Broken Hill and Ndola, offering facilities for all races.
There are also seventeen district hospitals, the majority of which have a medical officer in charge and 33 rural hospitals.
There is a mental hospital accommodating 164 patients.
In addition to the hospital facilities described above, there are 140 rural health centres and 33 urban clinics.
During 1962 a total of 180,042 patients were admitted to Federal Government hospitals and there were 9,487,052 out-patient attendances at Government institutions.
Extensions to the Mental Hospital are being planned and active consideration is being given to the training of state registered nurses in the Llewellin Central Hospital, Kitwe.
Communications

During 1954 the Federal Government assumed responsibility for civil aviation, inter-territorial main roads and certain other aspects of communications. These responsibilities reverted to the Northern Rhodesia Government at the end of 1963.

There are main airports at Livingstone, Lusaka and Ndola with bituminous surfaced runways, and one at Kasama with gravel runway, all of which lie on the north and south corridor through the Territory. Subsidiary airfields serve most of the outlying districts.

The Rhodesia Railways line runs north and south from the Congo border (near Sakania) to the Victoria Falls. Branch lines serve the copper mines from Ndola. The total length of permanent way is 655 miles.
The total mileage of maintained roads in the Territory at the 31st December 1962 was 21,129.8 made up as follows:—

In 1962 the approximate number of cattle owned by Europeans was 214,704. African owned livestock totalled 1,056,188 cattle, 175,782 sheep and goats and 61,251 pigs. More than 52,640 head of local cattle were slaughtered by licensed butcheries during the year.

The fishing industry is primarily an African concern and plays an important part in the economy of rural areas. Lakes Bangweulu and Mweru and the rivers Kafue, Luapula and Zambezi provide large quantities of fish, most of which is transported to line-of-rail centres, either dry or preserved on ice. Fishing is being stimulated on the Southern Sector of Lakes Tanganyika and Kariba.
Output of sawlogs, poles and firewood amounted to 212,598 tons in 1962.

A large variety of manufactured goods are now produced in the Territory. Main manufactures are: Cement products, tiles, steel and wood furniture, steel finishing work, beer, mineral waters, metal castings and nuts and bolts, sugar, plastics, soap, copperware, nails, dry ice, pottery, sweets, cigarettes, edible oils and fats, and boats.
Trade

Internal trading continues to improve. Higher wages, especially on the Copperbelt, have raised the purchasing power of Africans and this is reflected in increased turnover. The agricultural industry has, overall, experienced favourable conditions and sales of agricultural implements are higher. Exports of such items as blankets and batteries to neighbouring countries have been maintained.

The mainstay of the economy, however, continues to be the primary industries of mining and agriculture, and the principal exports, copper, maize and tobacco. The amount of copper produced or made available for sale is regulated in order to help balance the world supply and demand position of this commodity.

Local industrial products include steel and metal work, cement, soap, furniture, oxygen and acetylene, clothing and blankets, boat building, plastics, and car batteries. There are also in operation an iron-foundry, saw-mills, a recently established pottery, a brewery, a sweet factory, and a sugar refinery. New construction and development continue at a high rate in the mining industry, and Government has made considerable provision in its £30 million Development Plan to assist the development of agriculture in rural areas from a subsistence basis towards commercial operation in a cash economy.
Co-operative and Marketing Organizations

Co-operative Societies have again enjoyed a successful year in 1962 and the agricultural co-operative marketing sector has continued to progress in spite of a fall in both the price of maize and the export price of F.A.Q. groundnuts. This fall in prices is reflected in the reduced turnover of £7,219,958 compared with £8,453,252 in 1961.

There were ten dissolutions during the year and nine new registrations, leaving a total of 227 societies with a European and African aggregate membership of 35,619. Paid up share capital and deposits amounted to £579,759.

Co-operative Marketing organisations handled 841,646 bags of maize (200 lb. bags), 165,365 bags of groundnuts (180 lb. bags) and 3,744 bags of other produce (200 lb. bags) plus 3,447 lbs. of cotton at a total value of £2,348,023. In addition 9,081,486 lbs. of tobacco was marketed at a value of £1,571,082.
Societies also continued to handle milk, cheese, butter, cream, eggs, coffee and cattle.

Geographical Features
Nyasaland may be divided into the following four main zones :
(a) The dominant feature of the deep trough-like depression which is part of the Great Rift Valley of Eastern Africa and the greater part of which is occupied by Lake Nyasa. The
water level is about 1,550 feet above sea level. The deepest part of the Lake is near its northern end and is about 2,300 feet.
(b) The high tableland, separating Lake Nyasa from the basin of the Luangwa River, ranging from an altitude of 4,500 feet in the Central Plateau to 8,000 feet in the Nyika Plateau in the north.

(c) The Southern or Shire Highlands Plateau, bounded on the west by the Shire River and on the south-east by the Ruo River, having a general elevation of 2,000–3,500 feet and rising to the mountain masses of Mlanje (10,000 feet) and Zomba (7,000 feet).
(d) The lowlands of the Lower River Shire Basin in the extreme south. This area contains much marshland and is liable to extensive flooding.
Three smaller and shallow lakes, Lakes Malombe, Chilwa and Chiuta lie to the south of Lake Nyasa.

History

The modern history of Nyasaland may be said to have begun with the discovery by David Livingstone of Lake Nyasa in 1859. British interest in the area was sustained by various missionary societies including the Universities Mission to Central Africa (Anglican), the Church of Scotland and Free Church of Scotland Missions. The country during these early years was occupied by migrant tribes warring with one another and selling their prisoners to Arab and half-caste slave dealers. The missionary pioneers were followed by other Europeans—missionaries, traders, hunters and planters—and in 1883 a British Consul resident at Blantyre was accredited to "The Kings and Chiefs of Central Africa"; the second Consul established himself at Zomba, now the seat of Government. Access to Nyasaland from the coast was difficult during the latter part of the nineteenth century. In 1889 a Portuguese expedition under Major Serpa Pinto came into conflict with one of the Makololo Chiefs, in consequence of which the Acting Consul, a pioneer planter named Buchanan, proclaimed a
Digitized by Google
THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST

British Protectorate over the Shire country on the 21st September 1889. In 1891 an Anglo-Portuguese Convention ratified the work of Mr. H. H. (later Sir Harry) Johnston, Mr. (later Sir) Alfred Sharpe and others, and a Protectorate was proclaimed over the countries adjoining Lake Nyasa. In 1893 the name of the Territory was changed to "The British Central Africa Protectorate" but in 1907 a further Order in Council revived the old name of "The Nyasaland Protectorate". The Northern Angoni in what is now the Northern Province did not come fully within the sphere of British administration until 1904.
It has been agreed that Nyasaland shall become an independent state within the Commonwealth on 6th July, 1964.
Constitution

The Governor is required to consult the Cabinet in the exercise of his functions and normally acts in accordance with the advice he receives. He retains reserved powers in the fields of the economy and finance, public safety and operational control of the police. He is assisted by a Deputy Governor.
The Cabinet

The Cabinet which is collectively responsible to the Legislative Assembly, is composed of a Prime Minister, normally not more than eight other Ministers and the Financial Secretary, who is ex officio Minister of Finance. Provision exists under the Constitution that the number of Ministers can be increased if the Prime Minister considers it necessary and Her Majesty's Government concur and under which, at a time to be agreed between the Nyasaland Government and Her Majesty's Government, the Portfolio of Finance can be assumed by an elected member.
The Governor, on the advice of the Prime Minister, has power to appoint Parliamentary Secretaries to assist Ministers.

The Legislative Assembly consists of a Speaker, the Financial Secretary, so long as he is a member of the Cabinet, and, for the present 28 elected members. Any Minister who is not a member of the Legislative Assembly has the right to attend and take part in the proceedings of the Assembly, but not to vote. The Assembly has a life of not more than five years.

The 28 elected members are elected in single member geographical constituencies on a two-tier qualitative franchise containing members of all races. Twenty of these are elected in one set of constituencies by voters who satisfy the lower qualifications and eight in another set of constituencies by voters who satisfy the higher qualifications. The last general election was held in August 1961. A general election is to be held by May 1964 for an enlarged Assembly of 55 members.

The appointment of a Chief Justice is made by the Governor after consultation with the Prime Minister. Other judges of the High Court are appointed by the Governor on the advice of the Judicial Service Commission. Judges' salaries are prescribed by law.
The Constitution provides for Public, Police and Judicial service Commissions with executive powers.
The Constitution also embodies a Bill of Rights.
Land Policy
Land policy is controlled so far as African Trust Land, which comprises 87% of the total area of the Protectorate, is concerned, by the Nyasaland

Protectorate (African Trust Land) Orders in Council, 1950 to 1963. Under these Orders in Council Africans may use and occupy African Trust Land not held under lease in accordance with the African law and custom prevailing in the area. The Orders in Council empower the Governor at his discretion to lease trust land for terms up to 99 years, while the Secretary of State's sanction is required for the grant of any greater estate. The remainder of the land is either held in private ownership or is public land, which includes areas of state forest; the disposal of public land is controlled by the Public Land Ordinance 1951, which empowers the Governor to make grants, leases or other dispositions subject to the provisions of any Order in Council or any general or special instruction of the Secretary of State.
Taxation

The taxation legislation provides, broadly speaking, only for the taxation of income which is derived from a source in Nyasaland . For example, dividends from companies not incorporated in Nyasaland are not liable for Nyasaland income tax, though such dividends may well be effectively taxed in the country of their origin.
Income Tax is calculated under two schemes of taxation, the larger amount so calculated being the amount payable.
Scheme 1

No account is taken in this scheme of a taxpayer's personal circumstances (whether he is single or married for example). Tax is calculated upon taxable income , which is the income which accrues to a taxpayer less the amounts which he is allowed to deduct under the legislation in production of that income. The tax rates rise from £1 10s. per year to £30 per year in steps dependent upon the amount of the taxable income. If the taxable income does not exceed £61 in the year, the tax applicable is £1 10s. and where the taxable income exceeds £1,200, the tax is £30.
Scheme 2

For the purposes of this scheme, a taxpayer's personal circumstances are taken into account, and personal allowances as shown below are deducted from taxable income, before tax at the rates provided is calculated. The amount remaining of taxable income after personal allowances are deducted is known as chargeable income.
The personal allowances are:—
Single Persons —£300 reduced by £1 for every £4 by which the chargeable income exceeds £2,000.
Married Persons —£720.

Children —£144 is allowable for each child (with a maximum of 4 children) for whose maintenance the taxpayer is responsible. The child or children claimed for must be under the age of 18 at the end of the year of assessment, or if over 18 must be in full time attendance at a University, college, school or other educational institution. No allowance is given in respect of any child who has an income of £150 per year (or more) in his own right.

Life Insurance . An allowance is given for life insurance premiums paid during the year of assessment in respect of policies on the taxpayer's life, or on that of his wife. The allowance is subject to two restrictions:—
(a) The premium allowable per policy is restricted to 7% of the capital sum payable on death, exclusive of any additional benefits payable such as a bonus or profits.

Pay As You Earn

As from 1st April 1964 a scheme for PAYE will be introduced, and tax calculated as described above will be deducted by employers in weekly or monthly instalments from salaries and wages as they are earned.
Tax Concessions made for Industry

(a) Premiums paid for the right to use certain assets such as patents, designs, trade-marks and copyright. —An allowance is granted, the sum allowable per year being the payment made divided by the number of years for which the right of use is granted. If this period is more than 25 years, the allowance is granted over 25 years.
(b) Research, etc. —Expenditure on research and experiments, not being of a capital nature, incurred by a taxpayer in relation to his trade is allowable as a deduction.

(c) New manufacturing businesses. —In respect of manufacturing businesses which commenced operations on or after 1st April, 1963, expenditure incurred not more than 18 months before commencement of the business can be allowed as a deduction, as long as the expenditure claimed would have been allowed had it been incurred after the commencement of the business.
(d) Capital Allowances

(i) Initial Allowance. —An initial allowance is granted, if claimed by the taxpayer, on new farm improvements, fencing, industrial buildings, railway lines or on additions or alterations to any of these items at rates as follows:—

Farm improvements, industrial buildings and railway lines10%
Fencing33.3%
An initial allowance of 20% can be claimed also in respect of articles, implements, machinery and utensils used for the purposes of the taxpayer's trade.
(ii) Annual Allowances. —Annual Allowances are granted as follows:—

Farm improvements, railway lines and industrial buildings5%
Fencing10%
In respect of all other itemsat rates fixed by the Commissioner.

(e) Investment Allowance: Manufacturers. —An allowance of 10% of the cost to a taxpayer of new or unused industrial buildings, plant or machinery, which are used for the purpose of manufacture is allowable. Plant and machinery
does not include motor vehicles, but industrial buildings include hotels, and buildings used for the processing and distribution of fish.
The owners of plantations producing tea, coffee or tobacco are regarded as manufacturers for the purposes of the investment allowance.

Currency

At present the former Federal Currency issued by Bank of Rhodesia and Nyasaland remains in circulation. Towards the end of 1964 or in 1965 the present currency will be replaced by currency issued by Nyasaland and the old currency will be withdrawn during 1965.
Development Plan

On 1st January, 1964, with the dissolution of the Federation of Rhodesia and Nyasaland the Nyasaland Government re-assumed responsibility for the various services formerly provided by the Federal Government. The current Development Plan which was prepared in 1962 covered only those services which were the responsibility of the Territorial Government during the period of Federation. With the take-over of former Federal responsibilities the Government has decided that a new Development Plan should be prepared in 1964.
Education

On 1st January, 1964, with the dissolution of the Federation of Rhodesia and Nyasaland responsibility for educational services provided by that Government became the responsibility of the Government of Nyasaland which is now responsible for all educational matters.

A new Education Ordinance replacing that of 1945 was enacted in March, 1962, and, amongst other things, it established the popularly elected District Councils as Local Education Authorities, making the control and development of primary education a local responsibility. Each Authority through its Education Committee has carried out a survey of local educational needs and is preparing a Development Plan.

Certain very important steps were taken during the years 1962 and 1963 in the re-organisation of education. In primary education, the selection process at the end of the fifth year has been abolished and the aim is that every pupil entering an assisted primary school shall complete the primary course which is now of seven years duration. At the same time the secondary course has been lengthened to five years and the teachers' course has been extended to three years.

The Ministry's three-year Development Plan which forms part of the national Plan for the period 1962–65 lays particular emphasis on Secondary and Higher Education which, between them, account for £1,570,000 of the £2,307,000 allocated to Education.
Local Education Authorities, of which there are 20, are responsible for primary education and in 1963 controlled 928 primary streams with an

enrolment of 146,356 boys and 77,463 girls. The majority of schools are owned by Voluntary Agencies, but more schools are coming into the proprietorship of the local authorities who now directly maintain 191 streams. The Government does not directly maintain or manage primary schools except in the urban areas where there are special problems. There were 17 Government streams in 1963 with 1,404 boy pupils and 1,112 girl pupils.

Priority is being given to the development of secondary education. In 1963 there were 8 streams with 697 pupils in Government Schools, and 27 streams in 17 voluntary schools with 2,528 pupils, a total of 2,377 boys and 848 girls. In 1963–64 as part of a programme of day secondary school development 7 double stream Government day secondary schools are being opened, and a further 8 being built for opening in 1965. This raises the entry to secondary schools from 600 in 1961 to 1,540 in 1964 and to 2,180 in 1965.

In technical and vocational education there were in 1963 183 male and 48 female students in Government schools and 196 male and 280 female in voluntary aided schools. A total of 280 men students were enrolled in part-time courses at Government institutions and 25 men and 3 women at voluntary aided institutions. A Further Education Centre to cater for a variety of technical and general courses including part-time, day release and evening classes was opened in Blantyre in 1963. The building of the Polytechnic commenced in 1963.

Teacher Training is conducted in 2 Government and 11 Voluntary Colleges. The new Government College at Soche Hill, Blantyre, was opened in 1963 and aims at training teachers particularly for the developing secondary schools. There were 1,410 students in training, 1,013 men and 397 women in 1963.
Expenditure on education during 1963 amounted to £1,711,254.
Libraries

Public libraries exist at Blantyre, to which Government gives financial support. Libraries are also maintained at secondary schools and at various mission stations while the British Council maintains a collection of books on a variety of subjects and makes these available to study-groups and at welfare centres in the Southern Province. The Nyasaland Society maintains a library of books on Africana available for the use of members.
Health
Public Health reverted from the control of the Federal Government to that of the Nyasaland Government on the 1st November, 1963.

The principal diseases encountered in Nyasaland are malaria, bilharzia, hookworm and relapsing fever. Leprosy is fairly widespread. Epidemics of smallpox rarely now occur, but there has recently been a severe outbreak in the Centre Province. Sleeping sickness, absent from the Protectorate for a number of years, re-appeared in a small way in the Southern Province in 1952 but again subsided.
There is 1 Central Hospital, 2 General Hospitals, 13 District Hospitals, 19 Rural Hospitals and 2 Special Hospitals—1 Mental and 1 Leprosy—with accommodation for 282 and 485 persons respectively.

There are beds for Europeans and Asians in the Central Hospital and the General Hospitals.
Mission Hospitals provide 1,615 beds, inclusive of maternity beds.

Communications
During 1963 the Nyasaland Government re-assumed from the Federal Government responsibility for Civil Aviation, inter-territorial roads, railways, and other aspects of communication.

The total length of line within Nyasaland operated by the Nyasaland Railways is 273 miles. There is only one route, from the southern extremity of Nyasaland via Limbe to Salima in the Central Region. At Chipoka, south of Salima, trains connect with the vessels operating on Lake Nyasa. In 1962 949,391 short tons of freight and 612,574 passengers were carried.
The total mileage of Government maintained and grant-assisted roads is 6,116, of which just over 266 have a bituminous surface.

A lake service is operated by the Nyasaland Railways employing one passenger and cargo ship (the " Ilala "), two cargo ships and five barges towed by five tugs. The " Ilala " schedule operates between the following lakeshore stations:—
Monkey Bay, Chipoka, Nkota Kota, Likoma Island, Nkata Bay, Usisya, Ruarwe, Mbamba Bay (Tanganyika), Mlowe, Tekero, Manda (Tanganyika), Deep Bay, Kambwe (for Karonga) and Itungi (Tanganyika).

The main airport is at Blantyre from which air services of Central African Airways and East African Airways connect with Salisbury and Nairobi via Dar es Salaam. There is also an airport at Lilongwe and twelve out-station aerodromes, to eight of which aircraft of Air Malawi operate internal scheduled services. The meteorological services are controlled by the Nyasaland Director of Civil Aviation and meteorological briefing is available at Blantyre and Lilongwe airports and Mzimba aerodrome. Air services to, from, and within Nyasaland are under the control of a Higher Authority which is a body represented by Ministers from Nyasaland and the Rhodesias. External services are provided by Central African Airways Corporation and its subsidiary Air Malawi.
Broadcasting
The Nyasaland Government assumed responsibility for broadcasting from the Federal Government on the 1st September, 1963.
Pending the establishment of the Malawi Broadcasting Corporation on the 1st January, 1964, the service operated until the end of the year as a division of the Information Department.

The transmission facilities available to Radio Malawi on take-over comprised a 10 Kw. short wave transmitter in the 76 metre band and a 300 Watt medium wave transmitter in the 244 metre band. This latter transmitter is located at Blantyre and serves the Blantyre/Limbe area.

Short wave programmes from the Blantyre studio are sent to the transmitter at Mikuyu, near Zomba, by means of a post office circuit comprising a landline link from Blantyre to Limbe and a V.H.F. link to Mikuyu. Broadcasting hours have been increased to seven hours daily since the Nyasaland Government took over on the 1st September. Programmes are in Chinyanja and Tumbuka (for the Northern Province) and English.

Vernacular programmes include daily news bulletins, features, magazine programmes, plays, religious programmes, book reviews, request programmes, variety shows, indigenous folk music, and programmes for particular communities such as farmers. Detailed listener research is not possible at the moment, but judging from the thousands of letters received each month, the programmes greatly increased their popularity with their local audiences during the last quarter of the year.
It is estimated that there are some 30,000 inexpensive transistorised dry-battery sets in Nyasaland, an indication of the popularity of broadcasting, which continues to make encouraging progress.
Principal Occupations
The principal occupations are in the following trades or professions :—
Europeans . Government service, religious missions, tobacco, tea, farming, wholesale and retail trading, engineering contract work.
Asians . Wholesale and retail trading.

Africans . The bulk of the population is engaged in agriculture; the remainder are employed in Government service, transport services, tobacco and tea industries, retail trading, and building construction.
On 31st October, 1963 there were 18 employees' trade unions and 8 associations of employers.
Main Crops and Products
The main export crops are tea, tobacco, tung-oil, groundnuts, rice and cotton. Other major crops grown mainly for local consumption are maize, millet, cassava and miscellaneous pulses.

Exotic timber and pole plantations were extended in 1963 to give a total area of 34,519 acres. The most successful trees planted are various species of Pine, Eucalypt and Gmelina arborea . Timber and fuel production for 1962 amounted to 2,548,945 cubic feet.
There is extensive fishing in Lake Nyasa and commercial enterprises are in process of development; fishery research is being undertaken.
No minerals were exported on a commercial scale during 1962 although samples of various minerals were sent out of the country for analysis.
The export of manufactured articles is very restricted but local needs of soap, cigarettes and mineral waters, are largely met from factories in the Protectorate.

The chief imports are metals, metal manufactures, machinery and vehicles; fibres, yarns, textiles and apparel, foodstuffs, oils, resins, waxes, paint and varnishes.
Marketing and Co-operative Organizations

The marketing of the principal cash crops grown on African trust land, namely, tobacco, cotton, maize and groundnuts, is handled by a statutory board, whose aim is to ensure a steady price to the producer, as near as possible to world prices, and to secure improvement of the quality of the product. In 1962 Co-operative Societies were appointed as buying agents of the Board in all areas in respect of groundnuts and cotton. Tea and tung, which are mainly European-grown crops, are marketed by the industries themselves, while tobacco, whether grown on African trust land or alienated land, is sold on auction floors, at which buyers representing United Kingdom and other extra-territorial interests bid.

On the 31st December 1962 there were 110 primary and 7 secondary co-operative societies, with 31,133 members and 70 member societies respectively. The products handled exclusively by Co-operatives were rice, coffee and ghee. The other major crops are only handled on an agency basis.

PITCAIRN ISLANDS GROUP

The small, volcanic islands of Pitcairn (two square miles in area), Henderson, Ducie and Oeno, of which only the first is inhabited, lie about 3,500 miles from Panama and 3,000 from Auckland. Regular sea communication with the outside world is limited to the brief calls of large passenger liners, which heave to for a few hours. A small radio station is of particular importance in arranging such calls with passing ships, especially when a doctor is required.

Pitcairn was discovered by Carteret in 1767. In 1790 the Bounty mutineers landed with some women from Otaheite and these were the forebears of the present inhabitants. It was annexed as a colony in 1838 when two British ships visited the island. The population was removed at its own request in 1856 but 40 soon returned, and today the Islanders number about 126. There has been some emigration to New Zealand which accounts for the recent decrease in population.

Pitcairn is a British colony by settlement and was brought within the jurisdiction of the Western Pacific High Commission in 1898. On the 1st April 1952 by Order in Council made by Her Majesty the Queen, the administration was assumed by the Governor of Fiji, following the separation of the offices of the Governor of Fiji and High Commissioner for the Western Pacific. There is an Island Council consisting of the Chief Magistrate, two assessors and a secretary. The Chief Magistrate is elected triennially and the other officers annually, by the people. The principal source of revenue is the sale of stamps. A Government school is established under a trained teacher. Medical attention is given by a nurse resident on the island, assisted in cases of emergency by doctors from passing ships.
Chief Magistrate—John Christian.
ST. CHRISTOPHER, NEVIS AND ANGUILLA
Area and Geographical Features

The total area of the Colony is 138 square miles (St. Kitts 65, Nevis 36, Anguilla 35 and Sombrero 2 square miles). St. Kitts has a range of lofty mountains traversing its central part (Mount Misery 3,711 feet). Nevis is nearly circular in shape with a central mountain rising to 3,596 feet. Both islands are of volcanic origin. Anguilla is flat and composed of oligocene limestone and clay. Sombrero is a rocky islet. Hot mineral springs with valuable medicinal properties are to be found near Charlestown, Nevis.
Population
The figure for the population at the 1960 Census was 56,591.
Principal Towns
Basseterre, the capital of St. Kitts, has a population of 15,726, and Charlestown and its environs, the capital of Nevis, has a population of approximately 2,850.

History

St. Christopher, discovered by Columbus on his second voyage in 1493, was the first island in the West Indies to be colonized by the English, when Sir Thomas Warner took settlers there in 1623. In 1624 the French, under d'Esnambuc, also colonized one-half of the island.
After a severe battle with the Europeans in about 1626, the Caribs, the native people of the island, were driven out in 1629.

Intermittent warfare between the French and British settlers during the seventeenth century ravaged the economy of the island. It was, however, ceded to Britain by the treaty of Utrecht in 1713. The last fighting on the island took place in 1782 when the French captured Brimstone Hill after a memorable siege and once more took possession of St. Christopher. The island was finally restored to Britain by the Treaty of Versailles in 1783.

Nevis, also sighted by Columbus on his second voyage, was settled by the English in 1628 and soon became one of the most prosperous of the Antilles. Although it suffered from French and Spanish attacks in the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries, it maintained a sound economic position until the middle of the nineteenth century.

Columbus probably also first sighted Anguilla. The island remained continuously British after its colonization in 1650 but was subject in its early days to severe depredations by marauders, freebooters and pirates.
Constitution

The constitutional arrangements are the same as in Antigua (see page 64) except that there is a separate Police Force. The Administrator appoints the Head of the Force in his discretion and the gazetted ranks after consulting the Police Service Commission.
Land Policy
Aliens cannot own land except under licence from the Governor.
Taxation

The main taxes in force are Customs duty and Income tax. Income tax rates range from 6d. in the £ to 15s. in the £ upon the chargeable income of an individual. Companies pay a flat rate of 8s. in the £.

Development Plans

The Five-Year Development Plan which was drawn up in 1955 for the period 1955–60 was further revised in 1959. The new plan envisaged expenditure of $5,966,000 over the five year period; of this sum $2,751,000 came from Colonial Development and Welfare funds, $768,000 from local revenue, $2,447,000 from local and external loans. The Development funds were allocated as follows: Trade and Production $582,000, Social Services $629,000 and Communications and Works $4,755,000.

A new Five-Year Development Plan was approved for the period 1960–65. This envisaged expenditure to a total of $7,376,350, of which $4,426,350 will come from Colonial Development and Welfare funds and $2,950,000 from loans. The funds have been allocated as follows: Industrial and Area Development $2,249,500, Agriculture, Veterinary and Fisheries $1,411,500, Communications and Works $1,367,129, Social Services $2,320,416 and Administrative $27,805.
Education

In 1962 there were 32 Government primary schools (St. Kitts 17, Nevis 10, Anguilla 5), two grant aided primary schools and three private primary schools. There were also three Government senior schools (St. Kitts 2, Nevis 1), and five secondary schools (Government 4, private 1). The total enrolment at all schools was 16,261 pupils. Government expenditure on educational services during 1962 was $1,074,445.
Libraries

There are three main public libraries in the Colony, one in each island with headquarters in St. Kitts and subsidiary book distribution points throughout the islands. Book circulation amounts to over 51,000 volumes annually.
Health

The total expenditure on medical and health services in 1962 was $890,637 compared with $96,864 in 1938. In 1960, beds available in two general hospitals totalled 157, those in two cottage hospitals 40. There were 24 Health Centres and Outposts at work in the Colony. Their chief functions are to improve standards of nutrition, to promote maternal and child health, to improve environmental sanitation, to control tuberculosis, venereal disease and yaws and to educate the community in health matters. There are also a tuberculosis ward and a leprosarium.
Communications

One airport, about two miles north of Basseterre by road, serves the island of St. Kitts. The runway is 4,125 feet by 300 feet and lies in a west-east direction. This is being extended and a new terminal building and control tower are being constructed.

There are landing strips with terminal buildings in Nevis and Anguilla. The air service to these islands is operated by L.I.A.T., a subsidiary of B.W.I.A.

Under a Colonial Development and Welfare grant a new passenger service vessel was built in British Guiana for daily voyages between St. Kitts and Nevis and put into operation in 1959. It replaces the one destroyed by hurricane in 1956.
Broadcasting
There is a Government owned broadcasting station situated in St. Kitts which broadcasts on 570 k/cs.
Principal Occupations

The staple industry is sugar and about 7,600 persons are employed on estates or in the sugar factory at Basseterre. In Nevis there is a high percentage of peasant ownership of land and, apart from the cultivation of sugar cane, sea island cotton of good quality is grown in large quantities for export. In Anguilla, where the majority of the inhabitants are peasant proprietors, the main industries are livestock raising, salt production and fishing.
There are seven organizations registered under the Trade Union Act, with a total membership of 4,500. The largest is the St. Kitts-Nevis Trades and Labour Union, membership 4,001.
Main Crops and Products
These are sugar, sea island cotton and salt.

ST. HELENA
Situation and Area

The island is situated in the South Atlantic Ocean, 700 miles south-east of Ascension and about 1,200 miles from the south-west coast of Africa. It is 10½ miles long and 6½ miles broad, covering an area of 47 square miles.
Population

The estimated population at the 31st December 1962 was 4,624. It is largely descended from settlers and others of British origin and persons of Asian or African blood who were introduced by the East India Company. The language of the Island has always been English and the English way of life has become firmly established.
Digitized by Google
THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST
Principal Town
Jamestown, the capital, is the only town and has a population of some 1,600.
Geographical Features
St. Helena is rugged and mountainous and of volcanic origin. The highest peak, Mt. Actaeon, rises to 2,685 feet. There are no rivers or streams but there is a good supply of fresh spring water.
Climate

The cool South Atlantic trade winds blow throughout the year. The climate is mild and varies little, the temperature in Jamestown, on the sea-coast, ranging in summer between $70^\circ$ and $85^\circ$ , and in winter between $65^\circ$ and $75^\circ$ . Inland it is some $10^\circ$ cooler. The annual rainfall varies from an average of about 8 inches in Jamestown to between 22 and 38 inches inland.
History

St. Helena, then uninhabited, was discovered in 1502 by the Portuguese commander, Juan de Nova Castella. Its existence appears to have remained unknown to other European nations until 1588 when it was visited by Captain Cavendish on his return from a voyage round the world. Soon afterwards the island became a port of call for ships of various nations voyaging between the East Indies and Europe. In 1633 the Dutch formally annexed it but made no attempt to occupy it. In 1659 it was annexed and occupied on behalf of the East India Company but the first official authorization of the Company's occupation occurs in a charter dated 1661. In January 1673 the Dutch seized the Island but were driven out again in May by the English navy. A Charter to occupy and govern the Island was issued by Charles II to the East India Company in December 1673 and it remained under that Company until April 1834 when it was brought under the direct government of the Crown by an Act of Parliament of 1833. Napoleon Bonaparte was exiled in St. Helena from 1815 until his death in 1821.
Constitution

An Order in Council and Royal Instructions of 1956, as amended by additional Royal Instructions of 1963, provide for (1) an Executive Council consisting of the Government Secretary and the Colonial Treasurer as ex officio members, not more than two other official members and not more than three unofficial members at least one of whom must be an elected member of the Advisory Council; and (2) an Advisory Council consisting of the Government Secretary and the Colonial Treasurer as ex officio members, two other official members, four nominated unofficial members and eight elected members. The legislative authority is the Governor, acting after consultation with the Advisory Council; power is reserved to Her Majesty in Council to legislate by Order.
Land Policy
Individual ownership of land is recognized and there is no restriction on alienation. Crown land may be disposed of on conditions approved by the Governor.
Taxation

An Income Tax Ordinance came into force on the 1st January 1954. The rate for individuals is 1s. 3d. in the £ on the first £1,500 and at the rate of 1s. 9d. in the £ on any excess of £1,500 derived from local sources.

In the case of a married person who can prove to the satisfaction of the Commissioner of Income Tax that his wife was living with him or wholly maintained by him during the year immediately preceding the year of assessment there is an abatement of one-third of the tax. Import duties and an import tax are levied on a large range of goods and an export tax is charged on flax products. There are also taxes on motor vehicles, shops and entertainments.
Public Finance

In 1962 the revenue derived from import and export duties (£24,927) was about 8.23% and Post Office revenue (including Ascension) was about 10.16% of local revenue. In the same year the main heads of expenditure (excluding Colonial Development and Welfare expenditure) were:

Currency

The Colony's currency consists of Bank of England notes and United Kingdom coins which are legal tender in the United Kingdom. South African currency is also accepted in the Colony. The amount of currency in circulation at the end of 1961 was estimated at £50,000.
Development Plans

A further allocation of £200,000 has been provided for an extension of the development programme to cover the years 1959 to 1964 which will be financed almost entirely from a grant under the Colonial Development and Welfare (Amendment) Act, 1959.

While a considerable proportion of the funds are being devoted to road improvement and to development in agriculture, other phases of the programme include housing, extension of the electrical distribution system, teacher training, school meals and school playgrounds.
Education
Education is compulsory and is provided free for all children between the ages of 5 and 15 years.

In 1962 there were eight primary schools, two of which provided all-age education; three secondary schools and one selective secondary school. At the end of that year the total number of scholars was 1,250.
The expenditure on educational services during 1962 was £22,451, of which £3,478 was contributed from Colonial Development and Welfare funds. In 1935 the expenditure was £980.

ST. HELENA
Library
There is a free Public Library subsidized by Government and managed by a Committee.
Health

The Island is very healthy and none of the ordinary tropical diseases are endemic. There is one hospital in Jamestown with 60 beds, the number of in-patients during 1962 totalling 795. There are six out-patient dispensaries at convenient centres in the Island which are regularly visited by the doctors and the Health Sister. A mental hospital with 22 beds is situated a little way out of Jamestown.
During 1962 £22,121 was spent on medical services compared with £2,637 in 1935.
Communications

Under normal conditions ships of the Union Castle Line call at intervals of four weeks—both on their way from England to South Africa and on their journey from South Africa homewards. These are the only regular mails. Air mail letters are carried by sea to Cape Town or to London and thence by air to their destinations. The Island has an internal telephone system. Cable and Wireless, Limited, operate a cable and radio telephone station connecting with Cape Town and Ascension.
In 1962, 39 merchant ships with a total tonnage of 185,652 called at St. Helena.
Some 65 miles of roads in the Island are all-weather roads for motor traffic.
There is no broadcasting service in the Colony.

ASCENSION

The island of Ascension, 34 square miles in area, lying in the South Atlantic, latitude $7^\circ 56'$ S. and longitude $14^\circ 22'$ W., was taken possession of in 1815; until 1922 it was under the supervision of the Admiralty and a small naval station was maintained there.
The population at 31st December 1962 was 374 of whom 284 were St. Helenians.

By Letters Patent dated 12th September 1922 (since superseded by Order in Council of 22nd March 1956) it was made a Dependency of St. Helena and placed under the control of the Secretary of State for the Colonies.

Ascension is a barren, rocky peak of purely volcanic origin, and destitute of vegetation, except at the highest point (2,870 feet), but has been cultivated to an extent permitting the maintenance of about 900 sheep and cattle. It is famous for its turtles, large numbers of which visit between December and May.

Cable and Wireless Limited own and operate an important cable station which connects the Dependency with St. Helena, Sierra Leone, St. Vincent, Rio de Janeiro and Buenos Aires, and through these places, over the Company's system, with all parts of the world.

In 1942 the Government of the United States of America, by arrangement with His Majesty's Government, established an air base which became of considerable importance during the period of hostilities. The United States Government has recently reoccupied Wideawake Airfield by agreement with the Government of St. Helena in connexion with the extension of the Long Range Proving Ground for guided missiles centred in Florida.

Under normal conditions the Government of St. Helena is represented in the Dependency by the local manager of Cable and Wireless Limited, who is appointed Resident Magistrate for this purpose and has the aid of another employee of the Company who is appointed a Justice of the Peace.
Resident Magistrate—J. R. Bruce.
TRISTAN DA CUNHA, etc.

Tristan da Cunha lies in latitude $37^\circ 6'$ S., longitude $12^\circ 17'$ W., about midway between South Africa and South America. It is volcanic in origin, nearly circular in shape, covering an area of 38 square miles, and rising like a cone to 6,760 feet.
The climate is typically oceanic and temperate. Absolute maximum and minimum temperatures recorded at sea level are $76.8^\circ$ and $36.9^\circ$ respectively. Rainfall averages 66 inches per year.

The island was taken possession of in 1816 during Napoleon's residence on St. Helena. When the garrison was withdrawn in the following year, three men, headed by Corporal William Glass, elected to remain and became the founders of the present settlement. For the next 65 years the colony thrived, largely because of its position on a main sailing route. New settlers, mostly from shipwrecks, included Dutch, St. Helenian, Italian and American elements. In the 1880's, however, a period of decline set in, due to the replacement of sail by steam, the infestation of the island by rats, and a boat disaster in which 15 able-bodied islanders were lost. Nevertheless attempts to move the inhabitants to South Africa at the beginning of the present century were only partially successful.

The Society for the Propagation of the Gospel maintained a missionary teacher on the island since 1922; a number of missionaries had also served on the island prior to this. In 1932 the missionary was officially recognized as Honorary Commissioner and Magistrate.

By Letters Patent dated 12th January 1938 Tristan da Cunha and the neighbouring uninhabited Nightingale, Inaccessible and Gough Islands, were made dependencies of St. Helena, though as a matter of practical convenience the administration of the group has continued to be directly supervised by the Colonial Office.
Digitized by Google
ST. HELENA : ST. LUCIA
In 1937–38 a Norwegian Scientific Expedition visited the group, studying the natural science of the islands and the sociology and health of the community.

In 1942 a detachment of the South African Defence Force built a meteorological and wireless station which was manned for the rest of the war by the Royal Navy. The station operated by the South African Government was in daily contact with passing shipping.

The coming of the Navy re-introduced the islanders to the outside world and marked their emergence from a state of dependence on charity, for it was a naval chaplain who recognized the possibilities of a crawfish industry at Tristan. Towards the end of 1948 an agreement was entered into between the Governor of St. Helena and a South African fishing company, permitting the latter to establish a fishing and canning industry on certain conditions, which include the maintenance on the island at the Company's expense of a doctor, nurse, agriculturist and teacher. Provision was thus made for basic services and the way was open for the islanders to earn regular wages and convert them into goods in the Company's canteen. Following on this, the office of Administrator was created; the first assumed duty early in 1950. The Administrator was also the Magistrate.

On October 10th 1961 a volcanic cone erupted close to the settlement of Edinburgh. The population of 264 islanders and their expatriate staff were evacuated to Nightingale and on 11th October were picked up by the liner Tjisadane and taken to Cape Town. Thence they came to the United Kingdom where new homes were provided for them. A scientific expedition organised by the Royal Society visited the island in April 1962. This was followed in October 1962 with a visit by an exploratory party to determine whether the Settlement was habitable and the beaches usable and safe. It was decided that those of the islanders who wished to do so could return. An advance party of 52 islanders accompanied by the Administrator, Mr. P. A. Day, and a medical officer, Dr. S. G. Gooch, left to prepare for the return of the main party in March 1963; 198 islanders returned to the island on the Danish vessel m.s. Bomholm , landing on November 10th. Fourteen remained in Britain.
Administrator—P. A. Day.
ST. LUCIA
Area and Population
According to recent aerial surveys, the area is about 238 square miles. The population at the 1960 Census was 86,108.

Principal Town Census Population
Castries (capital) 4,353
Geographical Features

The island is mountainous, with magnificent scenery. The highest peak is Mt. Gimie (3,145 feet); the most spectacular are the Gros Piton (2,619 feet) and the Petit Piton (2,461 feet) which are old volcanic, forest clad cones rising sheer out of the sea near the town of Soufrière on the leeward coast. A few miles away in an ancient crater are hot, sulphurous springs. The mountains are intersected by numerous short rivers. In places, these rivers debouch into broad, fertile and well cultivated valleys.
Climate

There is a dry season roughly from January to April, and a rainy season from May to August, with an Indian summer in September–October. Towards the end of the year it is usually wet. The island lies in latitudes where the north-east trade winds are an almost constant influence.
